text: The Four Treasuries, Imperially Designated, Subdivision Twelve Collective Records of the Court and the People First Category of the Miscellaneous Section in the Novels Category: Abstract We, the subjects, respectfully present the Collective Records of the Court and the People in six volumes, originally titled as authored by Zhang Zuo of the Tang dynasty. Zhang Zuo has another work, Dragon Sinews and Phoenix Marrow Judgment, already recorded. This book is listed in the Literary Records of the New History of the Tang Dynasty as thirty volumes, in the Literary Records of the History of Song as twenty volumes, and in the Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History as three supplementary volumes. This edition, however, differs from these records. According to the Reading Records of Zhao Gongwu, it is divided into thirty-five sections, but the current edition is a continuous compilation without sections, differing from Zhao Gongwu's account. Additionally, Mo Xiufu's Records of the Customs and Geography of Guilin mention that during the Kaiyuan period, Yao Chong falsely accused Zhang Zuo of accepting a legacy and sentenced him to death. His son submitted a petition to be substituted, and Zhang Zuo was reduced to exile to Lingnan instead of execution. After several years, he was appointed as the Chief Clerk and died. At that time, it was before the Tianbao era, yet the book contains events from the first year of the Baoli era, when the stone of Ziyang moved. Baoli is the era name of Emperor Jingzong of Tang. There is also a reference to Meng Hongwei's response to Emperor Xuanzong.

The times and eras of the events do not align. In the Suichutang book catalog compiled by You Mao, "Chao Ye Qian Zai" and its supplement are listed as two separate works. It is suspected that "Chao Ye Qian Zai" was written by Zhang Zuo, while the supplement was added by later scholars. All entries from the mid-Tang period and later should be considered part of the supplement. Chen Zhensun mentioned that the original thirty-volume version is a condensed version of the current text. It is believed that the text has been compiled and edited by Song scholars, combining the main text and supplement, and modifying the classification, which differs from the original. It is unclear when the text was divided into six volumes from the original three. The book records Tang dynasty stories, including humorous and strange tales, which makes it somewhat chaotic. Therefore, Hong Mai criticized in his "Yongzai Suibi" that the book's records are fragmented and contain inappropriate language. However, there are many reliable and verifiable accounts. Sima Guang also used this text as a reference in his "Zizhi Tongjian." By collecting and selecting widely, the text has contributed to the expansion of knowledge. The imperial review and publication took place in the eleventh month of the 46th year of the Qianlong era.

Chief Compiler [Minister] Ji Yun, [Minister] Lu Xixiong, [Minister] Sun Shiyi Chief Proofreader [Minister] Lu Feichi Imperial Decree: The Four Treasuries Records of the Court and the World, Volume One By Zhang Zuo of the Tang Dynasty During the Zhenguan period, there was a wealthy man named Wei Quan from Gu County, Ding Prefecture. His mother suddenly went blind. He consulted a fortune-teller named Wang Zhen. Wang Zhen divined and said, "Next year, there will be a man wearing blue clothing who will come from the east. On the first day of the third month, he will cure her blindness." As the time arrived, a man wearing a blue jacket appeared. Wei Quan invited him to have a meal. The man said, "I do not know medicine, but I know how to make a plow." He worked for the host and took an axe to search for the plow's beam. He found a crooked mulberry tree near a well and cut it down. Wei Quan's mother's eyes suddenly regained their sight, and she could see clearly again. This was caused by the crooked mulberry tree near the well. Péi Guī, the Langzhong of Zhou, had a concubine named Mistress Zhao, who was very beautiful. She once consulted a fortune-teller named Zhang Jingcang about her destiny. Zhang Jingcang said, "Your eyes are long and look downward. According to physiognomy, those with pig-like eyes are promiscuous women. Your eyes have four whites, and according to the *Five Husbands Protecting the Home* theory, you will eventually be degraded due to adultery. Be cautious." Mistress Zhao laughed and left. Later, she indeed committed adultery and was sent to the imperial harem.

Dù Jǐngquán, originally from Xindu, was known by his birth name Yuánfāng. During the Chóngdé period, he was renamed Jǐngquán. He was upright and stern, passed the imperial examination, and later became a Langtaishanglang and Pingzhāngshì. At the time, Lǐ Zhāodé, the Neishǐ (Chancellor), was imprisoned for his strictness. Dù Jǐngquán argued in court that Lǐ Zhāodé was fair and upright, which angered Empress Wu Zetian, who believed he was face-saving. As a result, Dù Jǐngquán was demoted to the position of Zhìzhōu Zhìshǐ. Initially, while serving as the governor of Zhìzhōu, a skilled diviner on the road predicted that he would be reappointed as a high-ranking official, reaching the rank of Sanpǐn but without wearing a purple robe. By this time, he was wearing a purple shirt in summer and died prematurely. A man from Yíngzhōu, Zhang Huàilǐ, the county magistrate of Ānxīàn, and Jìn Xíngzhōng, the county magistrate of Gōnggāo in Cāng Prefecture, consulted with Cài Wēiyuǎn for divination. After completing the divination, Cài Wēiyuǎn told Zhang Huàilǐ, "You will soon rise to a high position, reaching the rank of Fāngbó (a high-ranking official)." To Jìn Xíngzhōng, he said, "You will become a capital official this year, but your term will end, and you should retire." Both men took the imperial examinations. Zhang Huàilǐ was appointed as Zuǒ Bǔquē (a censor) and later served as the governor of two prefectures. Jìn Xíngzhōng was appointed as Chéngménláng (a gatekeeper) and died in autumn. In the second year of the Kaiyuan era, a Taoist priest from Liángzhōu, Liáng Xūzhōu, used the Nine Palaces to predict that Zhang Zhuo would face a year of great misfortune, with the Five Ghosts adding to his year and the Heavenly Warrior influencing his fate. Using the *I Ching* for divination, he encountered the hexagrams *View* (观) and *Scattering* (涣), indicating fear and anxiety. Later, the situation related to feng shui improved. A Taoist priest at An Guo Temple, Lǐ Míng, without revealing his name, secretly instructed someone to perform divination, predicting that Zhang Zhuo would be imprisoned in the Heavenly Jail and face a capital crime, but could be spared. If not, he would die of illness with no remedy. Indeed, Zhang Zhuo was accused by the censor Li Quan Jiao and ordered to be executed. However, the Minister of Justice Li Zhibai, the Left Censor Zhang Tinggui, Cui Xuansheng, and others petitioned for clemency, and he was spared and exiled to Lingnan. The predictions of the two Taoist priests were indeed verified.

In Quanzhou, a guest named Lu Yuanqin contracted a severe skin disease, but his nose remained unaffected. On the fifth day of the fifth month, officials obtained the gall of a king cobra, intending to present it to the emperor. Someone suggested that snake meat could cure the disease, so they took a piece of snake meat and ate it. After a few days, Lu Yuanqin showed signs of improvement and fully recovered within a hundred days. In Shangzhou, another person contracted a severe skin disease. His family, fearing the disease, isolated him in a straw hut in the mountains. A black snake fell into a wine jar. The patient drank the wine without knowing it contained snake, and he gradually improved. Later, he found snake bones at the bottom of the jar, realizing the cause of his recovery. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the Fengge Shilang Zhou Yunyuan entered the government office after court. Princess Taiping summoned a doctor who entered through the Guangzheng Gate. The doctor saw a ghost grabbing Zhou Yunyuan's head, with two other ghosts following with sticks. They entered the Jingyun Gate directly. The doctor reported this to Princess Taiping, who then informed the emperor. The emperor sent attendants to investigate, finding no issues in the office. Zhou Yunyuan returned to his room and later went to the toilet. A senior official wondered why it took so long and went privately to check on him. Zhou Yunyuan was lying face down, staring straight ahead without speaking, with saliva dripping from his mouth. The attendants reported this to the emperor, who asked the doctor. The doctor said it could be prevented if treated within a certain time. The emperor covered Zhou Yunyuan with a brocade quilt and had him carried to his residence. He died in the middle of the night. The emperor wrote a poem to mourn him.

In the year of Longsight, there was a man named Yang Yuanliang from Xiangzhou, who was around twenty years old. He worked at the Wenshan Temple in Fangzhou. One day, he dreamed of a divine being who said, "My temple is in disrepair. You should repair it for me. I will grant you the ability to cure all diseases." Upon waking, Yang realized the dream and began to test his newfound ability. He found that he could indeed cure all illnesses. In Gan County, there was a village headman whose back had a swelling as large as a fist. Yang cut it with a knife, and within a few days, it healed. He earned ten thousand coins per day by treating patients. After building the temple for the divine being, his healing ability gradually diminished. In the year of Ruyi, there was a man named Zhao Xuanjing from Luozhou who died and came back to life after five days. He claimed to have seen a monk who gave him a piece of wood about a foot long and instructed him to use it to treat illnesses. Zhao later found the wood on a table. He tested the wood, and it immediately cured the patient. His home became crowded with hundreds of patients every day. The censor Ma Jizhi investigated Zhao for gathering a crowd. The patients filled the left office of the censor's headquarters. Empress Wu Zetian heard about Zhao and summoned him to the palace. She had her own patients treated with the wood, and they were cured. She allowed Zhao to treat the common people. After several months, Zhao earned more than seven hundred strings of cash. Eventually, the wood lost its effectiveness and disappeared. In Luozhou, there was a man who suffered from a condition where his throat responded to speech. When he spoke, his throat would respond. He sought help from the skilled doctor Zhang Wenzhong. Zhang thought about it overnight and came up with a method. He read the *Bencao* (Herbal Medicine) aloud, and the man's throat stopped responding when it reached a particular herb. Zhang collected the herbs, combined them into pills, and the man took them. The condition was cured immediately. Another account says he consulted the doctor Su Cheng. Hao Gongjing was collecting herbs on Mount Tai when he encountered a ghost who saw him and ran away. Hao made a pill to kill ghosts. There was a patient who suffered from an illness, and when he took the pill, he was cured.

A person from Dingzhou, Cui Wu, fell off a horse and broke his foot. The doctor ordered him to take copper powder mixed with wine, and he was completely cured. After his death, more than ten years later, when he was reburied, it was seen that his fractured shinbone was bound with copper powder. In the Lingnan region, it is a custom to make poison, give it to slaves to eat, and bury them in the ground. Mushrooms grow directly over their stomachs, and eating them causes immediate death. Those that grow on hands, feet, and forehead die on the same day; those growing from the sides die in a few days; those growing further away may die in a month, two months, or even up to three years. Only Chen Huaiqing's family medicine can cure it; if it is applied to the head of a horsewhip and waved around, it is toxic, and touching it or wiping it on the mouth will cause death. Zhao Yanxi said that for a bite from a venomous snake, apply moxa to the spot and burn it, which will immediately heal the wound; otherwise, it will be fatal. For any snake bite, burn the spot where it bit, and the poison will be drawn away. Eating wild gourd causes immediate death; where wild gourd grows, there is white morning glory that can cure its poison. Where the kingfisher drinks, there is a rhinoceros that does not wash its horn; anything that drinks that water will die because the kingfisher eats snakes. Medical books say that a tiger bitten by a poisoned arrow eats clear mud; a wild boar bitten by a poisoned arrow eats wild ginger and wild ginger; a pheasant wounded by an eagle has its wound treated with rehmannia leaves. Orpiment can harm mice; Zhang Zhuo once tried it, and the mice became intoxicated, not recognizing people, but still knew to drink muddy water to recover. Birds, beasts, insects, and other creatures can be cured of poison; how much more so humans? For bee stings, apply beetle powder; for horse bites, apply burned horsewhip ash. It is taken from their mutual compatibility. For spider bites, apply realgar powder; for broken tendons, take the root of *Inula japonica*, squeeze out the juice, place the tendons opposite each other, and apply the juice and seal them; they will reconnect as before. In Sichuan, children who run away often cut their tendons and use this method to reconnect them, with no failures.

During the Yonghui period, there was a man named Cui Shuang who would eat three dou of raw fish to be satisfied. Later, when he was hungry and preparing to make sashimi, it was not yet ready. Cui Shuang could not resist his hunger and then vomited an object shaped like a toad. From then on, he could no longer eat sashimi. During the illness of Cui Rong, who served as the head of the Imperial College and was in charge of drafting imperial edicts, he was sick for over a hundred days. He suffered from internal parasites that caused extreme pain he could not endure. He expelled an object shaped like a gecko from his lower body and needed to be cautious, eventually passing away. During the reign of Emperor Xiaowen of the Northern Wei, the four major families, including the Li family of Longxi, feared they would not be included. They traveled at night on a neighing camel, covering double the usual distance to Luoyang. By the time they arrived, the four major families had already been determined. Therefore, they are still called "Camel Li" today. Zhang Wencheng said that before the Danyang era, the number of candidates selected each year did not exceed several thousand. After the Chui Gong era, the number of candidates selected each year often reached fifty thousand. Without increasing the number of officials, the selection process became more complex. There must be a reason for this. Let me try to explain. For example, among the ten categories of officials, including those from the Mingjing and Jinshi examinations, the Three Guards, and other miscellaneous positions, only a fraction of the truly capable individuals were selected. The selection process was often manipulated by powerful families, and those who could not write were sent to the Eastern Office, while those who could not recognize writing were sent to the Southern Pavilion. There were also draftsmen, carriage drivers, and farmers who did not perform any actual work, and they were all given preferential treatment. They did not engage in learning but only sought wealth. Therefore, the selection process became so crowded that it was like a flock of sheep, and the Ministry of Personnel was so noisy that it was like an ant colony. If the selection process were truly based on merit, there would be no one suitable. The problem has been accumulating for a long time.

During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong, Vizier Zheng Yin, as the head of the Ministry of Personnel, presided over a corrupt and disorderly selection process. Candidates would be called for selection, and one candidate tied a hundred coins to the belt of his boots. Zheng Yin asked him why, and the candidate replied, "In today's selection, without money, it cannot be done." Zheng Yin remained silent. At the same time, Cui Shi, also serving as the head of the Ministry of Personnel, had a candidate who, when called for selection, claimed he could lift heavy gates and carry grain. Cui Shi said, "If you are so strong, why not apply to the Ministry of War?" The candidate replied, "Everyone outside says that those with strength under Cui Vizier will be selected." During the Jinglong era, three hundred officials were appointed through backdoor channels, rising to high positions from the ranks of butchers and peddlers. When Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, Shangshu Song Jing and Yushi Daifu Bi Gou petitioned to stop the appointments of those who were appointed through backdoor channels. After Song Jing and Bi Gou left office, a ghost named Peng Qing accepted bribes from those who were appointed through backdoor channels and reported to the emperor, saying, "I saw Emperor Xuanzong's anger and he said, 'I gave people positions, why did you take them away?' As a result, those appointed through backdoor channels were all restored to their old positions." During the revolution of the false Zhou dynasty, officials were sent to the ten circuits, and the selection process was disrupted. Those who passed the Mingjing and Jinshi exams and village teachers were all searched and promoted without being tested or trained, and were given good positions, degrading the reputation of scholars and appealing to the hearts of the ignorant. Those with mediocre talent gained positions and felt honored, while those with real talent gained positions and felt humiliated. When Zhao Wang Lun usurped power, the whole country's filial and clean officials, talented scholars, and extraordinary individuals were not selected through proper testing and were given positions indiscriminately. Merchants, butchers, and fugitives who were not law-abiding were all made marquises, almost all the copper from the Taifu was used up and not enough was left to make seals, and even those with white board marquis titles. More than half of the court officials wore sable fur hats. The common people were fortunate, but the virtuous felt ashamed. How corrupt the court was, alas!

During the reign of Empress Wu, when the Khitan leaders Li Jinzhong and Wan Rong attacked and defeated the Tang military camps, they imprisoned hundreds of Han captives in underground prisons. When they learned that General Ma Renjie and other officials were approaching, they ordered the guards to deceive the prisoners by saying, "Our families are starving and freezing, unable to survive. We will wait for the arrival of the national army and then surrender." The prisoners were given a bowl of porridge each day and led out to be comforted by the statement, "I have no food to feed you, but I cannot bear to kill you. I will let you all go." The prisoners bowed and begged for their lives, and so they were deceived and released. Upon reaching Youzhou, the prisoners told of their hunger and cold, which delayed the Tang soldiers. Upon hearing this, the soldiers raced to enter first, reaching Huangzhang Valley where the Khitans had set up an ambush. The Khitans then sent elderly men to the Tang army, offering old cows and skinny horses on the roadside. The Tang generals, Ma Renjie and others, abandoned their infantry and led their horses ahead, racing to enter. They were ambushed and captured by the Khitans, who had set up a trap and cut off their retreat. The generals were seized and killed, and the valley was filled with the dead, few of whom survived. In the fourth year of the Jinglong era, a fire broke out at Lingkong Temple in Luoyang, destroying everything. Only one statue of a Taoist deity remained standing. It was then made into a clay statue, and the temple was renamed Shengzhen Temple. At the northern gate of the imperial court in Chang'an, there was a large locust tree. During the Sui Dynasty, it was called the Tangxing Gate. Emperor Wen of Sui moved the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang. The chief architect Gao Jing often sat under this tree to inspect. The tree was planted crookedly, and there was a desire to remove it. The emperor said, "Gao Jing sat under this tree, so do not cut it down." Until today, one hundred and thirty years after the beginning of the Tianbao era, the tree still stands with its branches and leaves towering and its roots sprawling, different from other trees. Chenting Gate is directly opposite the Tangxing Gate, and now the Tang family resides there.

After the Yonghui era, people sang the song of mulberry branches, with a woman named Wei singing it. The joy reached the Shendong era, and Wei responded to the song. Zheng Yin, a flatterer, composed over ten songs of mulberry branches and presented them to Wei, who was very pleased and promoted Zheng Yin to the position of Secretary of the Ministry of Personnel, rewarding him with a hundred bolts of silk. Since the Longshuo era, people have been singing the song of Turkic salt. During the Shengli era, Yan Zhifang was dispatched to negotiate with the Xiongnu and was appointed as the Spring Official with the rank of third grade. Wu Yanshou was sent to marry the daughter of Moche, and gold and silver vessels, brocade, and clothing were sent as gifts. The Turks rebelled, captured the Han envoys, and made Yan Zhifang the Khan. This was the response to the Turkic salt song. During the Tiaolu era, the Great Emperor wanted to perform a ritual to honor Mount Song but it was stopped due to the Turks. Later, he wanted to perform a ritual to honor the soil but the Tibetans invaded, so it was stopped. During the Yongchun era, the emperor visited Mount Song, and a folk song said, "Mount Song has many layers, not afraid of not being able to climb, but afraid of not being able to climb." Three times the army was mobilized, and the roads were crowded with soldiers. The emperor fell ill at the foot of the mountain and died on his way back to the palace. After the Yongchun era, people all sang the song of willow trees. Later, Xu Jingye committed a crime and was exiled to be the governor of Liuzhou. He then forged an edict to appoint himself as the governor of Yangzhou, killed the Chief Secretary Chen Jingzhi, and rebelled in the Jianghuai region. Li Xiaoyi was sent to suppress the rebellion, and the head of Xu Jingye was brought back to Luoyang by fast horse. The song of willow trees was a response to these events.

During the year of Emperor Ruyi of Zhou, the Yellow Deer Song first began to be sung. Its lyrics say: "Yellow deer, yellow deer, hidden in the grass; a bow shoots you and you are wounded." Soon after, the Khitan rebelled, killing the governor Zhao Wei, and his camp and office were captured and destroyed. The supreme commander, Cao Renshi, Zhang Xuanyu, Ma Renshi, and Wang Xiaojie, along with hundreds of thousands of troops, were defeated by the rebels in the Yellow Deer Valley, and all the armies were annihilated, leaving no survivors. The Yellow Deer Song thus proved to be true. From the reign of Emperor Chui Gong onward, the lyrics of the Bina'er were all evil and crooked. Later, Zhang Yizhi, whose pet name was Bina'er, was a historical figure. In the year of Jinglong, Princess Anle built the Anle Temple in the Daoguang Ward of Luoyang, spending several million coins. A children's rhyme said: "Poor Anle Temple, the tree is hung high." Afterward, the rebel Wei was executed, and Princess Anle was also killed, and her head was hung on a pole, and she was posthumously titled a traitorous commoner. After the reign of Shenlong, a rhyme said: "South of the mountain, the nests of crows and magpies; north of the mountain, the golden camel. The sickle blade is not sharpened, and the axe is not fitted with a handle." This is the response to the Khitan's strength, where the people could not cut mulberry trees for silkworms, plant trees, or harvest grain. In the year of Jinglong, a rhyme said: "Poor Saintly Temple, dressed in green fur, pulled to drink in the river, and the carp was trampled to death." By the time of Jingyun, Prince Chao from Junzhou entered the capital to rebel, but he fled and was killed in Luoyang. In the year of Jingyun, a rhyme said: "A single hemp thread pulls the Tianzhu, it breaks away. When Emperor Shenwu ascended the throne, he ordered the Tianzhu to be torn down, and the copper was collected and sent to the imperial treasury." This is the omen. In the year of Jinglong, a rhyme said: "The yellow pine ox pulls the harness, the two feet are on the ground, the shoe laces are broken. In June, King Ping executed the rebel Wei, who wanted to rebel. The broken shoe laces mean the rebellion will not succeed. Wei is the descendant of the yellow ox."

The Chief Clerk of the Hall of Brightness, Lu Binwang, wrote in his *Capital City Ode*: "Suddenly, a wind rises, and wings are born; in an instant, lost in the waves and deposited in the mud and sand." Afterward, Lu Binwang joined Xu Jingye in raising an army in Yangzhou, suffered a great defeat, and drowned in the river, which was a warning to others. Since the Lintai era, when people drank and sang songs, those who continued singing after the music ended were called "salted fish." Later, Yan Zhiwei, who defected to the Turks, led them to conquer Zhao Ding. After Yan Zhiwei returned, Empress Wu was greatly angered and crucified him in the Western Market, ordering officials to shoot him. Prince Wu Yizong of Hebei took seven steps, fired three arrows, and all missed, revealing his cowardice. Yan Zhiwei's body was riddled with arrows like a hedgehog's spines, and his bones and flesh were crushed. His nine clans were eliminated, and even distant relatives and strangers were all executed. Children aged seven or eight were forced to watch, and people threw food and fruits at them, competing to hit them as a form of entertainment. The supervising censor could not bear to harm them and reported to save them. This is how the term "salted fish" came to be. Grand Tutor Changsun Wuji made a hat from black sheep wool, and the whole world admired it. His hat was called the "Sogdian Turban." Later, he was imprisoned and exiled to Lingnan for his crimes. The term "Sogdian Turban" was thus adopted. Prince Wei wore a headscarf worn forward, and the whole world admired it. It was called the "Prince Wei's Fall." After his death due to a crime, by the time of Emperor Xuanzong, Lu Song also wore a headscarf in the same style, and it was called the "Lu Song's Fall." Within a year, Lu Song died. After the Yonghui era, the whole world sang the song of Wu Meiniang. Later, Wu Zetian was made empress. The great emperor died, and Empress Wu took power, changing the dynasty to Great Zhou. For more than twenty years, Empress Wu was strong and powerful. The Three Princes of Wu, including Liang, Wei, and Ding, all established their own offices, and more than ten other marquises joined them. The transfer of power was imminent.

After the Xianheng era, people all said, "Do not speak carelessly, or the old lady will be angry with the third uncle." When the third uncle heard it, he laughed until he died. As a result, Empress Wu ascended the throne, and until Emperor Ruizong succeeded him, the old lady referred to was Empress Wu, and the third uncle referred to was Ruizong, the third son of Emperor Gaozong. Wei Shuyin, the son of the Chief Secretary, named Shuyin. The name "Shuyin" reversed reads as "execution." Consequently, he was accused and executed. Wu Sansi, the Prince of Liang, was given the title of Duke of Dejing at the beginning of the Shenlong era. The title "Dejing" was a metaphor for those who coveted the throne. He indeed had ambitions to seize the throne and was killed by Zheng Ke and others. During the reign of Empress Wu, there was a saying: "Zhang Gong drinks wine, Li Gong is drunk." "Zhang Gong" referred to the brothers of Zhang Yi, and "Li Gong" referred to the prosperity of the Li family. Sun Quan, the Governor of Youzhou, led a northern campaign in May. His military advisor, Li Chuyu, advised him, "In May, the south is fire and the north is water. Fire entering water will surely be extinguished." Sun Quan did not listen, and as a result, eight thousand men were lost. In the past, when Dou Jiande rescued Wang Shichong at Niukou Valley, he said, "Dou entering Niukou, how could there be a return?" Indeed, he was captured by Emperor Taizong. When Sun Quan went north, Li Chuyu said, "If food enters the throat, there is no hope of survival." People in Shandong called warm rice "su," and Youzhou and beyond were all part of Yan territory, hence the saying. Since the Longshuo era, people have made a drinking game: "Mother and child are separated, the platform collapses." "Mother and child" referred to the cup and plate, and "platform" referred to the plate collapsing and the cup falling over. During the Yongchang era of Empress Wu, the fabrication of crimes began. Ten palace guards were drinking in Qinghua Ward and played this game. One of them reported it, and all ten were executed in public. Later, when the original residence of the imperial family was exiled to Junzhou, "mother and child are separated" referred to the separation of the imperial family, and "platform collapses" referred to Empress Wu being deposed and the Wu family being exiled.

In the seventh month, Emperor Taizong ascended the throne in the eastern capital, Luoyang. At the White Horse Temple, the head of a bronze statue fell off the gate of the hall without apparent cause. After this, strict laws were enforced against monks and nuns, compelling them to show respect to their parents. Those who had not completed this ritual were dismissed, and those who left after being dismissed were punished. About eighty to ninety percent of them returned to secular life. In the fifth year of the Kaiyuan era, in spring, the official responsible for astronomy reported that there were anomalies in the celestial phenomena, indicating a severe disaster. Emperor Xuanzong was shocked and asked, "What is this omen?" The official replied, "There should be thirty distinguished scholars who will die unjustly on the same day. This year, the new successful candidates in the imperial examinations match this number." One of these successful candidates, Li, was the son-in-law of a wealthy nobleman. The emperor did not mention this matter but secretly warned the nobleman, saying, "Whenever there is a grand banquet, you may keep your son-in-law at home." The nobleman lived in Zhaoguo Lane, where music and songs were performed, and the Qujiang Pool was flooded, with several connected boats gathering all the successful candidates. Li heard about it and jumped over the wall to run away. The crowd was pleased, and he quickly boarded the boat, which then moved into the water, sinking levelly. The musicians, singers, and boatmen continued endlessly, and all thirty successful candidates died. Shah Hou Chuxin was the governor of Jingzhou. When a guest visited, he ordered his servant to prepare food. The servant whispered in his ear, saying, "We need to knead dough for the bread." Shah Hou replied, "Two people with two sheng each will suffice." The servant entered the kitchen but did not come out for a long time. The guest reported this to Shah Hou, who hurriedly called the servant. The servant said, "I have already kneaded the dough." Shah Hou exclaimed, "A strange event has occurred!" After a while, he said, "We can burn the dough and make it into cakes. I will eat them after my official duties." Shah Hou also once stored a small bottle of vinegar, one sheng, for himself. His family did not get any leftovers. The servant said the vinegar was finished. Shah Hou took the bottle and placed it in his hand, noticing a few drops remaining, and sucked them with his mouth before giving the price and leaving. In all market transactions, he was involved personally, and those who knew about it looked down on him.

Guangzhou Luqu Shangjun Liu Qing lived alone in one room, bringing all his utensils and food into his bedroom. When a servant privately took a pinch of salt, Liu Qing whipped him until blood was drawn. During the summer, Xiahoubiao would find worms in his food and drinks but never bothered to filter them out. Once, when he was sending guests out, a servant stole a piece of meat. When Xiahoubiao returned and discovered this, he became furious and caught a fly, fed it the meat, and made the servant vomit it out. Zheng Rengai served as the governor of Mì Prefecture. One day, a young servant reported that his shoes were worn out. Zheng said, "Grandfather will take care of it for you." Soon, a man with shoes arrived. Zheng ordered a servant to go up a tree and retrieve some woodpecker eggs. The man had to remove his shoes to climb the tree. Zheng then ordered the servant to put on the shoes and leave. The man finally arrived barefoot, and Zheng looked pleased. Deng You, the military commander of the southern region, was a wealthy man from Sháo Prefecture with a thousand servants and slaves. He always provided for their daily needs but never entertained guests. One day, his grandson took a duck for personal use. Deng punished him for misappropriating family assets, whipping him twenty times. Wei Zhuang, a well-read man, measured his rice before cooking and weighed his firewood before burning it. If even a small piece of meat was missing, he would notice. His son, who was eight years old, died. His wife wrapped the body in the standard clothing of the time, and Wei Zhuang stripped off the old mat to wrap the body. After the burial, he carried the mat back home, overwhelmed with grief and wept uncontrollably. It was only because he was miserly.

Lu Jingqian, the Clerk of the Court in Huizhou, was involved in an incident related to Qi Lianhui in Xinkai, where he was investigated and spared from death but exiled. After appealing and clearing his name, he was appointed as the magistrate of Suian County in Muzhou. All previous magistrates of the county had died in office, and Lu wanted to refuse the appointment. His wife said, "If you were meant to die, you would have perished in the difficulties of Xinkai long ago. Now that you have become a county magistrate, isn't it fate?" So he went to the state, where the distance to the county by water was several hundred miles. In the upper sleeping hall, there were three burial pits, all containing the remains of former county magistrates. Lu ordered the workers to fill them. An owl hooted on the screen and then on the ceiling, but he did not consider these things to be significant. Every time he ate with his wife, dozens of rats, some yellow, some white, some blue, some black, would appear. When he tried to drive them away with a stick, they would grab the stick and cry. Other supernatural occurrences were too numerous to mention. By the end of his four-year term, he had fulfilled his duties without any loss. He was later selected and appointed as the commandant of Wei, then as the military governor of Weizhou, and eventually became a Langzhong and Vice-Chancellor. Zhou Gan Zibu was well-learned and talented. At the age of seventeen, he became the Left Guard Historian but did not reach the fifth rank. In the year of Dengfeng, he fell ill and was carried by a donkey to the foot of a mountain. By imperial grace, he was granted two ranks, bringing him to the fifth rank, but he could not get up. Villagers and relatives came to congratulate him, but he could not wear formal attire. He was covered with a red robe and died calmly. Grand Master of Ceremonies Lu Chongdao was exiled to Lingnan for his son-in-law, Chief Secretary Cui Shi, who rebelled; and Yu Lin Langjiang Zhang Xian was exiled for verbally expressing intentions to rebel with Xue Jieren. For years, he mourned every day, and his eyes were swollen. Unable to bear the sorrow, they both fled back. Lu Chongdao hid and arranged for his son to marry a Cui family daughter, but before it was completed, an imperial messenger came to take her as a concubine. The messenger fully admitted and concealed it, and Lu Chongdao and his three sons were also arrested. They were each beaten with one hundred strokes and all died.

Governor Liu Rengui of Qingzhou, upon learning of the many ships lost during maritime transport, was stripped of his title and demoted to commoner status. Subsequently, he served in Liaodong to redeem himself. While lying outside the walls of Pingyang, he fell ill and pulled aside the curtain to watch soldiers attacking the city. Suddenly, a soldier came straight up to the front and sat with his back to him, scolding him and not leaving. The soldier continued to curse, saying, "You want to look, I want to look, what business is it of yours?" Refusing to leave, the soldier was soon hit by an arrow from the city wall, which struck him directly in the heart and killed him. Without this soldier, Governor Liu Rengui would have been hit by the arrow. Ren Zhi was selected to take the imperial examinations at the same time as Zhang Yue. After Zhang Yue became a Grand Secretary, Ren Zhi was never selected. He came to visit Zhang Gong, who gave him a bundle of silk to use for food expenses. On his way back to his residence, after not even one or two months, his illness worsened. Using the silk to buy medicine, and when the silk ran out, his illness worsened on its own. Not only did this happen, but it occurred in other places as well. How unfortunate is my fate! Governor Pei Youchang of Hangzhou fell gravely ill and ordered the County Magistrate of Qiantang, Xia Rong, to visit him. Xia Rong said, "Sir, there is nothing to worry about; your wife should build merit early to ward off misfortune." Lady Cui said, "What is needed to ward off misfortune?" Xia Rong replied, "Sir, you should marry two concubines to suppress it; if you do not do so within three years, the danger will be even greater." Lady Cui angrily said, "This barbarian is crazy; my son is healthy." Xia Rong stepped back and said, "Lady, if you do not believe me, I dare not speak further; Sir's fate is destined to have three wives; if he does not marry again, it will be a bad omen." Lady Cui said, "It is better to die than to be involved in such matters." That year, Lady Cui suddenly died. Pei Youchang remarried two more women, and Xia Rong's words proved true. Prince Guangping killed the traitorous Wei Cui, and General Du Qu led the troops to almost completely eliminate the Wei clan. Even infants in their cradles were strangled to death. Many others were also wrongfully killed. Fuhuizi said, "This is the crime of the traitorous Wei; why should distant relatives suffer? It is like Ran Min killing foreigners with high noses, or Dong Zhuo executing eunuchs without beards, all of which are unjust deaths; such is the will of fate."

During the An Lushan Rebellion, Zhang Jiafu, the Minister of Personnel, was appointed as the Governor of the Hebei Circuit and tasked with maintaining order and providing relief. Upon arriving at the Wu She Inn in Huizhou, he received an imperial edict ordering his execution upon arrival. However, shortly after, another edict was issued showing mercy and releasing him. The messenger, however, fell asleep on horseback and was delayed by one station. By the time he arrived, Zhang Jiafu had already been executed. This must be the will of heaven. Shen Junliang, who witnessed the events, recalled that in the first year of the Shangyuan era, Zhang Renyi, a mid-level official in the Ministry of Personnel, asked him when he would be promoted. Shen Junliang replied that Zhang Renyi had not been in his position long enough to get comfortable, so he should not worry about his promotion. Soon after, Zhang Renyi went to the bathroom, and Shen Junliang told his colleagues that Zhang Renyi would live for more than ten days and should not worry about his official position. However, seven days later, Zhang Renyi died. In another incident, Liu Zhimou, a local official in charge of military affairs, was temporarily managing the granary during a grand feast. He ordered the killing of pregnant cattle, pigs, sheep, and donkeys, claiming that their meat was tender and tasty. The fetuses continued to move for a long time before dying. Shortly after, Yang Shunchen's servant died without any apparent illness. The servant's heart warmed up, and he revived after seven days. He claimed to have seen a white-browed water buffalo and its offspring, who said they were killed because the mother was five months pregnant. Soon after, pigs, sheep, and donkeys with their offspring came to complain. Liu Zhimou admitted to the servant that he had followed Yang Shunchen's orders. Three days later, Liu Zhimou died, and five days after that, Yang Shunchen also died. In a different story, Zhang Wencheng, a local official in charge of timekeeping, heard an owl call out early in the morning from a tree in his courtyard. His wife thought it was an omen of bad luck and spat at it. Zhang Wencheng said that he should clean the house quickly because he was about to be promoted. Before he finished speaking, guests arrived to congratulate him. Another account states that in the second year of the Jingyun era, Zhang Wencheng was appointed as a vice minister of the Ministry of Rites. His hat, belt, and green robe were allegedly possessed by spirits, leading to false accusations. Thousands of families in the capital and other regions were executed, and Shu Prince You was implicated in these accusations. With the fall of the Sui Dynasty, the matter was eventually forgotten.

In the Yifeng era, a long star appeared in the eastern sky, visible for over thirty days before disappearing. From then on, there were rebellions by the Tu Fan, the Xiongnu, and Xu Jingye, as well as uprisings by Bai Tieyu, disturbances in Boyu, and the strong and fierce actions of the Khitans, who turned their camps, the Turks who broke through Zhao Ding, Ma Renshi, Zhang Xuanyu, and Wang Xiaojie, all leading to the loss of a million soldiers. For over thirty years, there was continuous warfare. After the Tiaolu era, a bird as large as a dove, colored like a crow, flew with a sound like a thousand winds, forming a group. People called it the "Shuque" or "Turkic Bird," and believed that if it appeared, the Turks would come, and indeed they did, without fail. During the Tianzhu era, Wu Zetian had a fondness for creating new characters and was superstitious. A man from Youzhou named Xun Ruyi submitted a petition, suggesting that the character "囗" (which means "country") should have the character "武" (military) placed inside to stabilize it. Wu Zetian was pleased and issued an edict to follow this suggestion. A month later, another petition came, stating that placing "武" inside "囗" made it look like the character for "prison" (囚), which was inauspicious. Wu Zetian was shocked and immediately issued another edict to change the character to "八方" (eight directions). After Emperor Zhongzong ascended the throne, he indeed confined Wu Zetian in the Shangyang Palace. In the second year of the Chang'an era, on September 1st, the sun was completely eclipsed, and the Murchech invaders reached Bingzhou. By the night of the 15th, the moon was also eclipsed, and the invaders retreated. Folk sayings went: "When jujubes block the nose, hang the building; when cicadas call, the locusts will come; when the millet is sown, the porridge will be cut; when the wheat sprouts, the magpies will nest on the ground." That year, there was a great flood. In the fourth year of the Chang'an era, in October, there was continuous rain and snow for over a hundred days, and the stars were not visible. In January, the traitors Zhang Yizhi and Chang Zhong were executed, and Wu Zetian was deposed.

When the Youzhou Governor Sun Jian fell into enemy hands, General Xue Ne wrote to him, saying, "It is extremely inauspicious to enter the enemy's territory in the last month of the year." Sun Jian replied, "In the sixth month, Duke Xuan led a northern expedition. What do you know? Anyone who dares to say that the army will not return will be executed." On the day the army set out, a white rainbow hung from the entrance of the camp, and that night a large star fell within the camp. Soldiers and generals dared not speak up. After the army left, all ravens, kites, and other birds within Youzhou disappeared and followed the army. After twenty days, the army was annihilated, and the birds ate their flesh. In the first month of the Yanyuan era, on the seventh day, Venus was visible in the daytime across the sky. That month, the emperor abdicated to become the retired emperor, an omen of a change in leadership. By the ninth day of the eighth month, Venus was still visible in the daytime, and the era name was changed to Tianxian. On the seventh day of the second month, the retired emperor had the Chancellor of the Central Secretariat, Xiao Zhizhong, and the Vice-Chancellor, Cen Xi, executed, and the official Cui Shi exiled before eventually executing him. In the second year of the Kaiyuan era, on the night of the twenty-ninth day of the fifth month, a large meteor shower occurred, with large meteors resembling jars or basins passing through the Big Dipper and smaller ones following in great numbers, causing all stars to tremble until dawn. In the seventh month, the Prince of Xiang died and was posthumously titled. In October, the Tibetans invaded Longyou, stealing livestock and causing numerous casualties. In June of that year, a strong wind uprooted trees and blew off roofs, with about seventeen out of twenty trees in Chang'an being uprooted. The construction of Chang'an City began with the planting of over three hundred locust trees by Gao Jing, the Chief Architect of the Sui Dynasty, which were uprooted during the storm. In the Zhongnan Mountains, bamboo bloomed and produced seeds, covering the mountains like wheat. The following year was a great famine, with bamboo dying throughout the region. The same phenomenon occurred in Lingnan, where people ate the bamboo seeds. In Lique, a rain of noodles like rice grains fell, which people could eat. Later, Pei Kai of the Eastern Han Dynasty said that when bamboo and cypress trees in the country wither, the owner will die within three years, and if a bamboo tree in a household withers and produces fruit, the head of the household will die. In the fourth year of the Kaiyuan era, the retired emperor died.

During the fifth year of Kaiyuan, the states of Hongzhou and Tanzhou experienced another series of fires. During the day, people saw red, glowing embers, and wherever they appeared, fires broke out. In the Eastern Jin dynasty, when Wang Hong served as the governor of Wu Prefecture, he also encountered a similar disaster. He punished his subordinates for being careless. Later, due to an incident involving a red object resembling a flag flying towards a house, a fire broke out, revealing that the disaster was not caused by human error. Those affected by the fire were thus exempt from punishment. In the eighth year of Kaiyuan, the Khitan people rebelled, and Chinese troops were sent to save the situation. They camped near the Yellow River, where a sudden flood swept away over 20,000 people. Only those who stayed awake playing dice and took high ground were saved. Villages and shops were destroyed, and in the Shangyang Palace, nearly 18 out of 20 palace women died. In the same year, a section of the capital city sank into a lake, submerging 500 households. Initially, in Dengzhou, three crows' mouths saw two children splashing water, followed by the appearance of a large snake over ten encirclements wide, opening its mouth to the sky. Some people tried to shoot it, and soon after, a heavy rainstorm caused more flooding, drowning 200 households and the children and snake, whose whereabouts are unknown.

The County Magistrate of Luoyang, Song Zhixun, was fond of singing and was appointed to the position of military officer in Lianzhou. The prefect Chen Xigǔ was an ordinary person. Song Zhixun ordered the maid to sing, and every day he held a jade tablet respectfully in the courtyard, singing, while the maid followed through the window and sang along. Those who heard it all laughed loudly. Volume One of Chronicles of the Court and the World* *The Imperial Library, Four Treasuries Volume Two of Chronicles of the Court and the World* *By Zhang Zhuo of the Tang Dynasty During the Northern Qi period, the Prince of Nanyang entered the capital. The emperor asked what he found most enjoyable. The prince replied, "The most enjoyable thing is to catch scorpions." They were then caught and placed in a large bathing tub, which could hold two hundred stones. One person removed his clothes and entered the tub, only to be stung to death by the scorpions. He writhed and cried out in unbearable pain for a short time before dying. The emperor and the prince watched him die. At the end of the Sui Dynasty, during a time of chaos, the bandit Zhu Càn rose in the area between Xiang and Dang. In a year of famine, a measure of rice cost ten thousand coins, and there was no way to get it. People ate each other. Zhu Càn then drove men and women of all ages to a large bronze bell, which could hold two hundred stones, cooked human flesh to feed the bandits, and destroyed the lives of the people there. Zhou En, the prefect of Zhouzhou, was a major leader in the south of the Five Ridges. He specially sent his sons and soldiers to rob the river. A county magistrate from Annan came, and Zhou En relied on him to buy two maids. The magistrate had a difficult expression. Zhou En set up a feast every day, inviting him with great courtesy, and sent him off at the riverside pavilion. Immediately after, he sent his sons and soldiers to rob and kill, taking all the wealth, bringing his wife and daughters to the prefecture. The wife knelt and begged to be a maid, but he did not allow it, and also strangled her to death, taking her daughter. Before and after, officials' families who were closely related and treated with great courtesy were always robbed and killed, and no one could escape.

During the Zhou dynasty, the magistrate of Lin'an, Xue Zhen, had a penchant for eating human flesh. A creditor and a slave arrived at Lin'an and stayed at an inn. Xue Zhen invited them to drink with him. He got them drunk, killed them, and chopped them into pieces, then cooked them with mercury and melted down to the bones. Later, he wanted to eat his wife, but she became aware of his intentions and fled. The county magistrate investigated and uncovered the truth. The recorder of the state of Lin'an reported this to the imperial court, and Xue Zhen was sentenced to a hundred strokes with a bamboo cane and died. During the Zhou dynasty, the leader of Lingnan, Chen Yuanguang, set up a banquet and ordered a servant to serve wine. Chen became angry and ordered him to be dragged out and killed. His body was boiled until it was soft and served to the guests. Later, his hands were shown to the guests, who were terrified and choked and vomited. During the Zhou dynasty, the governor of Yingzhou, Dugu Zhuang, was cruel. When a bandit did not confess, he said if he were a soldier, he would confess everything and be released. He then returned his headband, and the bandit confessed everything, and all officials thought he would be released. But soon Dugu said bring me a tool, it was a long iron hook, very sharp, and he hung it on a tree. He told the bandit, you have never heard of soldiers dying under hooks, now I will hook you and send a strong man to pull the rope, and the hook will come out of your brain. He asked the judge, how is this method? The judge said it is appropriate to pacify the people and punish the guilty, Dugu laughed. Later he was demoted to be the governor of Shizhou. He fell ill and only thought of human flesh. He had his dead slaves and servants' rib meat cut and eaten, and he died after a year. During the Zhou dynasty, the interrogator Suyanli was called the 'Suo' interrogator. He interrogated prisoners by making an iron cage for their heads and also added wedges, often causing their brains to split and their marrow to come out. He also made phoenixes spread their wings, macaques drill fire, etc., he bound their hands and feet with wooden poles and turned them, and chopped their bones to pieces. He also hung prisoners under the beams and lowered them with stones. His cruel methods were like this. Suyanli was an outsider, he was the foster father of Xue Shizhi. Later he was convicted of corruption and exiled to die in Lingnan.

Zhou Lai Junchen fabricated charges against people and submitted petitions first, and the imperial decree followed the petition, leading to the confiscation and seizure of their property. Xu Yougong released a death row inmate and also submitted a petition first, stating that a certain person's crime should be forgiven, and the imperial decree followed the petition, and then the case was cleared. Xu Yougong often released death row inmates and always made recommendations to the emperor, not acting independently. Zhou Buque Qiao Zhizhi had a concubine named Bi Yu, who was graceful, beautiful, and talented in poetry and music. Qiao Zhizhi favored her and did not marry her. False Wei Prince Wu Chengsi temporarily borrowed her to teach the women in his household how to dress and comb their hair, and then did not return her. Qiao Zhizhi knew this and wrote the "Green Pearl Lament" to express his sorrow. Bi Yu read the poem, wept, refused to eat for three days, and ultimately drowned herself. Wu Chengsi pulled the poem out of her belt and discovered it. The person who fabricated charges reported him, and he was executed in the southern market, and his family was ruined and their property confiscated.

Zhang Yizhi, a prominent figure during the Tang Dynasty, held the position of 控监 (a specific title, possibly related to a type of official or supervisor). His brother Changzong served as the Secretary Monarch, and another brother Changyi was the magistrate of Luoyang. They competed in extravagance. Zhang Yizhi used a large iron cage to place geese and ducks inside. He lit a fire in the center, placed a copper basin with a mixture of five flavors, and the birds would run around the fire. When thirsty, they would drink the broth, and when the fire was painful, they would return. The birds were cooked through, their feathers fell off, and the meat turned red. They died when thoroughly cooked. Changzong used a live donkey in a similar manner, placing it in a small room, lighting a fire, and using the same method. Changyi used an iron stake driven into the ground, tying a dog's four legs to it, and releasing eagles and falcons to attack the live dog. The dog was eaten after its meat was consumed, but it was still alive and crying out in pain, making the sound unbearable to listen to. Zhang Yizhi once passed by Changyi and remembered a horse's intestine. He took a horse from his entourage, broke its ribs, and extracted the intestine, which took a long time to die. After their execution, the common people cut and ate the meat, which was as white and fatty as pork fat, fried or grilled. Changyi had his legs broken, his heart and liver pulled out, and then he died. His head was cut off and sent to the capital. A proverb says that the news of their death was spread quickly.

During the Tang Dynasty, Zhou Xing, a high-ranking official known for his cruel methods of investigation and prosecution, was notorious for his use of torture beyond the law. People called him "Old Lady with a Bull's Head" due to his intimidating demeanor, and the populace complained and slandered him. Zhou Xing then posted an edict on the door, stating that all accused individuals claimed they were innocent, and after execution, there was no more speech. Zhou Sizhi, a low-born individual from Lique who sold bread and pies, rose to power through informer activities. He was rewarded with a fifth-rank official position for reporting crimes and asked the emperor for a higher position. When the emperor asked if he knew how to read, he replied, "A mythical creature (Xiezhi) doesn't need to know how to read; it is for the country to touch the guilty." The emperor then appointed him, and he investigated and killed many people. He had no other words but told the prisoners, "Don't use your written words, just return my White司马. If you refuse, Lai Jun will give you Meng Qing, and you will suffer greatly." Those who died by non-fate were countless. The White司马Bamboo is the司马Bamboo on the North Mausoleum Hill, Lai Jun is the Censor-in-Chief Lai Junchen, and Meng Qing is the General Meng Qing's rod. Later, he was executed for privately storing silk.

The Minister of the Temple of Heaven, Ji Xu, spent the night with the Censorial Inspector, Wang Zhu, at an inn. Wang Zhu, due to their familial connection, spoke of a prophecy concerning Qí Lián Yào’s sons, Dà Jué and Xiǎo Jué, who were said to embody the auspicious symbol of a two-horned qilin (mythical creature). Qí Lián Yào himself was known by the alias "Guāng Zhì," which was interpreted as a foretelling of his dominion over the world. The next day, Ji Xu recorded the details and submitted them to Lái Jùnchén, who then had the case investigated by Hé Nèi Wáng Yìzōng. As a result, Wang Zhu and forty others were executed, and their families were disgraced. Later, when Lái Jùnchén himself became involved in a scandal, the judiciary sentenced him to death, but the emperor did not issue the order for three days, causing public astonishment. When the emperor entered the imperial garden, Ji Xu approached him on horseback and asked what was troubling him. Ji Xu replied that he was fortunate to serve as the emperor’s eyes and ears, and he found it strange that the execution order for Lái Jùnchén had not yet been issued. The emperor responded that Lái Jùnchén had served the state well, and he was still considering the matter. Ji Xu then reported that Yú Ānyuǎn had accused Huǐ Zhēn of treason, and the evidence supported the claim; Huǐ Zhēn was now serving as a magistrate in Chengzhou. Ji Xu further stated that Lái Jùnchén had gathered a group of lawless individuals, falsely accused virtuous people, and accumulated bribes like a mountain, leaving the streets filled with the spirits of the wrongfully accused. Such a person was a traitor to the state, and what was there to pity? The emperor then ordered the execution of Lái Jùnchén in the western market, reinstated Yú Ānyuǎn to his former position as a courtier responsible for the imperial kitchen, and rewarded Ji Xu with the title of Censorial Inspector and the honor of wearing a red robe. Ji Xu was later appointed to handle the affairs of Qí Lián Yào, and for this service, he was granted the titles of Minister of Personnel and Senior Minister. However, he was eventually demoted to the position of magistrate in Wenzhou, where he died.

The King of Cheng sent someone over a thousand miles to the southern regions to fetch a large snake, about eight to nine feet long, and tied its mouth with a rope, placing it horizontally under the threshold of the door. Officials from various states and counties who paid homage were called to enter but could only look straight ahead and not look around. They were frightened, collapsed, and were wrapped by the snake several times. After a long time, they were freed and treated as a joke. The king then captured turtles and tortoises, made people remove their clothes, and allowed the turtles to bite their bodies. The turtles did not release them until they died. The people suffered severe pain, cried out, and could not speak anymore. The king and his concubines watched together, treating it as entertainment. Then, using bamboo, they poked the turtles' mouths, and the turtles bit the bamboo and released the people. They burned the tortoises' backs with mugwort, causing pain, and released the people who were tested. All of them lost their souls and could not recover even after death. The Governor of the Northern Border, Zhang Dan, was fond of killing. When the Turks surrendered, he wrote a proclamation to insult Mochuo, with words that were extremely disrespectful. He wrote on their backs, carved into their flesh, stained them with ink, and roasted them with fire, causing unbearable pain. They made sounds like insects and birds day and night. Then, he sent them to Mochuo, and the literate person read the proclamation. They were cut into pieces and killed. The Xiongnu were resentful and dared not surrender.

In the court of the palace, the Palace Attendant and Imperial Censor Wang Xu conducted an investigation into the women living in separate houses within a residence, focusing on their reputations and appearances. Those who did not comply were bound with ropes around their genital areas and struck with bamboo sticks, causing unbearable pain. One woman was hung upside down and struck on the head with a stone. They were accused of adultery with the magistrate of Chang'an, Fang Heng, and refused to confess for three days. The woman said, "If the magistrate treats me so cruelly, I will report him to the authorities when I die. If I am sent to the palace, I will appeal to the emperor. He will never let me go." Wang Xu, fearing the consequences, released her. The Imperial Censor Li Song and Li Quan, along with Wang Xu, were known in the capital as the "Three Tigers." Li Song was the "Red Tiger," Li Quan was the "White Tiger," and Wang Xu was the "Black Tiger." All three were ruthless and lawless, using poison without restraint. They had wild and careless personalities and were cruel and harsh in spirit. Whenever they interrogated prisoners, they would bind them, poke them with bamboo sticks, press on their knees, and use broken tiles. They would make prisoners climb ladders, hang upside down, and suffer from extreme conditions. The prisoners begged for death, and the "Three Tigers" would arbitrarily torture and fabricate evidence, claiming it as true. Even revered figures like Zhou Gong and Confucius would be forced to confess to crimes, and virtuous figures like Bo Yi and Shu Qi would be accused of crimes. Interrogations would continue until the victims were exhausted or dead. Victims were treated like students or apprentices of these torturers. Those being pursued would swear oaths, threatening to face the "Three Tigers" if they betrayed their principles. The victims' families and friends were permanently separated from them, and people in the capital would swear oaths, threatening to face the "Three Tigers" if they betrayed their principles. The cruelty of the "Three Tigers" was such that it instilled fear in the hearts of all.

A Man from Jingzhao Named Gao Li Gao Li, a poor man from the capital region, worked in the Imperial Censorate delivering official documents for an official. At that time, a lower-ranking official forged a document and gave it to Gao Li to chase after someone and threaten for money. The matter failed, the lower-ranking official fled, and the pursuit was unsuccessful. The Imperial Censor Zhang Xiaosong caught Gao Li and beat him until his kneecaps fell off, causing both legs to be paralyzed. He was then suppressed and sent to replace the lower-ranking official, who bore false charges, and was sentenced to death according to the law. The Chief Justice reported the case, stating that according to precedent and the penal code, a person with a severe illness should not be punished. Zhang Xiaosong suddenly changed color with anger and said, "Paralyzed legs, what use? He could still forge orders. Then carry him to the market and behead him." Xie You, the Military Governor of Qianfu, was cruel and dangerous. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, a member of the imperial family was exiled to the Qianzhong region. Xie You threatened to say that Empress Wu Zetian had granted self-slaughter and personally followed orders, with no other edict. The prince was terrified and hanged himself. Afterward, Xie You slept on a flat platform with more than ten concubines. At night, he did not notice assassins cutting off his head and fleeing. Later, the family broke, and records were compiled. The head was painted and inscribed with Xie You's name, considered as a filthy object. Then it was known that the prince had ordered assassins to kill him.

The Uyghur leader Zhou Moche captured the city of Hengding and took the envoy for marriage alliance, Yang Qi Zhang, a high-ranking official of the third rank, into the territory of the Xiongnu, where he was subsequently captured. As the Uyghurs advanced towards Zhaozhou, the loyal official Duan Cong was also captured. Duan Cong called for Yang Qi Zhang to escape together, but Yang Qi Zhang was afraid and dared not act, so Duan Cong returned first. Empress Wu Zetian rewarded Duan Cong and restored his former position. Soon after, Yang Qi Zhang arrived, and the emperor ordered the Duke of Hedong, Yi Zong, to interrogate him. Yang Qi Zhang said that someone had predicted he would reach the third rank but would face danger from knives and arrows. He had escaped but was chased, cut, and shot, but did not die, and he hoped Yi Zong would pity him. Yi Zong had a cruel and vicious nature and reported that Yang Qi Zhang had initially hesitated and requested his execution. The emperor agreed and ordered Yang Qi Zhang to be led to the south of Tianjin Bridge and tied to the drum stand of the guardhouse, with his hands and feet spread out. Duan Cong was ordered to shoot three arrows, none of which hit. Then Duan Jin shot and hit. All officials were ordered to shoot arrows, like a hedgehog's spines. He still breathed faintly. They then cut him open with a knife, took out his heart, and threw it on the ground. He still twitched and jumped dozens of times. Yi Zong's cruelty was thus evident. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, Yang Wuling constructed the warehouses for the Longning and Anle mansions. Upon completion, he was specially appointed as the Grand Constructor. However, he was involved in embezzlement of tens of thousands of coins and was dismissed from his position. He later submitted a memorial reporting that in the Sanmen Pass of Shaanxi, mountains were being blasted and rocks burned to create a栈道 (suspended path) for pulling ships. The river was swift, and the laborers were not paid. If the ropes broke or the栈道 (suspended path) collapsed, dozens of people would be killed. To cover up the lack of payment, the money from the laborers was used to buy rice to make up the numbers, and the laborers were reported as having escaped. Their parents, brothers, and wives were all imprisoned, and those pulling the ships were ordered to wear two iron balls on their chests and backs. When the栈道 (suspended path) collapsed and they fell onto the rocks, none survived. The roads were filled with the cries of the dying, and the sound echoed through the mountains. Everyone called Yang Wuling a monster, saying that Heaven had created this monster to destroy the people.

The Imperial Censor Li Quan Jiao was notorious for his cruel and harsh methods of fabricating charges, earning him the nickname "Human Head Rākṣasa" in the imperial court. Wang Xu, a central official, was known as "Ghost-Faced Yakṣa" for his terrifying appearance. When interrogating prisoners, he would pull the wooden handle of the manacle forward, a method he called "Donkey Pulling Out a Stake." He would also hang the manacle on a tree, a practice he named "Calf Hanging from a Cart." He would have the prisoner hold the manacle while stacking bricks on top, a method he called "Immortal Offering Fruits." He would then stand the prisoner on a tall tree and twist the manacle backward, a technique he named "Maid Climbing a Ladder." He used these methods to interrogate Liao Fu, a minor official in Liuzhou, and Zhang Xing, a gatekeeper, and even summoned spirits and ghosts to help him, claiming that he could turn birds into phoenixes and snakes into dragons. Chen Huaiqing was a man from the southern region of China who raised over a hundred ducks. One day, while cleaning the duck pen, he noticed a bright light in the excrement. He used a basin of water to sift through it and found ten taels of gold. Curious, he observed where the ducks had been eating and discovered a spot under the foot of a mountain behind his house. Digging there, he found more gold, which he smelted into several dozen pounds. No one knew how he had become so wealthy, and he eventually rose to the position of Governor of Wuzhou. In the early years of the Zhou Dynasty, Xia Wenrong was a county magistrate in Changjiang, Suizhou. At the time, people believed he was skilled in divination and fortune-telling, and Zhang Zhe, a censor, was sent to serve as the warehouse manager in Chuzhou. After Zhang returned, he visited Xia and, with a stick, drew the character for "liu" (柳) on the ground, saying, "You will serve in this state." Six months later, Xia was indeed appointed to the same position, and later he was promoted to magistrate of Pingchang in Dezhou. Zhang was known for his precise calculations of time and the sun and moon, and his predictions were always accurate. In Suzhou, Yang Tingyu was the magistrate of Jiaxing. He was the nephew of Wu Zetian by marriage and was known for his greed and insatiable appetite for money. He wrote a poem that said, "Turning back the waves, Tingyu beat the barbarians to collect money, but it was not enough. His aunt, now the Empress, saw him as a king, and no one else could touch him." He was eventually accused by Kang Li, a censor, and sentenced to death. At the time, his mother was in the capital and saw Xia Wenrong, who demanded a thousand sheets of yellow and white paper from her.

After ten days, Mother came as he had said. Rong said, "I will be spared from death." Indeed, within ten days, an imperial decree came to change the remaining years of the old mother. Also, the Tian Guan commandant, Liu Wujì, forged a letter to Rong, saying that the Wei Han Liu clan was unstable, with Wei and Han states alternating unpredictably. Indeed, later, Guan Zhong was appointed as the recorder of Wei state, and immediately after, he was appointed as the recorder of Han state. At that time, the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant submitted a petition to the emperor, accusing the Tian Guan commandant of unfair appointments. The emperor blamed the Left Censor Cui Xuanwei, who replied, "My appointments are extremely fair." The emperor said, "If so, let the Tian Guan commandant and the Ling Tai Feng Ge commandant exchange positions." Thus, Liu Wujì was made the chief clerk of Liuzhou, and the Ling Tai commandant was made the recorder of Han state. During the Zhou dynasty, the official Zhang Yiwang moved his old residence for renovation and saw a ghost. Feng Yi saw it and said, "There is a hidden corpse in your new hall, a third-rank general from the Jin dynasty. He is extremely angry. You should avoid it." Zhang smiled and said, "Since I was young, I have never known such a thing. My parents often talk about it." After more than a month, Feng entered and saw the ghost holding a bow and arrow following Zhang after he ascended the steps. The ghost drew his bow and shot Zhang in the armpit and spleen. Zhang felt the pain himself and touched it with his hand. On that day, he died. During the Zhou dynasty, the Left Censor Zheng Congjian's residence was often not good. He ordered a witch to observe it and found that there was a hidden corpse with the surname Zong and the wife's surname Kou under the foundation. He asked it, "You sit on my door. I often meet you when I enter and leave. It is not me who is bad, but you." They dug three meters deep and indeed found the old remains with an inscription as he had said. They moved it out and reburied it, and thus the problem was resolved.

Zhou 地官郎中 Fang Yingshu was appointed as 天官侍郎. The next day, as he was about to take office, at midnight, a kitchen worker named Wang Laoshi got up. Suddenly, he heard someone outside calling, "Wang Laoshi, don't get up; Fang Shi Lang is not taking office." Three days later, Li Shi Lang took office. Wang Laoshi went back to sleep until dawn; Fang Yingshu indeed fell ill, got up after several days, and then died. The officials reported this, and the emperor immediately appointed Li Hui Xiu as Shi Lang. On that day, he took office. Wang Laoshi asked others about this, and everyone said they didn't know; he then realized it was a message from the divine. In the Northern Qi dynasty, there was a monk named Chou, from Ye. When he was young, he shaved his head and became a novice, and there were many others of his age. Every time they had a break, they would often wrestle and jump around for fun, but the monk, being weak and frail, was repeatedly mocked and beaten. The monk was ashamed and went into the temple, closed the door, and held the foot of the statue of Vajrapani, vowing, "If I am weak and bullied by my peers, I would rather die than live in shame." He prayed with all his heart. For the first one or two nights, it was as he had hoped, but his resolve grew stronger. On the sixth day, just before dawn, the figure of Vajrapani appeared, holding a large bowl filled with tendons. Vajrapani said to Chou, "Do you want strength?" Chou replied, "Yes, I do. Do you mean that my resolve is strong enough?" Vajrapani said, "Yes, but can you eat tendons?" Chou replied, "No, I cannot." Vajrapani said, "Why not?" Chou replied, "Because as a monk, I abstain from eating meat." Vajrapani then took the bowl and a spoon, and forced the tendons into Chou's mouth. The monk was afraid and hesitated, but Vajrapani threatened him with a vajra, and Chou ate the tendons. Soon, he finished eating. Vajrapani said, "You now have great strength, but remember to uphold the teachings and strive diligently." The divine figure then disappeared, and just as dawn broke, Chou returned to his residence. His fellow monks asked, "What happened, young man?"

Why did Cao Cao not respond immediately? After a short while, food was prepared in the hall. When they finished eating, the other officials began to playfully mock the monk, saying, "I have great strength, but I fear I cannot match you." They then tried to test his arm, finding it to be extremely strong, almost superhuman. The person was surprised and questioned the monk, who replied, "I will test it for you." He then entered the hall and walked along the wall from west to east for several hundred steps, jumping up to the beams multiple times. He could lift a weight of a thousand jin, and his fists were powerful and strong. Those who had previously mocked him crouched and sweated, too afraid to look up. The monk later achieved enlightenment and lived in Lintu Mountain. His retreat was several miles deep into the mountain, with grand halls and temples. Many monks followed him, and there were often thousands of people practicing meditation. Emperor Wenxuan of Qi was angry about the large gathering and led several thousand elite soldiers to personally go and punish them, intending to execute the monk. On that day, the monk led the monks to meet the emperor at the valley entrance. The emperor asked, "Why are you so hasty to come here?" The monk replied, "Your Majesty intends to kill me, but I fear that the blood will stain the temple. Therefore, I am here to accept my punishment." The emperor was greatly surprised and descended from his carriage to pay respects, requesting that the monk be allowed to repent. The monk remained silent. The emperor then ordered a meal to be prepared and distributed.

The emperor asked, "I have heard that the master of the Diamond Buddha resides in a place where one prays for strength. Now I wish to see the master and offer a little effort, is that possible?" The monk replied, "In the past, strength was merely human strength. Now I will show the emperor divine power. Do you wish to see it?" The emperor said, "Please come with me to witness it." Before this, the monk had built a temple, and people from various places donated thousands of pieces of wood, which were piled up at the entrance of the valley. The monk chanted over them, and the wood rose into the air, striking each other with sounds like thunder, breaking and falling like rain. The emperor was greatly frightened, and his attendants scattered in panic. The emperor knelt down and begged the monk to stop the demonstration. He then ordered the monk to build the temple without hindrance. Later, in Pingzhou, when the monk was constructing a banner, he fell ill and, on his deathbed, lamented, "Life and death are part of human destiny, even the Buddha could not escape them. But my merit was incomplete, which is why I regret it. After my death, I wish to be reborn as a powerful elder to complete this work." With these words, he passed away. Thirty years later, Emperor Yang of Sui passed by Pingzhou and saw the temple. His heart was filled with a sense of familiarity, as if he remembered it from his past practice. He paid homage, guarded reverence, and did nothing but pay respect. He ordered the reconstruction of the temple in Pingzhou, and it was completed. At that time, people called him the "Great Power Elder."

The True Lao Kingdom is located 500 li south of the state of Huan. The customs of the people there include offering betel nuts, dragon brain incense, and powdered shellfish to guests as a form of hospitality. Their wine is considered to be of a similar nature to that consumed in private, inappropriate settings. They drink with their wives, but avoid doing so in the presence of elders. They also do not want others to see them during intimate moments. These customs are similar to those in China. The people of the True Lao Kingdom do not wear clothes, and those who do wear clothes are laughed at. They do not use salt or iron, and they use bamboo bows to hunt insects and birds. When the parents of the Five Streams people die, their bodies are covered for three years before burial. They perform drumming and singing rituals, and relatives gather for feasts and dances for more than a month. They use all their wealth to create coffins and carve niches in the cliffs of high mountains near rivers to bury the dead. The body is lowered from the mountain using ropes, and the higher the body is lowered, the more it is considered a sign of filial piety. After the initial mourning period, they do not perform further sacrifices. When they first encounter a death, they do not eat salt for three years. The Lingnan Liao people enjoy consuming a type of insect called "mì jié," which is a red, wriggling larva of a rat. They feed it honey and place it on the table. They move it slowly with a knife or fork and pick it up with chopsticks to eat it, making a small, continuous sound in the process. This is why it is called "mì jié."

During the Liang Dynasty, there was a monk who was extremely diligent in his practice. Emperor Wu of Liang highly respected and trusted him. Later, the emperor issued an edict to summon the monk. At the time, the emperor was playing Go with someone and wanted to kill a piece. The monk immediately replied, "Kill it." The messenger then quickly went out and executed the person.
