After the game, the emperor said, "Summon the monk." The messenger replied, "The emperor had ordered the execution of the person, and I have already carried it out." The emperor sighed and said, "What did the monk say before he died?" The messenger replied, "The monk said, 'I have no sin. In my previous life as a novice monk, I accidentally cut a segment of a worm with a hoe. Now, I am that worm, and this is my retribution.' " The emperor shed tears and felt deep regret, but it was too late. In the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang, during the year of Ganfeng, there was a large storage facility at Jingximing Temple in the capital. Duke Wuyouning of Jianchang set up a special office to extort wealth from the people outside the law. Ninety percent of the people were ruined by this extortion, and they cried out to heaven in their suffering. The facility was so large that it was 100 steps long and had over 200 rooms. All the confiscated goods were stored there. One day, a divine fire burned the facility, destroying everything in an instant. The people cursed Duke Wuyouning, and he soon suffered from swollen feet as thick as a jar. His suffering was bitter and painful, and he died after several months. In the year of Ganfeng, a monk named Tan Chang from Jingximing Temple in the capital traveled with a slave and two mules to listen to lectures from Master Ling of Qizhou. On the road, he met a man wearing a patched hat and a rough robe, holding prayer beads and calling himself a wise man who observed the Five Precepts. As dusk approached, they reached Mawei Inn and stayed there. The wise man prayed and chanted scriptures all night without stopping. Tan Chang thought he was very diligent and sat with him until the fourth watch of the night. They then left the inn together. After traveling about ten miles, the wise man suddenly pulled out two daggers from his sleeves and stabbed Tan Chang, killing him. The slave dismounted and ran into the grass to hide. The wise man rode the mule and drove the load away. The innkeeper did not wake up until morning and had a dream in which Tan Chang told him, "Last night, the wise man killed me." Soon after, the slave came to tell the innkeeper what had happened, just as in the dream. Three guardsmen, armed with bows and arrows, chased the wise man for over forty miles. When they caught up with him, they shot him, and he dismounted the mule and begged for mercy. They arrested him and took him to the county for execution.

During the late Northern Wei dynasty, in Songyang, Du Chang's wife, Suo Li, was extremely jealous. She had a maid named Jin Jing, and when Du Chang ordered her to comb her hair, Suo Li cut off her two fingers. Soon after, Suo Li was bitten by a fox and lost her fingers. She then had another maid named Yu Lian, who could sing beautifully. Du Chang admired her talent, but Suo Li, out of jealousy, cut off her tongue. Later, Suo Li developed a severe tongue ulcer and sought repentance from a monk named Cuo. The monk, who already knew about Suo Li's jealousy, told her, "Because of your jealousy, you have already lost fingers and cut off a tongue. If you continue, you will lose your tongue again. Only sincere repentance can save you." Suo Li prostrated herself in prayer and begged for mercy. After seven days, the monk made her open her mouth wide and chanted over her. Two snakes fell out of her mouth, and her tongue healed. From then on, she no longer acted out of jealousy. During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Tang dynasty, in Puyang, Fan Lüe's wife, Ren, was also extremely jealous. She had a maid whom she favored, but she cut off her ears and nose with a knife. Fan Lüe could not stop her. Soon after, Ren became pregnant and gave birth to a daughter who was born without ears and nose. As the daughter grew older, the maid was still around. When the daughter asked about the reason, the maid told her the whole story. The daughter cried bitterly and blamed her mother. Ren felt deep remorse and regretted her actions, but it was too late.

Hu Liang, the county magistrate of Huazhao in Guangzhou, followed General Zhou Rengui to campaign against the Lao and obtained a chief's concubine. Upon arriving at the county, Liang was not at the government office. His wife, He Shi, burned iron nails to scald her eyes. The concubine then hanged herself after her death. He Shi became pregnant and gave birth to a snake whose eyes were without pupils. She asked a Zen master about the matter. The master said, "Lady, you once burned iron to scald a woman's eyes. Because of your poisonous nature, this is the woman's revenge. She is the woman who was scalded. If you raise this snake, you can avoid difficulties; otherwise, disaster will befall you." He Shi raised the snake for one or two years, and it gradually grew larger. It did not see anything and remained only in her clothes and bedding. Liang did not know; when he pulled aside the bedding and saw the snake, he was greatly shocked and killed it with a knife. He Shi's eyes became dry, and she could no longer see anything. She regretted it but it was too late. Liang Renyu, a general, first had sexual relations with a maid. His wife, Li Shi, was very jealous and cruelly bound the maid, hitting her head. The maid cried out, saying, "I am lowly and cannot help it." She asked, "Why is the lock around my neck so painful?" After the maid died, about a month later, Li Shi fell ill and often saw the maid coming to call her. She developed four boils on her head, and her brain became ulcerated. She screamed day and night, suffering unbearable pain, and died after several months. Xia Rong, the deputy county magistrate of Zhiyang County in Jingzhou, favored his maid. When Zhang Jingxian, the county magistrate of Fangsi, went on a mission and was not around, his wife, Yang Shi, killed the maid and threw her into the latrine. When Zhang returned, he deceived him, saying, "The maid has escaped." Zhang thought his wife was harshly cruel and did not inquire further. The maid complained to Xia Rong, who investigated the matter. He asked Zhang, "Your wife is very ill and describes the situation." Zhang suspected there was something private and was angry. Xia Rong said, "Your wife wrongfully killed the maid and threw her into the latrine. Now I am investigating this matter. You should ask her about it." Zhang realized the truth and questioned his wife. She was very ill and confessed the whole matter. Xia Rong ordered the bones to be taken from the latrine, bathed in fragrant water, and given a proper burial. The maid did not want to be released and died after a month.

The Left Shangshu (Left Minister) Wei Anshi's daughter married the Taifu Zhubu (Imperial Treasury Clerk) Li Xun. Before they were married, Li Xun had a concubine. After they were married, he gave the concubine away to another man, who became her new master. The daughter contracted a contagious wasting disease, and Wei Anshi feared that the concubine might have been the cause. He ordered the County Magistrate of Henan, Qin Shouyi, to bring the concubine in for severe torture. Under the pressure of the torture, she eventually confessed to fabricating the illness. She was punished with more than three hundred strokes and threw herself into a well, dying within three days. Her daughter also died shortly after. People at the time believed that the daughter's death was caused by the spirit of the wronged concubine. Wei Anshi was demoted and later died in August of the first year of the Taiji era in the state of Pu. Wang Hong, a man from Hengshui in the state of Hebei, was known for his laziness and lack of moral principles. He often reported innocent people to the authorities, fabricating charges against them. He once traveled to the region of Hebei and met an old man who told him that every year, the old man would enter the village temple to pray. Wang Hong then falsely accused two hundred people of a crime and was appointed as a Yuye Jiangjun (Guerrilla General). Soon after, he was promoted to Shiyushi (Inspector of Judicial Affairs). At one point, he was sent to investigate the case of Wang Anren, the Duke of Shengzhou, who was accused of a crime. Wang Hong used a large wooden frame to bind and torture Wang Anren, but Wang Anren did not confess. In a fit of rage, Wang Hong struck Wang Anren to death with a weapon. Wang Anren's son, who was serving in the military, was also captured and executed. Later, Wang Hong traveled to Fen Prefecture and had a meal with the Military Governor Mao Gong. After the meal, Wang Hong ordered Mao Gong to be beheaded, taking his head as a trophy. The people were terrified by his actions. Eventually, Wang Hong was punished for his false accusations and was exiled to Leizhou. He had a concubine named Huayuan, who was his favorite. While on a boat, Wang Hong forged an imperial edict to summon Huayuan. Huayuan advised him, saying, "Given the current situation, how can you afford to act so recklessly?" Wang Hong became angry and said, "This old woman wants to ruin my plans!" He then bound her hands and feet and threw her into the river. The crew saved her, but Wang Hong whipped her two hundred times and buried her on the shore. Soon after, a false imperial edict was issued, and the Inspector of Judicial Affairs Hu Yuanli investigated the matter. Wang Hong was arrested and brought back to the place where Huayuan died. Suddenly, it was said that Huayuan appeared and called out to Wang Hong, but no one else could see her. Wang Hong cried out, "I am guilty! I deserve to die!" He heard the sound of a wooden frame and a stick, and he died in the middle of the night.

A man from Yuhang, Lu Yan, died in the summer but was revived after seeing a king who said his fate was not yet over. The attendants said his house was ruined and unsuitable. A man from Cangzhou named Li Tan had recently arrived and was destined to die. The king ordered that Li Tan's house be given to Lu Yan. Lu Yan then entered Li Tan's coffin and came back to life. He began speaking Wu dialect and did not recognize his wife and children. He told his story and went to Yuhang to find his family. His wife and children did not recognize him until he told them his story. During the reign of Empress Wu, the Wulong area of Fuzhou was plagued by frequent tiger attacks. There was a beast resembling a tiger but much larger. One day, at noon, it chased a tiger and killed it inside a house. The beast did not eat the tiger's meat, and after that, there were no more tigers in the county. The event was recorded and reported, and consulting a book of auspicious signs identified the beast as a "chui er" (a mythical creature that does not eat living things). If there were tiger attacks, it would kill them. During the reign of Empress Wu, the Prince Chengwang brought a tiger cub to the palace. The tiger later killed a palace woman. The Empress ordered that the tiger be starved for several days before it died. The Empress ordered the burial of the tiger, building a pagoda, setting up a thousand-person offering, and erecting a stele named the "Tiger Pagoda," which still stands today. Fu Huangzhong was the magistrate of Zhuji County in the state of Yue. A man from the department of Zhuji County drank heavily, fell asleep near a cliff, and was saved by a tiger. Suddenly, a tiger smelled the man, its whiskers entered the man's nose, causing him to sneeze, and the tiger jumped away, leading to the man falling off the cliff and being saved by a passerby. Yang Cheng lived in Xian County and was appointed as a Counselor. Zheng Gang lived in Wenxiang and was appointed as a Remonstrant. Li Zhou Nan lived in Qujiang and was appointed as a Proofreader. People believed that the further away one was, the higher the position, and the closer one was, the lower the position.

袁守一性格浅薄，行为急促，当时人们称他为“料鬭鳬翁”。他历任万年尉、雍州长史等职。窦怀贞每次都想鞭打他，于是趁中书令宗楚客不在时，在宗楚客门前送生菜，以此除掉他。窦怀贞不知情，只是拱手作揖说：“皇上要出宫了，你如此检校，做得不错。”袁守一立即弹劾他。一个多月后，窦怀贞被任命为左台御史大夫，袁守一请假不改，请求解除职务，窦怀贞呼唤并安慰他，袁守一却惶恐不安。宗楚客得知此事后，将袁守一调任右台侍御史，在朝堂上与窦怀贞抗衡，说：“与你罗师，罗师是市郭儿，与我无交涉。”不久，宗楚客因谋反而被诛杀，袁守一因他是党羽而被流放至端州。 黄门侍郎崔泰之哭悼特进李峤的诗曰：“台阁神仙地，衣冠君子乡。昨朝犹对坐，今日忽云亡。魂随司命去，鬼魄逐阎罗王。此时罢欢笑，无复向朝堂。” 尚书右丞陆余庆转任洛州长史，其子嘲讽他说：“陆余庆笔头无力，觜头硬。一朝受词诏，十日判不竟。送案褥下，余庆得而读之，曰：‘必是那狗。’遂鞭之。” 周定州刺史孙彦高被突厥围城数十重，不敢前往防御。需征发兵员时，他通过小窗接入文书，锁州宅门。等到贼人登垒，他才躲入柜中藏匿，吩咐奴仆说：“牢牢掌管钥匙，贼人来索要时，切勿给他。”昔日有愚人入京选皮袋，被贼盗去，那人说：“贼偷我袋，将终不得我物用。”有人问他原因，他答道：“钥匙尚在我衣带上，彼将何物开之？”此乃孙彦高之流也。

姜师度喜欢奇异的事物，担任沧州刺史兼监察官，建造抢车运输粮食，开凿河流和修建堤坝，州县因此沸腾。在鲁城边界内种植水稻，设置屯田，但稻穗被螃蟹吃光，又征调劳力捕捉螃蟹，百姓苦不堪言，歌谣唱道：“土地虽好，却只能种稻，全部被水沫覆盖，年年征调捉蟹的劳力，百姓无法生存。”后来他又担任陜州刺史，用永丰仓的粮食运输，另征三钱作为费用。一夜之间，他忽然想出一个计策，建造水塔，从仓库到河边建槽，长达数千丈，命令放米，不顺畅的地方用大耙推动，但米大多积攒成粉末，加上风吹扬起，每运一次损失一百石，动辄损失成千上万，派遣仓库管理员赔偿，家产全部耗尽，又让纳税人自行测量，有的赔偿数十斛，非常损害百姓，最终停止了这种做法。 岐王府参军石惠恭与监察御史李全交写诗说：“御史不是长期任职的职位，参军也不会久留，待你调任后，这个职位还会回到我这里。因为竞争发放文书，李全交为此判了十余纸以回报，实际上是借助拾遗张九龄的手。 御史中丞李谨度由宋璟推荐，母亲去世后，他不肯公开表达哀悼，讣告到来时都隐瞒了，官员们抱怨他无用，命令他的原籍瀛州申报李谨度母亲去世的消息，尚书省向御史台发出文书，然后他才哭泣，他的庸碌无能都是这样的。

Wang Yi was the corrupt official of the imperial court, Jiang Hui was the corrupt official of the Ministry of Personnel, and Cui Tai was the corrupt official of the inner court, and they were collectively known as the "Three Corruptions of the Capital." Yang Tao, when serving as a secretary in the imperial court, was urgently ordered to draft an imperial edict. The clerk holding the key to the treasury was elsewhere, and there was no old copy to retrieve. Yang Tao then carved through the window to retrieve the document, and people began to call him the "Window-Carving Secretary." A scholar or graduate from the Imperial Academy, Xin Hongzhi, wrote a poem: "You are the grass by the river, and in spring, your heart overflows. I am the mirror on the hall, and when I am reflected, I become clear." His fellow scholar, Chang D

During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, Qu Chongyu, a military officer from Ji Prefecture, composed a poem as he sent Siguang, his subordinate, to the capital. The poem began with Qu Chongyu expressing his fortune in encountering a clear and upright path. As Siguang departed, the wilderness echoed with sorrowful cries. Siguang, upon hearing the cries, asked who the great scholar was. The response was that the sound and rhythm were taught by a scholar from Wu. Siguang then remarked that the teacher was wise and the disciple was intelligent. In another incident, the magistrate of Lingchang, Liang Shifang, called for a document known as "bird feathers" (a metaphorical term), but the village headman failed to deliver it. The magistrate issued a judgment, questioning why the village headman did not bring the "bird feathers." The assistant remarked that the magistrate had made a good judgment, but the "bird feathers" were too numerous. The magistrate then requested a pen and wrote, "The magistrate called for 'bird feathers,' what are these 'bird feathers'? The village headman did not bring them." Knowledgeable people found this situation amusing. The *Chao Ye Qian Zai* (A Collection of Anecdotes from the Court and the People) is a book compiled by Zhang Zuo during the Tang Dynasty. In the second volume, there is a story about the powerful and feared Lai Junchen, a high-ranking official during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian. His power caused other officials to be wary of him. Hou Min, an official in charge of the imperial garden, flattered Lai Junchen, but his wife, Dong Shi, advised him to stop. She argued that Lai Junchen was a traitor and that his power would not last. She suggested that Hou Min should respect and distance himself from him. Gradually, Hou Min withdrew his flattery, which angered Lai Junchen. As a result, Lai Junchen demoted Hou Min to the position of magistrate in Wulong, Fuzhou. Hou Min wanted to resign, but Dong Shi advised him to leave quickly and not seek a new position. So, he left and presented his credentials to the local governor, who made a mistake in writing his name. Upon seeing an additional name at the end of Hou Min's credentials, the governor became furious and refused to accept him, causing Hou Min to feel distressed. Dong Shi advised him to stay, and after fifty days, a rebel force attacked Wulong, killing the previous county magistrate and taking the entire family captive. Because Hou Min did not calculate his actions, he was spared. Later, when Lai Junchen was executed and his followers exiled to Lingnan, Hou Min was also saved.

During the Tang Dynasty, Ji Mao, the Chief Prefect of Jizhou, wished to marry his son Xiu to the daughter of Cui Jing, the County Magistrate of Nangong. Cui Jing refused, but due to a pressing matter, he was coerced into agreeing. Cui Jing's wife, Zheng, initially unaware, embraced her daughter and wept, saying, "Our family has low status; we have never had a daughter marry into a family of such standing." She lay down resolutely, refusing to move. Their younger daughter, however, told her mother, "Father is in urgent need; I am willing to sacrifice myself to resolve his difficulties. Even if I were to become a servant, it would not be fitting to decline a family of noble lineage. How can the door of such a family be a source of shame? If you cannot agree, I will take it upon myself." With that, she climbed into the carriage and departed. Xiu later became a Chancellor, and his wife was known for her high moral character. People spoke highly of her. However, Xiu once clashed with Heiwei Wang Wu Yizong, a member of the royal family, and was subsequently exiled to serve as a Military Governor in Wenzhou, where he died. The Inspector of Censorship, Li She, had a mother who was known for her purity and integrity. Li She requested grain and rice to be delivered to their home. His mother sent someone to measure it and found that three stones remained. She asked for the reason, and the official replied, "Inspectors are not allowed to keep any surplus." Li She then asked about the cost of the carriage, and the official said, "Inspectors are not allowed to reimburse carriage fees." His mother became angry and ordered the surplus grain and the carriage fee to be returned to hold Li She accountable. Li She then pursued the granary official and punished him with a fine. All the other censors were filled with shame. Lu Xian, the Left Censor of Wenchang, had a second daughter who was first married to Zheng. Her husband died early, and she vowed never to remarry. She was beautiful and eloquent. Her brother-in-law, Li Sichong, a General of the Imperial Guard, proposed to her after her sister's death. He requested permission to remarry her, and Lu Xian's brothers did not dare to inform him. They prepared a grand ceremony with rich gifts and presented them to the Lu residence. However, Lu Xian's wife, Lu, refused to open the gate and shouted, "Old man, I am not your match!" She then climbed over the wall to her relatives' home and cut her hair. Li Sichong reported this to the emperor, who did not force her to change her mind. She later became a nun and practiced diligently.

A Woman of Chaste Resolve from Cangzhou The wife of Gao Denglian from Cangzhou, Lady Li, was married for less than a year when her husband passed away. At the age of eighteen, she remained steadfast in her resolve, setting up a spirit table to honor her late husband. She offered food three times a day and mourned at the shrine, wearing plain clothes and eating simple fare for six or seven years. One night, she dreamt of a man of remarkable appearance who wished to be her companion. Lady Li refused him in her sleep, and from then on, she dreamed of this man every night but still did not accept him. She believed him to be a malevolent spirit and tried to banish him with charms and spells, but he could not be dispelled. Lady Li sighed, "I have sworn not to change my fidelity, but I am being troubled by this. It is likely because my appearance has not yet faded." She then took a knife to cut her hair, wore a coarse hemp dress, did not wash her hair, allowed her hair to grow wild, and covered her face in dirt. The spirit thanked her, saying, "Madam, your integrity is as unyielding as bamboo and cypress; it cannot be taken away." From then on, she no longer dreamed of him. The local magistrate honored her door with a plaque, and to this day, there is still a place called "Chaste Woman's Lane." Yang Yingchuan's Niece, Lady Ronghua, was skilled in writing from a young age and once composed a new poem about a bride. Many people admired and passed it around. The poem reads: "The birds have ceased their startled sleep, The window opens with the dawn. Her hairpin is made of gold threads, Her mirror rests on a jade stand. She appears as if she emerged from a pool, People suspect she came from the moon. She laments that she will never be seen again, She wishes to leave but hesitates." Initially, when the Minister of War, Ren Huan, was granted two palace maidens of exceptional beauty by imperial decree, his wife became jealous, and the two maidens' hair fell out completely. Emperor Taizong heard of this and ordered an official to bring a golden flask of wine, saying, "Drink it and die immediately." As a third-rank official, Ren Huan was entitled to have concubines. The emperor said, "If she is not jealous, then there is no need to drink. If she is jealous, then drink it." Liu, Ren Huan's wife, bowed after receiving the decree and said, "I have been married to Ren Huan for a long time, and we both came from humble origins. We have supported each other and achieved honor and official positions. Now, Ren Huan has many favored concubines. It is truly better to die." She drank the wine and fell asleep. However, the wine was not poison, and she woke up at midnight. The emperor said to Ren Huan, "Her nature is like this; I too should fear her." He then ordered the two maidens to be moved to a separate residence.

During the Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty, Wei Gou of Jingzhao had a servant named Tao Fu, who, whenever he was about to go on a campaign, showed great courage and strength. When Wei Gou was appointed as Left Guard Langzhong, he allowed Tao Fu to become a free person. Tao Fu’s family had a yellow calf, which they presented to him. Because of this, Tao Fu asked Wei Gou to take his surname. Wei Gou said, “Just take my surname and be of the Wei family.” Tao Fu knelt and said, “I dare not share the same surname as your honorable master.” Wei Gou replied, “You may do so. This has a deep meaning, hence he is still called 'Yellow Calf.'” Wei was none other than the later Empress Wu Zetian. She did not allow others of different surnames, for she was concerned that in the distant future, descendants might marry into the Wei family; this was her intention. Empress Wu Zetian once dreamed of a parrot with very beautiful feathers, both wings broken. She asked the ministers about it, but they all remained silent. The Grand Secretary Di Renjie said, “The parrot is your surname, Your Majesty. The broken wings represent your two sons, the Prince of庐陵 and the Prince of Xiangwang. If Your Majesty restores these two sons, their wings will be whole again.” Wu Chengsi and Wu Sanshi had red necks, indicating their close favor with the empress. Later, the Khitan surrounded Youzhou and sent a proclamation to the court saying, “Return our Prince of Xiangwang!” Then Wu Zetian recalled Di Renjie’s words and said, “You once interpreted a dream for me, and now it has come true. I wish to name a crown prince; who is the most suitable?” Di Renjie replied, “Your Majesty has a virtuous son, and also has a virtuous nephew. The choice between them should be made by Your Majesty’s own judgment.” Wu Zetian said, “I have my own virtuous son. Chengsi and Sanshi are merely skin diseases.” Wu Chengsi and others, fearing this, covered their ears and ran away. She then issued an edict to summon the Prince of庐陵 and make him crown prince, appointing him as commander-in-chief. At first, when recruiting soldiers, no one responded. But when word spread that the crown prince was coming, the northern hills were filled with soldiers, no room for people. The enemy withdrew on their own.

薛季昶担任荆州长史时，梦见一只猫儿伏卧在堂限上，头朝外。他去问占卜者张猷，张猷说：“猫儿是爪牙，伏在门限上，这是阃外之事，您必定知道军马的重要性。”不到十天，薛季昶就被任命为桂州都督、岭南招讨使。 给事中陈安平的儿子年满后参加科举考试，与同乡李仙药一起睡觉时梦见十一月养。李仙药解释说：“十一月养是冬丝，您必定会被送到东司。”几天后，陈安平的儿子果然被送到吏部。 饶阳的李瞿云勋官刚刚完成科举考试，夜晚梦见一只极大的母猪。李仙药解释说：“母猪是防主，您必定会得到屯主的职位。”几天后，李瞿云果然如其所言得到了屯主的职位。 张鷟曾经梦见一只紫色、五彩成文的大鸟飞到庭前，没有离去。他告诉祖父说：“这是吉祥的征兆。”从前蔡衡说过：“凤凰有五种，红色的是文章凤，青色的是鸾，黄色的是鹓雏，白色的是鸿鹄，紫色的是鸑鷟。”这只鸟在凤凰之左，您应当成为帝王的辅佐。于是张鷟以此为自己命名。张鷟初次参加科举考试到达怀州时，梦见祥云覆盖了他的身体。那一年，他在对防考功员外骞味道的考试中被认为是最好的。后来，他最初担任岐王的属官时，梦见自己穿着红色的衣服骑着驴子，睡梦中自问：“我应该穿着绿色的衣服骑马，为什么穿着红色的衣服却骑着驴子？”那一年，他参加科举考试及第，被授予鸿胪丞的职位，未经考试就被授予五品官职，这正是他的应验。

Pei Yuanzhi from Hedong first took the imperial examination for the jinshi degree. The next morning, when the results were announced, he dreamed of a dog coming out from a hole and pulling a bow to shoot at it. His arrow struck the dog, which he considered an ominous sign. He asked Cao Liangshi, a skilled dream interpreter, about the dream. Cao said, "The dog represents the character '第' (di), the head of the character. The bow represents the body of the character. The arrow represents the vertical stroke of the character. The striking of the dog means '第' (di), which is your rank." Soon after, when the results were announced, he indeed received the rank he had dreamed of. Right Chancellor Lu Zangong and Grand Secretary Cui Shi, who were part of the Tai Ping faction, were exiled to the south. They reached Jingzhou, where Cui had a dream of sitting and listening to a lecture while looking in a mirror. He asked Zhang You, a skilled dream interpreter, about the dream. Zhang said, "This is a very bad dream. Listening to the lecture from above and looking in the mirror suggests that your end is near. The character '镜' (jing) breaks down into '金' (jin) and '竟' (jing), which means the end. Is your end today?" Soon after, a censor named Lu Yiman brought an imperial decree ordering Cui to commit suicide. Du Xuan from Luo Prefecture had a cow that he deeply loved. One night, he dreamed that his cow had two tails. He asked Li Xianyao, a skilled dream interpreter, about the dream. Li said, "The character '牛' (niu) has two tails, which means '失' (shi), the character for loss." After several months, the cow was indeed lost. During the early years of the reign, Lai Junchen framed and accused the sons of a commoner named Xian. At night, he sent a witch to pray to the stars and the moon and curse the sons. They were subjected to severe physical punishment, and their servants falsely testified against them, leading the sons to falsely confess and be beaten to death. The people were deeply saddened. Zhang Zuo, a famous writer and official, criticized the situation, saying that ordinary people often believed in superstitious practices, and children and women placed great importance on talismans and spells. He condemned the practice of harboring evil, promoting deceit, and using blood and sincerity to manipulate reality, which was a method used by Jiang Zhong to frame people. During the height of Empress Wei's power, she engaged in superstitious practices and used mirrors to curse people, making them quickly fall into chaos. She also involved a woman named A Lai from Chongrenfang, who specialized in superstitious practices. After the death of King Ping, evidence of witchcraft was often found in the palace, all done by those who opposed Empress Wei.

Empress Wei buried her father, Wei Zhen, who was posthumously known as Prince Feng. After the burial, palace maids encountered a ghost teacher named Yong Wen Zhi, who falsely proclaimed the orders of Prince Feng, saying, "Those who have been palace maids have endured great hardship; they should be rewarded by changing their green clothing to red." Empress Wei mourned deeply and wished to follow the ghost's instruction, but before she could make the arrangements, someone reported that Yong Wen Zhi had falsely accepted bribes. Upon investigation, he was executed. During the reign of Emperor Zhongzong, there was a ghost teacher named Peng Junqing who was humiliated by a censor. On another day, in front of all officials gathered, Peng Junqing falsely proclaimed the edict of Emperor Xuanhe, saying, "The censor did not check and remove his hat and sash; let him go." He then said, "There is an edict to give him one beating." The chief magistrate said, "The censor did not follow the proper edict; it is not appropriate to beat him." Peng Junqing replied, "If there is no proper edict, then let the censor put his hat back on and dance, bow, and leave." The spectators were amazed. Floating Leisure Man, Zhang Yue, was the magistrate of Pingchang in Dezhou. During a severe drought, the prefect issued an order to summon mediums and monks for rituals. After more than twenty days without effect, Zhang Yue pushed a clay dragon down, and that night it rained heavily. In Jiangnan, where ghosts were prevalent, the superstitious locals would worship them when sick, as there were no doctors. Zhang Yue once stayed in Hongzhou for several days and heard of a local woman named He Po, who was skilled in playing the pipa and divination. Deputies Judge Guo and his companions sought her out. Her door was crowded with people, and gifts and offerings filled the path. Her appearance was full of joy, and her spirit was unusually high. Guo bowed twice and placed money, asking about her rank. He Po tuned the strings and harmonized the sound and breath, saying, "This man will be wealthy and honored this year, achieving the first rank; next year, he will achieve the second rank; the following year, he will achieve the third rank; even further, he will achieve the fourth rank." Guo said, "Auntie, you're wrong. Those with fewer ranks are higher in rank, and those with more ranks are lower." He Po replied, "This year, he will lose one rank; next year, he will lose two ranks; the following year, he will lose three ranks; even further, he will lose four ranks; after five or six years, he will have no rank at all." Guo cursed and stood up.

In the city of Chongren, there was a woman named Fang Ailai, who was known for her skill in divination using a lute. Her house was often bustling with people, and the scholar Zhang Zhuo of Fuxiu once visited to observe her. He saw a general dressed in a purple robe and jade belt, a very impressive sight. The general placed a piece of fine silk and asked for a session of divination. The old woman plucked the strings of her lute, burned incense, and closed her eyes while singing. She revealed that the east was connected to Dongfang Shuo, the west to Xifang Shuo, the south to Nifang Shuo, the north to Beifang Shuo, the upper realm to Shangfang Shuo, and the lower realm to Xiafang Shuo. The general bowed respectfully. After the divination, many requests were made, and it was necessary to examine the results carefully to resolve doubts. The old woman then arranged the outcomes at will. During the year 670 AD, in Zhaozhou, there was a man named Zu Zhenjian who possessed magical abilities. He could hang a water jar on a beam and cut it with a knife without it breaking or falling. In an empty room, he would close the door secretly and place a jar of water with a knife on top. After a long time, someone would enter to see, and they would find Zu Zhenjian dismembered into five pieces, with the water jar filled with blood. However, everything returned to normal after the person left. In the coldest month of winter, the water in a stone mortar would freeze. Zu Zhenjian would curse it to unfreeze. He sold divination in the city of Xindu, making a hundred coins a day, using the method of Junping. Later, he was accused by someone and led to the market to be executed. Despite this, he remained calm and composed, completely without fear. Before his execution, he wrote a poem with paper and pen, his spirit unyielding.

Lingkong Guanye, a Taoist priest, used a spell and a knife to cut through a patient's abdomen, severing peach and willow branches placed on the belly without causing internal damage. Later, he used two knives to cut a woman in half, causing her to split into two parts with blood flowing everywhere, and then magically reassembled her. In Henan Province, the Lide Lane and the West Lane of the Southern Market both have temples dedicated to foreign gods. Each year, foreign merchants worship these gods by sacrificing pigs and sheep, playing music, singing, and dancing, and then offering money to a chosen person (the "祅主"). The "祅主" takes a sharp knife, as cold as frost and snow, and cuts through their body, causing their intestines to spill out. After a short while, they sprinkle water and recite a spell, and the wounds heal instantly, just as they were before. This is a magical practice from the Western Regions. At the temple of the foreign god in Liangzhou, on the day of prayer, the "祅主" is nailed through the forehead with an iron nail, which passes through their armpit. They then leave the temple, flying like a bird, and travel hundreds of miles to the temple of the Western god, where they perform a dance. Afterward, they return to the old temple, and the nail causes no further harm. They lie down for over ten days and heal completely, but no one knows how this is possible. Ming Chongyan, a magician, was tested by the emperor, who ordered him to stop a sound in a dungeon by writing two peach-wood charms. The sound ceased immediately, and the emperor laughed and asked the musicians if they had seen two dragon heads opening their mouths upward. The musicians were terrified and dared not play their instruments. The emperor was very pleased.

The wife of Liu Jing, the magistrate of Luo County, fell ill, and the official Ming Chongyan, who was known for his medical skills, diagnosed her and said that she would recover if she ate fresh dragon liver. Liu Jing thought it was impossible to obtain. Ming Chongyan then drew a talisman and sent it with the wind into the sky. Soon a dragon descended into the water in a jar. The liver was extracted and eaten, and the illness was cured. During the hot summer, the emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits. Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits. They arrived and were eaten, and there was no difference. In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, but the emperor thought about them. Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins to leave. Soon he obtained a large fruit, saying it was from the garden of an old man in Gou City. The emperor went to the garden and caught up with the old man. He asked him, "How did you get this fruit?" The old man replied, "I buried a fruit in the ground, intending to present it, but I just sold it for a hundred coins." Ming Chongyan was sitting alone in the hall at night when he was stabbed to death. The knife was still in his heart. The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, but there was no trace of him. Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost due to exhaustion. Confucius said, "Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a harm." Indeed. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, there was a Daoist named Ding Shi from Bo Ye in Ying Prefecture. He had extraordinary conduct. Princess Taiping introduced him to Empress Wu Zetian, who tested him by having him drink three dou of wine from a silver jar in one go. When asked if he could eat soy sauce, he agreed and finished a dou of soy sauce from a silver jar with a spoon in a short time. Empress Wu Zetian wanted to appoint him to an official position, but Ding Shi preferred to become a monk and asked to be shaved. After Empress Wu Zetian regained power, Ding Shi said that if one could practice the Way, why bother with shaving? He grew his hair long. He was able to withstand one hundred lashes from a whip without any injury, and people could not understand how he did it.

During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong (701-710 CE), there was a man named Li Cide who claimed to be an expert in performing talismans and spells. He said he could suppress the will of Heaven and, within the palace, arrange beans to form an army and draw rivers on the ground. He also carved bamboo into spears and wrapped himself in armor. At the third watch of the night, he caused chaos among the palace attendants, leading to the deaths of several dozen of them. The General of the Feathered Forest, Yang Xuanji, heard the commotion inside the palace and led his troops to break in, killing Li Cide and several dozen eunuchs. Alas, Li Cide met his end through his own sorcery. Emperor Xuanzong then ordered the Buddhist monks and Taoist priests within the palace to demonstrate their abilities, but the contest dragged on without a clear resolution. Master Ye Fashan of the Xuan Dao Temple took two liters of walnuts, including their shells, and ate them all. Despite this, the monks still did not concede. Master Ye then burned an iron basin, causing his hands to glow red, as if he were about to strike the head of an elderly monk. The monk cried out, covered his head with his robe, and fled. Emperor Xuanzong clapped his hands and laughed heartily. The Taoist priest Luo Gongyuan, when he was young, was not particularly bright. He spent several years in Liang Mountain, where he suddenly gained extraordinary insight and foresight. He accurately predicted many events, and the emperor summoned him to the capital. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the crown prince held a ritual feast. Luo Gongyuan asked the prince for some silver and gold vessels, but the prince refused, insisting that he would give them to him later. Luo then said he would take them himself in a short while. The prince sealed the door of his room, but shortly after, when he opened it, he found no trace of the vessels. However, in the previously sealed eastern room, the vessels were found inside. Luo then asked to borrow the prince’s horse, but the prince became angry and refused. Luo said he had already taken it and would return it later. The prince rushed to the stables to check, and the horse was still there as if nothing had happened. A court official named Wei Shouyi, carrying several dishes, approached Luo Gongyuan to see what was happening. Meanwhile, a servant carrying Luo’s clothes and belongings was outside, but soon after, the clothes were found in Luo’s chest. Everyone was greatly surprised and could not understand how this had happened.

Ouyang Tong's son was skilled in calligraphy, but he was more slender and timid than his father. He often boasted of his ability to write, insisting on using ivory and rhinoceros horn for pen tubes, cat hair as the core, and autumn rabbit hair as the outer layer. He used pine soot as ink, adding musk, and required the paper to be firm, thin, white, and smooth before writing. This shows his high regard for his own writing. Xue Chuntuo also imitated Ouyang's cursive script, but it was too heavy and blunt, making it inferior to Ouyang's. Meng Zjian was from Pingzhou. When he was young, he fell ill and suddenly died, seeing the official palace as he had in life, unaware of his death. He met an old friend who was an official and asked, "Why did you come here?" Meng reported his experience, and the official realized that a boundary official was checking. The official asked, "You have not done any good deeds in life, so how did you return?" Meng replied, "I have recited the Heart Sutra and the High King Sutra many times, though I can't count them, perhaps thirty to forty thousand times. They were rechecked, and he returned." The official then asked if he wanted to know his position, saying it was very important, and showed him a register, saying, "Meng Zjian is destined to be a magistrate in Caozhou, then transfer to a clerk in Dengzhou." He immediately covered it and did not allow him to see it, so he went to a desolate thicket and entered a black pit, and then he lived. He did not know what his destiny was. Soon there was an imperial decree to recruit for transporting grain, and he was appointed as a magistrate in Caozhou. He realized, "This state I did not see a small book," meaning he recognized the state mentioned in the register. He was fully appointed as a clerk in Dengzhou, and after leaving his post, he was selected and appointed as a judge in Jinzhou, but he died before he could take up the position. During the reign of Emperor Taizong, in Duntou County, there was a virtuous person who picked a tree seed as large as a finger while gathering vegetables on the bank of the Yellow River. He took it home and planted it. After three years, it bore five fruits, which tasted like pears and apples, with a lot of juice and an exceptionally sour and delicious flavor. He sent them to the county, which then sent them to the state. The state, impressed by the flavor, presented them to the emperor, who rewarded him with ten bolts of silk. Later, the tree grew and bore up to three hundred fruits each year, and they were presented to the emperor annually, known as Zhu Nai. Today, the trees still exist in Debei and other states, where people graft their branches, making the fruit abundant. People believe the tree came from the Western Regions and was brought across the river.

At the end of the Western Jin dynasty, there was a magistrate named Xu Xun in Jingyang County who attained enlightenment in the western mountains of Yuzhang. There was a dragon in the river that caused trouble, and Xu Xun submerged underwater and cut it with a sword. Afterward, his whereabouts were unknown. Soon, a fisherman caught a stone that made a loud sound, and the sound could be heard for dozens of li. In the Tang dynasty, the Prince of Zhao, as the governor of Hongzhou, broke the stone open and found a pair of swords. Upon examining their inscriptions, one had the words "Xu Jingyang," and the other had the words "Wan Zhen." Thus, there emerged a teacher named Wan Zhen. In the middle of the Shangyuan era, officials of the ninth rank and above were ordered to wear a bag shaped like a fish made of silk and tied with a ribbon. This was taken as a symbol of strength, a sign of good fortune. This practice ceased during the reign of Empress Wu but was revived after the Jingyun era, when they resumed the practice of tying a white fish-shaped ribbon. Emperor Zhongzong ordered the creation of a large mirror in Yangzhou, casting copper into a tree with golden flowers and silver leaves. The emperor would ride his horse and look at himself in the mirror, and both he and his horse would appear in the mirror. The official responsible for this was the magistrate of Gaoyou County. In the second year of the Tianxian era of Emperor Ruizong, on the 15th and 16th nights of the first month, in the city of Chang'an, outside the Anfu Gate, a lantern wheel was made, twenty zhang high, covered with brocade and decorated with gold and jade, and lit with fifty thousand lamps, clustered together like a flower tree. Thousands of palace women, dressed in silk and brocade, adorned with pearls and jade, and powdered with fragrant powder, danced and sang under the lantern wheel for three days and nights. The joy was unprecedented.

Zhang Yizhi built a seven-treasure tent for his mother Ao Zang, adorned with silver, gold, pearls, jade, and other precious items, all of which were unparalleled in history. The tent featured an ivory bed, a mat woven from rhinoceros horn, a fur rug, a felt mat, dragon whiskers from Fen and Jin, and phoenix feathers from the middle river, all used as cushions. The Vice Minister of the Secretariat, Li Jiaoxiu, was pressured by Zhang Yizhi to drink together from a pair of鸳 cups, symbolizing their close relationship. However, Li Jiaoxiu, fearing Zhang Yizhi's power and disliking his age, drank excessively and became frequently drunk. He was often unresponsive even when called, and was eventually appointed as the governor of Hengzhou. After Zhang Yizhi's downfall, Ao Zang was taken into custody, and Li Jiaoxiu was demoted to the position of governor of Hengzhou. Zong Chuke built a new house, all of which was constructed with cypress beams, walls plastered with sandalwood and red powder, and the opening of the door emitted a pervasive fragrance. The steps and ground were made of polished stone, and those who walked on it would fall over. Zong Chuke was exposed by Jianchang Prince for embezzling more than ten thousand strings of coins, and his brothers were exiled. Princess Taiping visited his house and sighed, saying, "Look at how he lived and sat. We have been living in vain and dying in waste." One year later, he was promoted to Vice Minister of the Secretariat and later became the Chief Secretary. He was executed after the downfall of the Wei clan. In the Zhaosheng Temple in Luoyang, Princess Anle built a hundred-treasure incense burner, three feet high, with four doors, a vermilion bridge, railings, flowers, birds, beasts, celestial musicians, and other intricate carvings. It featured pearls, jade, coral, amber, glass, and all kinds of precious items, costing three million coins, using up all the treasures in the treasury.

Emperor Yang of Sui, during his northern inspection tour, constructed a grand pavilion called the Great Hall with seven precious canopies, spacious enough to accommodate hundreds of people, adorned with precious jewels, and shining brightly. He invited the Hun leader Qimin Khan to a banquet within the pavilion. The Khan was amazed and wondered if such a place existed in the world. Some people commented that the Great Hall might be an omen of misfortune, comparing it to Wang Mang's light carriages, which were seen as signs of divine displeasure. They questioned whether this reflected divine will concerning human affairs. Princess Anle was demoted to a commoner for her rebellious actions and seized the estates of the people to build the Ding Kun Pool, which extended 49 miles to the south, resembling the Kunming Pool. She piled stones to form mountains resembling Mount Hua, carved water channels to resemble the Tianjin River, and constructed pavilions and bridges adorned with brocade, painted with vermilion and cinnabar, and decorated with gold, silver, jade, and pearls. She also created a nine-bend wine cup pool with a stone lotus platform from which泉水涌出, showcasing the most magnificent sights in the world. The downfall of the rebellious person resulted in their punishment by being sent to the Ministry of Agriculture. Every day, men and women came to visit, causing traffic congestion. Those who received imperial orders to attend were officials who had to resign from their positions, while ordinary people were sentenced to one hundred strokes of the cane. Princess Anle made a dress adorned with bird feathers, followed by officials and commoners imitating her, leading to the hunting of rare birds and animals in the mountains and valleys until they were nearly extinct. During the Kaiyuan period, Emperor Xuanzong banned the use of precious items in the palace and prohibited people from wearing jewelry and fine silk, thus stopping the excessive hunting. During Emperor Gaozong's reign, Liu Longzi spread false prophecies, using a gold dragon head hidden in his sleeve with a bag of blood water, claiming that drinking the water would cure all diseases. People believed him, and he eventually gathered enough support to plot rebellion. After the plot was discovered, he fled but was eventually captured, executed, and his accomplices were also punished.

The Filial Son De Guo from the East Sea Region De Guo, a filial son from the East Sea region, cried for his deceased mother, and a large gathering of birds would appear. The event was verified, and he was honored with a sign at his gate. Later, it was discovered that he scattered food on the ground when he cried, attracting the birds. The birds heard the crying sound and came, thinking it was a signal; they were not truly intelligent. The Filial Son Wang Sui from Hedong Region Wang Sui, a filial son from the Hedong region, had a cat and a dog that nursed each other's young. The local authorities reported this, and he was honored. It was discovered that they were born at the same time and were placed in each other's nests, leading to this behavior being considered normal. From Paired Trees to Happy Vines, Wheat Branching, and Grain with the Same Ear Various natural phenomena, such as paired trees, happy vines, wheat branching, and grain with the same ear, are common and numerous. They are all human-made and not worth being surprised about. Tong Tai Finds Stones with Inscriptions in the Luo River Tong Tai found a white stone with purple writing in the Luo River. The inscription read, "The divine mother by the water, forever prospering the imperial cause." The stone was presented, and a county was established. It was later discovered that the stone was carved with fake writing. Another stone with similar writing was found in Wenshui County, and these stones were found to be fraudulent and were no longer used. Hu Yanqing Presents a Turtle with an Inscription to the Emperor Hu Yanqing from Xiangzhou found a turtle with an inscription on its belly. The inscription read, "May the emperor live ten thousand years." The turtle was presented to the emperor, and the high-ranking official Li Zhaode used a knife to scrape off the writing. He reported to the emperor, who said, "This is not a malicious act. Let it go and do not inquire further."

Empress Wu loved auspicious signs, and the court historian Zhu Qianyi reported a dream to her. In the dream, Empress Wu's hair turned white and then black again, and her teeth fell out and grew back. She was immediately appointed as the Chief Justice of the Criminal Court. There were more than three hundred prisoners in the prison. After the autumn equinox, there was nothing to do, so they made traces of a sage at the corner of the prison wall, five feet long. At midnight, all three hundred prisoners suddenly cried out. The eunuchs investigated and asked, "Last night, a sage appeared, three zhang tall, with a golden face. He said, 'You all are wronged, do not be afraid. The emperor will live for a long time and will grant amnesty to you.'" When they lit a fire to look, they saw a large trace, and a general amnesty was granted, changing the year to Dazhu. Bai Tieyu was a captive Turk from Yan Prefecture who used false doctrines to deceive the people. He first buried a gold and bronze statue under a pine tree in a deep mountain. After several years, grass grew on top of it. He told the villagers that last night he passed by the mountain and saw the Buddha's light. He set up a grand feast and chose a lucky day to reveal the holy Buddha. On the appointed day, he gathered hundreds of people and ordered them to dig at the wrong place, but they could not find it. Then he encouraged them, saying that if they did not sincerely offer alms, the Buddha would not appear. As a result, men and women competed to offer alms, totaling more than a million. They dug at the burial site and found the gold and bronze statue. The villagers spread the news far and wide, but no one could see the saint. He then declared that anyone who saw the saint would be cured of all illnesses. Within several hundred miles, old and young, men and women, all flocked to him. He then placed the statue in a bag made of red, purple, red, and yellow silk, with dozens of layers. People gathered to watch, and for each layer removed, they offered alms, totaling several thousand times, and then they saw the statue. In this way, he was deceitful for one or two years. The villagers submitted, and he rebelled, calling himself Guang Wang, appointing officials, and killing local officials, causing problems for several years. The general Cheng Wuteng was ordered to kill him.

Ruan Qingyuan, the Middle Court Attendant, was deceitful and treacherous. Initially serving the crown prince, he often had access to the palace and was frequently allowed to enter and exit. When temporarily ordered to leave, he immediately relied on his authority. Ministers and below all considered him a key person. When ministers were just eating, he would arrive, and the others would order him to sit. They would often send someone to urgently call him, saying the prince needed him to command the servants to spit out their food and then leave. All departments treated him similarly, soliciting favors, selling offices, and conducting illegal transactions, and whatever he sought was granted. Later, the crown prince gradually distanced himself from him. He still secretly entered the palace to eat the food of the servants, leaving in the evening with an excuse of stomach pain, claiming the prince had given him too much fruit, and soon after, he expelled the soldiers, the rice was yellow and smelly, and the vegetables were scattered. This is the usual state of affairs for those who gain favor through deceit. Spring Official and Shangshu Yin Zhiwei and Deputy Guest Censor Tian Guaidao accompanied him. When they arrived at the tent, Yin Zhiwei danced and twisted, embracing the boots of Moche and smelling them. Tian Guaidao bowed deeply but did not kowtow. Moche became extremely angry and had him suspended upside down for an entire night. The next day, he intended to kill Yuan Zhen, who advised against killing the envoy of a great country, considering it inauspicious, so he released him. Later, they argued in the palace, saying Moche would not agree to peace, but Yin Zhiwei insisted that he would, and indeed, Moche rebelled, capturing Zhao and Ding. The empress regnant then executed Yin Zhiwei's entire family and promoted Tian Guaidao to the position of Shangshu in the Department of Works.

Zhang Li She, a man prone to forgetfulness, was appointed as a military officer in Huai Zhou. Whenever he was invited to gatherings, he would write down the names on a tablet. At one point, the magistrate of He-nei, Geng Ren Hui, invited him, but was surprised when he did not show up. Geng personally went to the door and asked Zhang to look at the tablet, saying, "Why did you not come? There is no name on the tablet." At another time, Zhang had a daytime nap and was startled to find himself in the state capital, where he knocked on the governor Deng Yun's door to apologize, saying, "I heard that you wanted to punish me with death." Deng Yun replied, "There is no such thing." Zhang said, "The military officer Mr. Ji said so." Deng Yun became very angry and summoned the official Ji to ask about it. He was about to whip Ji, but Ji vehemently protested, saying he had never said such a thing. Zhang then pleaded, "I hope you will forgive him. I am afraid it was just something I saw in a dream." People at the time knew of his confused and forgetful nature. Yan Xuan Yi, the magistrate of Wu Yuan County, was also forgetful. Once, he went to the state capital and sat in the host's house. When a state official passed by, he mistook him for a county official and called for him to be whipped. The official said, "I am a state official." Yan felt ashamed and stopped. Shortly after, the county official arrived, and Yan suspected he was a state official and held his hand, inviting him to sit. The official said, "I am a county official." Yan again felt ashamed and stopped. Once, there was a messenger who delivered a letter from his brother, stopping at the steps. Soon, a village official reported that a recorder had arrived. Yan took a whip and whipped the messenger several times. The messenger did not understand why and was questioned. Yan said, "I made a big mistake. I asked the recorder to return home and fetch a cup of wine to warm and treat. After a long while, the recorder brought the wine. Yan forgot that he had asked for the wine and also forgot that the messenger had been whipped. He then gave the recorder wine to drink."

Deputy County Magistrate Guo Wu Jing of Nanpi, Cangzhou, first reported to the governor Wang Qing, who asked, "What is your surname?" Guo replied, "Wang." Soon after, Wang Qing returned and asked again, "What is your surname?" Guo again replied, "Wang." Wang Qing was greatly surprised and bewildered for a long time, looking up at Guo and saying, "All the assistants and clerks in Nanpi are surnamed Wang." In Dingzhou, a wealthy man named He Yuan managed three official stations. He always stayed and conducted business near the stations, devoting himself to trade with the nomadic tribes. He was immensely wealthy, owning five hundred silk looms. As he grew older, he became bewildered and did not join the military, leading to his family's financial ruin. However, when he returned to his former business, his family's fortunes gradually recovered, though not by his will. Such is the nature of things. In Chang'an, a wealthy man named Luo Hui made his living by cleaning latrines. The people in the neighborhood called him "Chicken Shop," saying that he gained something from his work, just as chickens gain from their feed. Luo Hui's family had immense wealth, and he was visited by a scholar named Lu Jingyang. Luo invited Lu to his residence, which was very beautiful. Inside, the scholar found the house well-furnished, with fresh clothes and luxurious bedding. Lu asked, "How can you be so content if you continue this occupation?" Luo replied, "I suspended my business for a couple of years, and my servants and animals died or were lost. Since I resumed my business, my family's plans have gradually been achieved, though not by my will. Such is the nature of things." Princes Teng Ying and Jiang Hui were both unable to be upright and cautious. The emperor bestowed names on the princes, and the five princes did not match the two princes. The emperor decreed, "Teng and Jiang, you manage your financial affairs yourselves and do not need bestowed goods. Give them as strings of coins." The two princes were deeply ashamed, and officials in the court were all self-motivated. They all considered accepting bribes as corruption, which would be a burden for life, and no one dared to commit such acts.

Ying Prefecture's Rao County magistrate Dou Zhifan was corrupt. When one village head died, Dou ordered a villager to create an image for him, each contributing one string of coins. Dou took the money for himself and said, "If the village head has committed a crime, we must rescue him urgently." Dou first created an image and gave it to the villager, settling the money at two thousand and five hundred strings, half of which he kept for himself. His greed was similar to this. Dou had only one son. When Dou was releasing hawks, his horse startled a mulberry branch, breaking someone's head. The people were pleased and said, "A thousand gold pieces can easily exchange for a rabbit's life." In Yi Prefecture's Xinchang County, the newly appointed magistrate Xiahou Biao asked a village head, "How many eggs can be bought for one string of coins?" The village head replied, "Three." Xiahou then sent someone to fetch ten thousand strings of coins to buy thirty thousand eggs, telling the village head, "We don't need them yet; just have the hens incubate them." After a few months, they grew into thirty thousand chickens. Xiahou ordered the county officials to sell them for thirty coins each, earning thirty thousand strings of coins in half a year. He then asked, "How many bamboo shoots can be bought for one string of coins?" The village head replied, "Five." Xiahou then took ten thousand strings of coins and bought fifty thousand shoots, telling the village head, "I don't need them yet; just plant them in the forest." By autumn, the bamboo grew, and each shoot was worth ten coins, totaling fifty thousand strings of coins. His greed and lack of morality were similar to this. In Bian Prefecture, the governor Wang Zhi'an had a refined diet. When he served guests, he served plain rice. A merchant had a horse that could travel three hundred miles a day and had not been sold for thirty years. The market valued it at fourteen thousand strings of coins. Wang said, "Four thousand is too little; increase it by a thousand more." He then ordered the merchant to buy a piece of fine silk for three thousand strings of coins. Wang asked, "How many taels of silk are needed?" The merchant replied, "Five." Wang ordered a servant to fetch five taels of silk, giving ten coins for each tael as payment for manual labor.

Governor Duan Chongjian of Shen Prefecture was greedy and violent. Upon taking office, he ordered the village head to conduct a census of guests, saying no one could claim to be without an upper household. He would take two from the upper households and one from the lower households, using threats of punishment to coerce them. Fearing punishment, people falsely reported their status. After completing the census, he said he did not need to call the guests; he only needed to see the hosts. The hosts would be everywhere distributing the burden. Each guest was required to contribute one bolt of silk, amounting to thirty carts within a month. Upon leaving office, he was sent to Lucheng County, where one cart of silk was not fully loaded, short by six hundred bolts. He immediately called the village head to make it up. The village head had nothing to offer, so he doubled the amount from the county magistrate, deputy magistrate, and military commander, sending it to the capital. He was then appointed governor of Liuzhou. Feng Xianxin, the military governor of Annan, ordered his son-in-law Pei Weiye to temporarily serve as governor of the state. Pei was greedy and violent, seizing gold, silver, and property worth tens of thousands of strings of cash. A leader brought Pei Lang, his wife, and demanded one thousand bolts of silk for a bridal procession. He obtained eight hundred bolts but still refused to let go, taking the new bride back and playing with her for three days before releasing her, and the leader never paid again. Pei then took the goods to Yangzhou and Annan, and when questioned, he surrendered the seized items to the authorities. Pei was also shackled and sent to Annan to apologize to the people, and he was eventually pardoned at the time of a general amnesty. Yan Shengqi, an official from Luo Prefecture, was appointed as a temporary censor to inspect the Jiangnan region. He had a strong liking for beef, and wherever he went, there was a lot of slaughtering of cattle. No matter the size of the matter, if gold was offered, it would be resolved. Everywhere he went, gold and silver became extremely expensive. Therefore, people in Jiangnan called him the "Gold Cattle Governor."

Zhang Changyi, serving as the magistrate of Luoyang, borrowed the power and influence of the easy-going style, and all his subordinate officials were willing to comply. The news spread like a drumbeat, and one day, a man surnamed Xue carried gold worth fifty taels, blocked his path, and presented it to him. Zhang Changyi received the gold and the complaint, and handed it over to Zhang Xi, the Director of Personnel, at court. After several days, he lost the complaint and asked Zhang Changyi about it. Zhang Changyi said, "I do not remember anyone named Xue." He immediately asked Zhang Xi to check the records for over sixty people surnamed Xue and appointed them all to official positions. Corruption in government was like this. Volume 3 of *Chao Ye Qian Zai* The Imperially Approved Complete Library Volume 4 of *Chao Ye Qian Zai* Compiled by Zhang Zhe during the Tang Dynasty Xin Dan served as the Minister of Personnel during the Sui Dynasty. The list of candidates for selection briefly stated: "Prefectures oppress, counties delay, towns do not report, and villages harbor resentment." A learned person asked Xin Dan: "The current emperor is wise and enlightened, and officials follow orders. The territory is expanding outward, and internal governance is orderly. You hold a position of balance, your duty is to be like a mirror, at the forefront of promotions and demotions, and you hold the power of praise and criticism. You should know what is right and wrong, what is stuck and what is subtle, make the unqualified ones stay in their place, and allow the talented to rise. Why do you sit idly and eat without working, hold a high position unworthily, and have promotions and demotions misplaced? Selections cause harm, petty people are in power, and virtuous people criticize. No one does not fight on your behalf, but how can you be at peace?" Xin Dan replied, "The children of the common people and people from all over the country are too many to know everyone. Who is favored and who is close? Those praised by Jie (a tyrant) cannot help but be happy, and those criticized by Yao (a virtuous ruler) cannot help but be angry. Those who get positions are happy, and those who lose them are angry. If we look at it in detail, it is not my fault. What is the illness? What is the illness? If you do not recognize them, why not ask for their names?"

If there are fewer officials, why not carefully examine the details and traces to truly understand the nature of the law and its application? Do you not know that precious gems can be found in stones and gold in sand? With your talents and wisdom, you are only suitable to be sent to the remote regions to guard against evil spirits. Complaints and grievances are not few and actually harm the harmony and peace. Xin Dan bowed twice and thanked, saying, "Mr. Xin Dan, you have criticized me, and I feel that I have committed many violations. I will deeply reflect on my actions and correct my mistakes. Please allow me to self-improve and waive my punishment. If I make more mistakes, I am willing to accept severe punishment." The teacher replied, "People like you are as numerous as the stars in the sky and are easily replaceable in the government. How can you delay and stay long? You must leave immediately, as there is no place for you to stay." Xin Dan covered his face with tears and said, "My faults have brought about my own downfall. I dare not show my face again to the holy court." The teacher pulled out his walking stick and sang, "Xin Dan, go. Open the way for talented people at the Ministry of Personnel. You will not meet peace this year, but you will definitely return to the capital next year."

During the Sui dynasty, Niuhong served as the Deputy Minister of Personnel. There was a candidate named Ma Chang who had the ugliest appearance. Niuhong looked down on him and, while lying on his side eating fruit, mocked him by saying, "I have heard that the horses of Fufeng are so fine that even if they come down from heaven, they would not be inferior. Now I see the horses of Fufeng, and they are not even as good as a donkey." Ma Chang responded immediately, "I have heard that the oxen of Longxi are so valuable that even if they are worth a thousand stones, they do not need a yoke. Now I see the oxen of Longxi, and they are lying on the ground eating grass." Niuhong was startled and got up, and thus gave him an official position. In the Chen dynasty, someone was sent to visit the Sui dynasty to test the depth of the envoy's wit and eloquence. They secretly ordered Hou Bai to change his appearance, wear old, ragged clothes, and serve as a lowly servant. The guest treated him as a lowly person and mocked him. Hou Bai replied, "The horses of my country have different prices. If they have good skills, a good appearance, and are suitable for riding, they are worth at least twenty thousand coins. If they are only strong and can carry loads, they are worth at least four or five thousand coins. If they are dry-tailed, have rough hooves, and have no skills, they are lying on the ground eating grass and are not worth even a single coin." The envoy was greatly surprised and asked his name, realizing that he was Hou Bai, and only then did he feel ashamed and apologize.

During the Tang Dynasty, Gao Shilin, a renowned scholar-official, was known for his wit and integrity. He once selected a person of high status who claimed to be skilled in humor and jest. Gao, in a playful mood, wore wooden shoes and challenged him to a humorous exchange. The man responded with a line that was both clever and obscure, something like, "How could you poke your nose into something that isn't your business, or step on someone's face without knowing it?" Gao, finding the exchange amusing, laughed and led him away. In the era of Empress Wu Zetian, foreigners were often allowed to submit memorials to the court, and many were promoted and rewarded for their contributions. One such foreigner became a high-ranking official known as a Right Platform Censor. On one occasion, Empress Wu asked Zhang Yuanyi, a court official, what amusing things had happened outside the palace. Zhang replied with a series of humorous observations about the eccentricities of his colleagues. He mentioned Zhu Qianyi, who wore green; Lu Renjie, who wore red; Yan Zhimou, who rode a horse named Ma Jifu; and Li Qianli, who used his name as a surname. He also noted Wu Qiwu, a Left Platform Censor, and the Right Platform Censor, both of whom were known as "Hu" (a term that could be interpreted as either "foreign" or "black"). Zhang then pointed out that a foreigner who had served as a Right Platform Censor was later reassigned to another position. During the period of political upheaval known as the Zhou Revolution, a man from Beizhou named Zhao Kuo, who was of humble background, rose to the position of Inspector of Judicial Affairs. He was often referred to as a "stain on the court." Li Zhaode, another official, once called him a "crow," a derogatory term. Zhang, in turn, referred to him as a "buzzard" and noted that he was once in a position of power, like sitting on an eagle perch. At the same time, Lu Kongqiu from Tongzhou, who served as a Remonstrant Censor, had a martial spirit and was often called the "commander of the external army." Zhang, however, referred to him as a "buzzard" and noted that he had entered the Phoenix Pool, a metaphor for a high position in the imperial court. Su Weidao, a man of great talent, learning, and wisdom, was highly respected and looked up to by others. In contrast, Wang Fangqing, who was considered ugly and unrefined in appearance and speech, was no more intelligent or talented than the average person. Both men served as侍郎in the Phoenix Pavilion, a high-ranking position in the imperial bureaucracy. When asked by Zhang which of the two was more talented, Zhang replied that Su was like a frosty eagle in September, while Wang was like a bird in October, implying that Su was more formidable and impressive.

A weak person once asked about the cause of a falcon's decline, and the response was that the falcon's skill had been dulled by frost, and people believed it embodied something. During the reign of the Tang Dynasty, the descendant of a Khitan leader named Wanrong launched a raid against the regions of You and He. Prince Yizong, the commander-in-chief, led his army to Zhaozhou and heard that the enemy, Lu Wu Zheng, was approaching with several thousand cavalry. Fearing the enemy's strength, Prince Yizong abandoned his weapons and armor and fled south to Jingzhou, leaving military supplies and equipment scattered along the road. When he heard that the enemy had retreated, he advanced again. Upon returning to the capital, the army held a grand banquet. At the banquet, Yuan Yi mocked Prince Yizong in front of the emperor, saying, "With a long and short measurement of an arrow and a horse from Shu, he tricked the enemy into retreating seven hundred miles, and alone he fought against the enemy, carelessly discarding his armor and weapons, riding a pig as a southern official." The emperor laughed greatly and asked Prince Yizong, "Do you have a horse, and why are you riding a pig?" Prince Yizong replied, "I am riding a pig because it is running away." The emperor laughed even more. Prince Yizong then said, "Yuan Yi had prepared this in advance and did not improvise." The emperor said, "You must match the rhyme with him." Prince Yizong said, "Please use the 'beng' rhyme." Yuan Yi responded, "Wrapping the head very carelessly, sweeping and not neatly, not having seen the face of peach blossoms, the skin is carelessly made into apricot eyes." The emperor was greatly pleased, but Prince Yizong had an extremely ashamed look, as he had a short and ugly appearance. This is why Yuan Yi said, "Long and short measurement of an arrow," referring to Prince Yizong's inconsistent and absurd actions. The emperor was delighted, and the king was deeply embarrassed.

The Short and Ugly Sister of the Wu Clan, the Eldest and Known as Dagē Xiànzhǔ, was riding with Wu Zetian. Wu Zetian ordered Yuan Yi to compose a poem, which read: "The horse wears a peach blossom brocade skirt, dragging green grass, and the robe clearly shows the hat, resembling Dagē." Wu Zetian laughed, and the sister felt extremely ashamed. The official Lou Shide, who was tall, dark, and had a limp in one foot, was nicknamed "Xingzhe Fangxiang" (行辙方相) and also called "Wei Linggong" (卫灵公). The official Ji Xu, who was tall and liked to look up and far, was nicknamed "Wangliu Luotuo" (望栁骆驼). The official Yuan Ben, who was short and thin, was nicknamed "Lingnan Kaodian" (领南考典). The official Zhu Qianyi, who was short and fat, was nicknamed "Guanglu Zhaishan" (光禄掌膳). The official Dongfang Qiu, who was tall, was nicknamed "Waijun Xiaowei" (外军校尉). The official Tang Bo'uo, who was short, was nicknamed "Yuqu Shuma" (鬰屈蜀马). The scholar Li Zhaode, who died, was nicknamed "Suihusun Xiuwen" (岁猢狲修文学士). The official Ma Jifu, who was one-eyed, was nicknamed "Duanyanshi" (端箭师). The official Zhang Ruzi, who liked to look up and far, was nicknamed "Xiacu Han" (呷醋汉). The official Su Zheng, who was light and thin, was nicknamed "Shikong Laozu" (失孔老鼠). The official Zhang Yuan Yi, who was short and had a small neck and eyes, was nicknamed "Niliu Xieha" (逆流虾蟆) by Ji Xu (吉顼). In Zhou, the magistrate of Qujiang County, Zhu Suihou, had a wife named Li Ti, who was a traveler. Erzhu Jiu was a young man with a charming appearance, and the people of Guangzhou called him "Sanqiao" (三樵). A song was sung: "Imperial decree to pursue Sanqiao, Suihou walked beside the road. He turned around and said to Li Lang, 'Call Erzhu Jiu.'" Zhang Zhuo (张鷟) called Suihou "Huanluntu Niao" (臛乱土袅).

Li Xiang, a man from Hedong, was valiant, resolute, and vigorous. He initially served as a minor official in Zizhou, where he was responsible for examining documents. The governor asked if the documents were fair, and Li Xiang replied that they were not. The governor said, "If you think it's unfair, you can take the pen and examine it." Li Xiang responded, "Please let me examine it, Your Excellency." The governor immediately wrote, "He is timid in making major decisions, yet meticulous in minor investigations; he is as insubstantial as paste and blinds the Holy and Divine Emperor." The examination resulted in a failing grade, and the governor remained silent and dismissed him. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, candidates for official positions did not need to take exams and were directly appointed to office. Many rose from humble beginnings to become censors, deputy censors, and secretaries who corrected omissions. Zhang Zhe once remarked, "There are so many censors that they could fill carts, and so many deputy censors that they could be measured by the bushel." He further added, "The wrists of the censors are twisted off, and the hands of the secretaries are broken." At the time, there was a man named Shen Quanjiao who was arrogant, frivolous, and self-promoting. He wore a tall hat and a long robe and would recite poetry in the southern courtyard. Someone added four lines to his poem: "Deputies do not study the law, and doctors do not seek the classics. A governor as insubstantial as paste blinds the Holy and Divine Emperor." Shen Quanjiao was subsequently brought before the Left Censor, Ji Xianzhi, who accused him of slandering the court and harming the state. Ji Xianzhi demanded that he be punished by flogging in the court, but Empress Wu laughed and said, "As long as you do not abuse your power, what do I care about what others say? There is no need to punish him." Ji Xianzhi then turned pale with fear. In the Tang Dynasty, He Rujin, the magistrate of Yuzhang, was known for his twitching eyelids and thick neck. Zhang Zhe called him "a well-fed calf." Once, Zheng Yin, a Tang official, insulted a candidate for office, calling him a "foolish man." The candidate replied, "I am a foolish man from Wu, and you are a foolish man from Wu." Zheng Yin then asked him to compose a poem about a "foolish Wu man," and he wrote: "A son of elm and a wife of elm, building a house and making a cart at the same time. Around the seventh, eighth, or ninth night, returning a book and then borrowing another one." Zheng Yin originally had the surname Mo, but he changed it to Zheng, and people began to call him "Mo Zheng."

During the Tang Dynasty, Li Jingxuan, the Chief Secretary of the Secretariat, served as the field marshal and campaigned against the Tibetans, reaching Shudun City. Upon hearing that Official Liu had been captured by the Tibetans and was unable to wear his boots, he fled in a panic. At that time, General Wang Gao and Vice Commander Cao Huishun, among others, also fled in panic, leaving behind their meal and leaving a trace on the ground for a thousand miles. At that time, a folk song circulated among the troops: "Yaohe Granny, Shanzhou Aunt, see the enemy but cannot fight; all due to Cao's new wife." Zhu Qinming, the Minister of the Ministry of Rites, was somewhat knowledgeable in the classics and histories but not concerned with current affairs. He was broad and fat, stubborn and suspicious. The small clerks in the office called him "Old Lady," meaning a meat block with no seven orifices, a reference to a story from the time of King Mu of Qin when a commoner found such a creature. During the Tianbao era, Jiang Shidu dug canals around the imperial court and markets in Chang'an, extending them to the highest point, where he climbed the Western Tower to look down. Jiang built a dam with logs and planks, and later was appointed as the Minister of Agriculture. Afterward, when the water rose, it surged and overflowed, and when it receded, it dried up. He also opened the Yellow River to divert water to Dezhou, costing millions of laborers, and the people suffered from flooding. He then ordered the laborers to block the river. In the sixth year of the Kaiyuan era, the water overflowed at the river mouth, breaking the dam and submerging Dezhou, causing all the people to drown. Jiang considered this a great achievement and his official rank was promoted. Another official, Fu Xiaozhong, served as the Grand Historian and claimed to be skilled in astronomy, practicing deceit and trickery. The people of the capital said, "Jiang Shidu looks at the ground with one eye, while Fu Xiaozhong looks at the sky with two eyes." When Emperor Shenwu ascended the throne, he discovered their deceit and had them both executed. Jiang Hui served as the Minister of the Ministry of Personnel, unable to read or write, and mismanaged the selection process, showing no discrimination. The selected officials sang, "This year's selection is just right; all due to the chief examiner's lack of talent. Behind the case, a lump of frozen pork." Hence, he was called Jiang the Secretary.

During the Tang Dynasty, the Minister of War Yao Yuanzong was tall and quick-tempered. Wei Guangcheng called him "a swift heron and crane chasing snakes." Lu Huishen, the Grand Secretary of the Yellow Gate, often looked down. Wei Guangcheng referred to him as "a cat watching mice."
