Jiang Jiao, the Grand Master of the Imperial Court, was fat and dark-skinned. Wei Guangcheng called him "a pig well-fed on mulberries." Ni Ruoshui, the Palace Attendant, was dark-skinned and had no beard. Wei Guangcheng referred to him as "a drunken tribal leader." Qi Chuchong, another Palace Attendant, had a squinting eye. Wei Guangcheng called him "a dim candle searching for lice." Lü Yanshi, the Palace Attendant, was tall with sparse hair. Wei Guangcheng referred to him as "an envoy from Japan." Zheng Min, another Palace Attendant, was called "a drunken Korean" by Wei Guangcheng. Cai Fu, the Imperial Historian, was a medical doctor in a small state who pretended to be knowledgeable about medicine. Zhang Xiaosong, the Imperial Censor, was short, ugly, and dark-skinned. Wei Guangcheng called him "a piece of smoky wood." Yang Shenshi, the Palace Attendant, was referred to as "a monkey on a high branch" by Wei Guangcheng. Xing Hui, the Imperial Historian, was called "a court musician" by Wei Guangcheng. Wei Tian, the Vice Minister, was referred to as "a Brahmin praying for rain" by Wei Guangcheng. Li Guang, the Palace Attendant, was called "a water-filled frog" by Wei Guangcheng. Because of these descriptions, these officials were criticized and ridiculed for their appearance or behavior. They were demoted from their positions as Imperial Historians to the new state of Xinzhou and became county magistrates. During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Tang Dynasty, Yuan Song, the magistrate of Guiyang, had a wife named Yan Shi who was extremely jealous. When Yuan Song was drinking with guests, he summoned a female servant to sing. Yan Shi arrived at the scene with her hair loose, barefoot, and holding a knife. The guests were shocked and scattered, and Yuan Song hid under the bed. The female servant ran away in a panic. Cui Miao, the governor, wrote an evaluation of Yuan Song, stating that the wife was strong and the husband was weak, with an inner strength but an outward gentleness. He questioned how a single wife could control the people and how the government could maintain order. The evaluation was submitted to the central government for review and decision.

#### Part 1: **唐郝象贤侍郎处俊之孙顿丘令南容之子也弱冠诸友生为之字曰宠之每于父前称字父绐之曰汝朋友极贤吾为汝设馔可命之也翼日象贤因邀致十数人南容引生与之饮谓曰谚云三公后出死狗小儿诚愚劳诸君制字损南容之身尚可岂可波及侍中也因涕泣众慙而退宠之者反语为痴种也** Hao Xiangxian, a Langya of the Tang Dynasty, was the grandson of Cujun, a Langya, and the son of Nanrong, the governor of Duntou. When he was twenty (weak crown), his friends gave him the courtesy name "Chongzhi." Whenever he addressed his father by this name, his father would deceive him, saying, "Your friends are extremely virtuous; I will prepare a feast for you and invite them." The next day, Hao Xiangxian invited about a dozen people. Nanrong led them to a banquet and said, "There is a saying: 'After the Three Dukes, there come dead dog children.' Indeed, it is foolish to trouble you all with naming, which harms my son's dignity, but how much more so to affect the dignity of a chancellor?" He then wept, and the guests felt ashamed and left. "Chongzhi" is a reversal of words meaning "foolish offspring." #### Part 2: **朱前疑浅钝无识容貌极丑上书云臣梦见陛下八百歳即授拾遗俄迁郎中出使回又上书云闻嵩山唱万嵗声即赐绯鱼袋未入五品于緑衫上带之朝野莫不怪笑后契丹反有敕京官出马一匹供军者即酧五品前疑买马纳讫表索绯上怒批其状即放归丘园愤恚而卒** Zhu Qianyi was dull-witted and ignorant, with an extremely ugly appearance. He wrote to the emperor, saying, "I dreamed that Your Majesty would live for eight hundred years and would appoint me as a Pick-up (a low-ranking official). Soon after, I was promoted to Langzhong (a mid-ranking official) and returned from a mission. I wrote again, saying, 'I heard that Mount Song is singing 'Long Live the Emperor,' and I was granted a red fish bag (a symbol of higher rank), though I was not yet a fifth-grade official, and wore it on my green official robe.' The court and the common people found this absurd and laughed. Later, when the Khitans rebelled, the emperor decreed that officials who provided a horse for the army would be granted the rank of fifth grade. Zhu Qianyi bought a horse and submitted a report to claim the red fish bag. The emperor became angry and dismissed him, sending him back to his estate, where he died in frustration. #### Part 3: **唐王及善才行庸猥风神钝浊为内史时人号为鸠集凤池俄迁文昌右相无他政但不许令史双驴入台终日迫逐无时蹔舎时人号为驱驴宰相** Wang Jishan of the Tang Dynasty was mediocre in talent and appearance, with a dull and turbid demeanor. When he was an Inner Historian, people called him "Gathering of Crows in the Phoenix Pool." Soon, he was promoted to the Right Chancellor of the Wenchang Office. He had no other policies but prohibited officials from bringing two donkeys into the office, forcing them to chase the donkeys all day without rest. People called him the "Donkey-Chasing Chancellor." #### Part 4: **周有逯仁杰河阳人自地官令史出尚书改天下帐式颇甚繁细法令滋章每邨立社官仍置平甚老三员掌簿案设鏁钥十羊九牧人皆逃散而宰相浅识以为万代可行授仁杰地官郎中数年百姓苦之其法遂寝** During the Zhou Dynasty, there was Lu Renjie of Heyang, who started as a clerk in the Earth Official Office and later became a minister, changing the national accounting system. The new system was very complex and detailed, with laws becoming increasingly elaborate. Each village had to set up a local office with three very old officials to manage records and lockboxes. Ten sheep were guarded by nine shepherds, and people fled in droves. The chancellor, with shallow understanding, believed this system could last for generations and appointed Lu Renjie as an Earth Official Langzhong. After several years, the people suffered greatly, and the system was eventually abandoned.

During a national mourning day, Yuan Yan, a clerk in the Ministry of Personnel, was tasked with managing a gathering and subsequently made a judgment, stating, "The office is always noisy; without the distraction of a national mourning day, people could not express their joy." Everyone present laughed at the situation. In the Ministry of War, Hou Zhi, a high-ranking official, was ordered to retire due to old age but refused and showed off his agility in the court. Zhang Cong, who was mourning for his mother, requested to resume his duties, while Gao Yun, whose mother had died, refused to perform the mourning rituals and said, "I cannot act as a filial official." Zhang Qi Zhen was accused of falsely claiming a mother's death to avoid work, and the court said, "Hou Zhi refused to retire, Zhang Cong requested to resume duties, Gao Yun refused to perform the mourning rituals, and Zhang Qi Zhen willingly endured mourning. All of them are not people of the moral code and are outside the realm of civilization, with hearts of beasts and faces of humans." Shen Zi Rong, a candidate for a position in the Ministry of Personnel, recited 200 judgments but failed to write them on the day of the exam. When asked, he said, "It is not my fate." Today, he recited judgments but none matched. There was one that was somewhat similar to a person's name. The next year, when asked about watermills, he again failed to write. When asked, he said, "I recited watermills in Lantian, but now they are asking about Fufeng. How can I write?" Those who heard this could not help but laugh. During an inner banquet, Emperor Wu was very happy, but Prince Yi Zong suddenly stood up and reported, "I have a report to make." Wu was surprised and asked, "What is it?" Yi Zong replied, "I was ordered to collect taxes from the previous household, and recently, the state and counties were ordered to send them, causing significant damage." Wu was angry and looked at the rafters for a long time, saying, "My relatives are drinking and enjoying themselves, but you, as a prince, are responsible for collecting taxes for three or two hundred households. This is a shock. I cannot bear to be a king. I order you to be dragged out." Yi Zong removed his hat and knelt down. The other princes saved him, saying, "Yi Zong is dull and made an unintentional mistake." Wu then released him.

Zhang Heng, who began his career as a low-ranking official, reached the fourth rank and, with an additional rank, would have entered the third rank. However, after dismissing court, he saw freshly steamed buns by the roadside and bought one to eat on horseback. He was subsequently impeached by the censor. Empress Wu Zetian issued an edict demoting him to an outer office and barring him from entering the third rank, thus he lost his examination eligibility. Li Liangbi, a Right Remonstrant of the Zhou dynasty, boasted about his eloquence and love for discussing metaphysical principles. He requested to be sent to the northern frontier to persuade the Khitan tribe. They fed him with a wooden plate full of excrement and threatened him with a white blade. Li Liangbi, fearing for his life, ate the entire plate of excrement and was then released. People mocked him, saying, "Li Remonstrant can pick up the leftovers of the Turks." He was later appointed as the magistrate of Zhenyuan County. After completing his term, he returned to Yingzhou, where he encountered Sun Wanyong, a leader of a rebellion, and his subordinate, He Aoxiao, who were gathering supplies from Cang and Ying. Li Liangbi told Li Huaibi, the magistrate of Luocheng, "Sun is a monkey, and monkeys are hard to deal with. The character '万' with grass in it means 'hidden in the grass.' I advise you to surrender." He Aoxiao granted Li Huaibi the title of Fifth-Rank General. When He Aoxiao was defeated, Li Huaibi and Li Liangbi, along with their four sons, were all executed by Hei Wuyi, the Duke of Heinei. Yan Zhiwei, a Spring Official and Grand Secretary of the Zhou dynasty, was an incompetent and timid official. He was sent to the frontier and received the title of Han Khan by the Khitan leader. When the Khitans invaded, he was sent ahead to Zhaozhou to persuade the locals. The general Chen Lingying and others were guarding the western part of the city. Yan Zhiwei told Chen Lingying, "Why didn't you surrender earlier? Once the Khan's troops arrive, those who do not surrender will not leave a single inch of land." Chen Lingying did not respond. Yan Zhiwei sang "Long Live the Emperor" below the city walls. Chen Lingying replied, "As a high-ranking official in the state, you have been entrusted with important duties. To sing for the enemy is shameful." Yan Zhiwei then sang, "Long Live the Emperor, Long Live the Year, Not Free." People at the time looked down on him for this.

During the Tang Dynasty, Cui Shi served as the Minister of Personnel and was notorious for his corruption and arrogance. He and his family members, including his brother, father, and son, were all involved in accepting bribes and engaging in various corrupt practices, leading to a state of chaos and disorder. His father, Cui Yi, was the Director of the Imperial Academy and accepted money from candidates for admission, which Cui Shi was unaware of. When a candidate was admitted, they complained, saying, "Why didn't you give me an official position?" Cui Shi replied, "Who is your relative? I will catch and whip him to death." However, he was immediately summoned for mourning duties, which greatly embarrassed him. The emperor, seeing that Cui Shi's father was old, granted him a special favor by giving him a ripe melon. Cui Shi gave the melon to his concubine but not to his father, which was widely criticized by the court and the public. At the time, Cui Cen and Zheng Yin were also serving in the Ministry of Personnel, and there was a popular song in the capital that said, "Cen Xi, the son of a barbarian, is a fine man; Cui Shi, the grandson of a noble, is a fine man. The three of them are closely related, but none of them are as good as Mo He Du, who is the most refined." In the Tang Dynasty, General Quan Longxiang of the Left Guard was known for his narrow-mindedness and often boasted about his poetry. During the Tongtian period, he became the governor of Cangzhou and, upon his arrival, presented a poem to the local officials: "From afar, the city of Cangzhou looks green with willows; in the center, a group of men sit together drinking wine." The officials praised him, saying, "You have a rare talent." Quan Longxiang replied, "I dare not say so; I just followed the rhyme." He then composed another poem about his autumn thoughts: "Seven hundred birds fly by the eaves, white as snow; in the backyard, I eat heartily; in the room, I lie down on my side, and the excrement gathers around me; in the wild, I catch a cicada." The officials did not understand the poem and asked for an explanation. Quan Longxiang said, "The birds flying by the eaves are hawks, worth seven hundred coins; the white object is a shirt hung on the garden pole, as white as snow; after eating, I lie down on my side in the room, and the excrement gathers around me; I catch a cicada in the wild." The officials laughed at his explanations. The crown prince held a summer banquet and asked the participants to compose poems. One participant wrote, "The frost is white and vast, the moon is red and round." The crown prince took up the pen and wrote a commentary: "Quan Longxiang, the talented man of Qin Prefecture, the bright moon shines during the day, and the frost rises in the summer." Quan Longxiang, having been exiled to Rongshan Commandery due to the fall of Zhang Yizhi, was recalled to the capital during the Shendong period. He then presented a poem to the emperor: "I have no business in Rongshan; today I return to the capital. Your Majesty has granted me a special favor."

In recent times, I have been appointed as the Right Gold-Standard Commander and composed a poem celebrating rain, which says, "When it disappears, there is no rain; when it arrives, there is no cloud. The sun is scorching, and the ground is steamy." As the governor of Yingzhou, on New Year's Day, several people in the capital attached a letter saying, "Change the year to the Year of Deep Gratitude. I respectfully think you should join in." The new officials were gathered and informed that there was an imperial edict changing the year to the Year of Deep Gratitude. When the letter was presented to the lower officials, everyone laughed. Long Xiang, upon hearing this, listened attentively and was surprised that the edict had arrived late. The counties of Gaoyang and Boye were disputing land and presented formal documents. Long Xiang then ruled, "The two counties are disputing land, which is not within the jurisdiction of the state. Since it is between two counties, it is reasonable to hand it over to the local authorities." Long Xiang showed a legal precedent and said, "Recently, when officials make judgments, they do not sign their names." Long Xiang replied, "Others do not understand. If I do not sign my name, how would they know who I am? I am just a stray donkey." Long Xiang did not know it was a taboo day and asked the court historian, "What is called a private taboo?" The historian replied, "It is the day of one's parents' death anniversary. One should ask for leave and sit alone in the room without coming out." On that day, Long Xiang sat quietly in the room, and a black dog suddenly entered. Long Xiang became very angry and said, "It has disrupted my taboo." He then changed the date of the document to the next day to avoid the taboo day, which made people laugh. Li Yide, originally a lowly person, betrayed his master and went into hiding. When Emperor Xuanzong raised an army and achieved success with the help of Wang Maozhong and others, Li Yide became a General of the Guard. When his former master met him on the road, he would avoid him and not look at him directly. Li Yide ordered his attendants to invite his former master, who was very frightened and came to his house. He asked to sit in the upper seat, and Li Yide personally served him wine and food. The former master, sweating profusely, declined. They stayed for several days, and Li Yide then presented a petition saying, "I have received too much honor and wealth from the state. My former master was of low rank and had not received any rewards. I offer to give half of my salary and resign my position to honor him. I wish the emperor to grant my humble request." The emperor praised his intentions and promoted his former master to the position of Langjiang (a military officer). Li Yide resumed his rank, and the court praised this action.

苏颋五岁时，裴谈拜访他的父亲，当时苏颋正在场，于是试着背诵庾信的《枯树赋》，即将背完时，为了避免提到裴谈的名字，他改了韵脚，说道：“昔年移树依依汉阴，今看摇落凄凄江浔。树犹如此，人何以任。”裴谈听后惊叹不已，长时间赞赏，由此知道苏颋日后必定成为文章大家。 唐娄师德，荥阳人，担任纳言。有人问浮休子：“娄纳言如何？”浮休子回答说：“娄纳言外表温和而内心谨慎，外显愚钝而内藏聪敏，表面晦暗而内心光明。他如万顷波涛，浑然不浊；如百炼之质，磨而不磷。可以说他是当代的贤人君子，名公之一。”那人又问：“狄仁杰担任纳言如何？”浮休子回答说：“狄仁杰粗略地浏览过经史，对文学有浅薄的了解，他有古人之风，给予诚恳的劝谏，剪除浮华之词，有烈士之操守。他的心神耿直，不为外物所染，胆气刚强，明察秋毫，能做出果断的决策。然而，晚年沉迷于金钱，与和峤之流为伍。”那人又问：“凤阁侍郎李昭德可谓名相吗？”浮休子回答说：“李昭德志向远大但器量狭小，气度高傲但智慧不足，他假借权势控制他人，对小人虽有警惕但过于刚愎自用，缺乏谦恭和宽容，这不是保全自己的方法。”不久，李昭德被依法惩处。那人又问：“洛阳令来俊臣如何？”浮休子回答说：“来俊臣雍容华贵，举止优雅。”

Is the person described as "貌忠赤之士" (appearing loyal and devoted) truly loyal, or is he like Jiang Chong, a notorious traitor? Answer: Junchen appears gentle on the surface but is actually cruel and treacherous, taking dangerous actions and having shallow virtue. His clever arguments seem wise, and his flattery seems loyal, but he overthrows families and countries and falsely accuses the innocent. Is he like Jiang Chong? A scorpion or wasp that harms others will eventually suffer the same fate. He soon became the Grand Steward, only to be executed in the western market. Is Wu Sanzhi a famous prince? Answer: Sanzhi relied on his royal connections to rise above the highest rank, appearing respectful but being extremely cruel. He appears fair and upright on the outside while plotting secretly, using the king's laws to take revenge and official power to harm others. Later enfeoffed as the Prince of Dejing, he was a great traitor and did not die a natural death, eventually being killed by the Prince of Jieming. Is Wei Yuanzhong a loyal and upright minister in recent times? Answer: Yuanzhong lacks both literary and martial skills, and his reputation and reality are both empty. He appears steadfast and strong but is actually fawning, facing Zhang Shiqi's faction head-on with the courage of a bear, yet flattering Wu Shi Kai's group with the timidity of a weak dog. He wavers between two choices, like a snake or lizard.

No intention to disrupt the court or ruin the government was not this person who followed Zhang San Si and denounced the families of the Five Princes. After careful observation, he did not die a natural death. However, he was exiled to Sizhou due to some offense and died of worry and anger. What is Xu Yougong, the Chief Justice, like? He is an upright and honest person, clear-sighted and brave, firm and able to prevent wrongdoing. In times of decline and hardship, he does not flatter to gain favor. In a chaotic and unstable court, he does not use humble words to avoid blame. When Lai Junchen fabricated charges, Xu Yougong cleared them. When Yuan Zhihong fabricated charges, Xu Yougong treated them leniently. He treads on the tail of a tiger without being startled, touches the scales of a dragon without fear, and perches like a phoenix.

In the midst of danger, one must remain upright to preserve oneself, and in the midst of chaos, one must remain loyal to avoid harm. In times of peace, how can Zhang Shizhi and Yu Dingguo be compared? When asked about the agricultural minister Zhao Lutang, the response was: Zhao Lutang has no interest in learning, does not recognize the written word, appears respectful but is cruel, has small intelligence but great ambitions, hesitates like a dog thief, acts suddenly like a greedy pig, is lured outward by the morning sheep, unaware of its death, and is like a moth that flies into the candle flame, unaware of its demise. His head is placed on his neck, how long can he last? Later, he was involved in the rebellion of Wei Shi, and his three clans were destroyed. When asked about Zheng Yin as the director of the personnel department, the response was: Zheng Yin is a reckless and cunning man of little learning and empty words, lacking in talent and virtue. He crouches like a fox in a noble position, crouches like a pheasant at the gate of power, relying on the talents of others, first on Jun Chen and then on Zhang Yi, disrupting the tranquility of the court and the peace of the household. Like a bird perched on the tip of a reed, or a fish swimming in a boiling pot, he lacks the grace of a gentleman and is ultimately just an ordinary man. Seeking honor in this way, he finds death to be a fortunate outcome. Indeed, he was executed for plotting rebellion.

At the end of the Zhenguan era, Jingyi lived in an empty, green mountain. There was often a magpie's nest near his dwelling, and he would regularly feed the magpie with his meals. A neighbor who lost cloth falsely accused Jingyi of stealing it, and he was imprisoned in Nan'gang. After more than a month, Jingyi did not confess to the crime, and they wanted to interrogate him. One day, the magpie perched on the prison tower and faced Jingyi, seeming to convey a message. On that day, it was announced that there would be a pardon. The officials asked the magpie about the message, and it described encountering a person wearing black clothes and a white collar, who said the pardon would come in three days. The next day, the pardon was announced, and Jingyi returned to the mountain. He realized that the person in black and white was the message conveyed by the magpie. In Ruzhou, Zhang Changqi, the younger brother of Zhang Yi, was arrogant and proud due to his favor. He was cruel and oppressive to his subordinates. Someone from Liang county reported that a white magpie had been seen by Zhang Changqi. Zhang Changqi ordered Yang Chuyu, the head of the household affairs department, to capture the magpie. There were seventy cages containing falcons, and he ordered the falcons' claws to be coated with wax. When the falcons encountered the white magpie and a group of other magpies, the magpies scattered upon seeing the falcons. Only the white magpie remained, and the falcon leaped up to catch it without any injury. The magpie was captured and sent in a cage. Zhang Changqi laughed and said, "This magpie has redeemed your life." Yang Chuyu bowed and said, "This is a sign from heaven that saved me; otherwise, I would have thrown myself into the river or the sea and not dared to see you again."

渤海高嶷，一位巨富，突然患病，持续了一个多月后突然去世，但他的心脏仍然温暖，几天后他苏醒过来。他说有一个白衣人，眼睛失明，拿着文书，阻止他的妻子和孩子们被杀害。高嶷原本不认识这位老人，防官说他的生命尚未终结，可以暂时放他回去。他恍然大悟，那白衣人其实是他家的老瞎麻鸡。于是他命令射杀这只麻鸡，从此不再受其困扰。 文明年间以后，天下各州出现了许多雌鸡变为雄鸡的现象，有的已经完全变性，有的则部分变性，这被认为是武则天登基的预兆。 卫镐是县官，他下乡到村里，见村民王幸正准备小睡。王幸梦见一个穿着黑衣的女人，带着十几个穿黄衣的孩子，都在乞求饶命，不停地磕头。不久后，卫镐非常厌恶这件事，便匆匆吃完饭去拜访朋友。有人告诉他，王幸家里很穷，没有东西招待，但有一只鸡已经抱了孩子十多天，正准备被杀。卫镐这才明白，那个黑衣女人其实是只乌鸡。于是他买下这只鸡，带到寺庙放生为长生鹅，但那只鹅不肯进寺庙，反而跟着卫镐走。后来，这只鹅跟着卫镐穿过街市，无论人多的地方都不肯离开。卫镐收养了这只鹅，左丞张锡亲眼见过这件事。 久视年间，越州有一位名叫祖録事的官员，他早上出门时看到一只鹅被带到市场，那只鹅一直看着他并叫唤。祖録事便用钱买下这只鹅，带到寺庙放生为长生鹅，但那只鹅不肯进寺庙，反而跟着他走。后来，这只鹅跟着祖録事穿过街市，无论人多的地方都不肯离开。祖録事收养了这只鹅，左丞张锡亲眼见过这件事。

During the Han Dynasty, the two doors of the southern gate of Yan County would suddenly open and close, with one calling out "yuanyuan" and the other "yongyang." This phenomenon occurred both in the morning and evening, and the sound could be heard in the capital city. By the end of the Han Dynasty, this was considered an ominous sign, and the gate was ordered to be destroyed. Afterward, the two doors transformed into a pair of mandarin ducks and flew away together. Later, Yan County was renamed Yancheng County. During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, Liu Jingyang, a military officer of the Left Guard, traveled to the south and obtained a pair of Asian koels, one male and one female, that could understand human language. When he arrived in the capital, he presented the female bird to the empress, but the male bird refused to eat. Wu Zetian asked, "What is wrong?" The bird replied, "My mate was taken by the envoy and I miss her." The empress then called out to Liu Jingyang, "Why did you hide one of the birds from me?" Liu Jingyang knelt and apologized, and the bird was then presented to the empress, who did not punish him. In Fengzhou, there is a river that flows from Tibet. In the summer, the water is as cold as ice and snow. There are fish that are about one or two inches long, which come and go at certain times, moving on the surface like porridge. People catch and cook them for food, but they cannot be exhausted. The origin of these fish is unknown. In Tongzhou, there are many otters, each with an owner who raises them. The otters live along the riverside. When an otter enters its den, a pheasant's tail is inserted in front of the den, and the otter will not come out. When the tail is removed, the otter comes out and catches fish, but it must go ashore to do so. People then take the fish. Once the otter has caught enough fish, it is allowed to eat on its own. When the otter is full, a stick is beaten to drive it away, and the pheasant's tail is reinserted, preventing the otter from coming out again. Once, a child was washing a horse in the Luo River when he saw something like a white silk ribbon, extremely bright and shiny, wrapping around his neck three or four times. The child fell into the water and died. Such incidents occur in all places where there is water or where people bathe or wash horses. People who die in such circumstances are said to be led by a turtle, but this is not true. This creature is called "White Te" and should be cautiously guarded against, as it is a type of dragon.

In Qi Prefecture there was a lake spanning ten thousand acres, teeming with fish, turtles, and all kinds of aquatic life. During the Xiangheng era, a monk suddenly arrived carrying a begging bowl to ask for food. A respected villager offered him a vegetarian meal, and after eating, the monk departed. At that time, fishermen caught a fish six or seven chi long, with silken scales, engraved armor, and a body of silken quality adorned with precious patterns—truly an extraordinary fish. They carried it to the state as a gift, but it died on the way back to the village. The villagers then cut it open and divided it among themselves, and inside the fish’s belly they found the vegetables and food the elder had given, still intact. The villagers then held a vegetarian meal in the lake to perform a Buddhist rite for the deceased. From then on, there have been no more aquatic creatures in the lake, and this remains the case to this day. In Hangzhou’s Fuyang County, at Han Xun’s estate, a well was dug only five or six chi deep, and dozens of fish were found in the soil. The earth was rich and moist. During the Zhenguan era, at the Board Bridge Inn in Weizhou, Zhang Di’s wife returned to her parents’ home. Three men from the Weizhou military unit, Yang Zhen and two others, stayed at the inn for the night and departed early in the fifth watch. That night, someone took one of the three guards’ swords and killed Zhang Di. The sword was then returned to its sheath. Yang Zhen and the others were unaware of this. At dawn, the innkeeper rushed to Yang Zhen and the others, who drew their swords, and blood was found everywhere. They were imprisoned and interrogated, and under torture, they falsely confessed. The emperor suspected the verdict and dispatched the imperial inspector Jiang Heng to re-investigate. Jiang ordered all people over fifteen years old from the inn to be gathered, but the number was insufficient and they scattered. Only one elderly woman over eighty years of age was left. She was released late in the day, and the jailer was ordered to secretly watch her, saying: “When the old woman comes out, if someone speaks with her, record their name and do not let it be revealed.” Indeed, one person was seen speaking with her, and their name was recorded. The next day, the same thing happened, and the person asked the old woman, “What is the investigation like?” This occurred for two days, and it was always the same person. Jiang gathered over three hundred men and women, and from among them, called out the one who had spoken with the old woman; the rest were released. When questioned, this person confessed, saying, “I had an affair with Zhang Di’s wife and killed him.” This was reported to the emperor, who granted him two hundred bolts of silk and appointed him as a Censor.

**Records from the Court and the Countryside, Volume Four** <Zi Department, Miscellaneous Stories, Miscellaneous Affairs, Records from the Court and the Countryside> Imperially Approved Four Treasuries of Literature **Records from the Court and the Countryside, Volume Five** **Tang Dynasty** Zhang Zhe **Compiled** During the Zhenguan era, Left Chancellor Li Xinglian's younger brother, Li Xingquan, had a son named Li Zhong from his previous marriage. Li Zhong had sexual relations with his stepmother. He then secretly took and hid something, and a false imperial edict was issued to bring him into the palace. Li Xinglian did not know about this and submitted a report to inquire. Being ordered to investigate, Li Zhong was severely interrogated. His stepmother faked a suicide by hanging with a scarf around her neck and lying on the street. The magistrate of Chang'an County interrogated her and she claimed that someone had falsely announced an imperial edict and called her away. A person in a purple robe was seen and kept her overnight, but his name was unknown. He forced her to hang herself and sent her to the street. Li Zhong, in fear, privately consulted an oracle, which led to suspicion. He was arrested and brought to the county. The county magistrate Wang Jing led him into a room for questioning, but he refused to confess. Wang Jing first ordered someone to hide under the table to listen, and another to run and report that he was called by a superior. Wang Jing locked the door and left. The mother and son said to each other, "We must not confess, and we must keep our private words secret." When Wang Jing returned and opened the door, the person under the table also got up. The mother and son were greatly alarmed and confessed everything, accepting punishment.

Li Jie, the Prefect of Henan, received a complaint from a widow accusing her son of being unfilial. The son could not defend himself, merely saying that he had offended his mother and was content to die. Li Jie observed his demeanor and concluded that he was not an unfilial son. He told the widow, "You are a widow with only one son. Now that you have accused him of a crime that could lead to his death, have you no regrets?" The widow replied, "My son is a troublemaker who does not obey his mother. What do I have to be sorry about?" Li Jie said, "If that is truly the case, you can buy a coffin to retrieve his body." He then sent someone to follow the widow. After she left, she told a Taoist priest, "The matter is settled." Soon after, the coffin arrived. Li Jie still hoped she might have second thoughts and tried to persuade her repeatedly, but she remained firm in her decision. The Taoist priest stood outside the door, and Li Jie secretly ordered him to be captured. Upon questioning, the priest confessed, "I had an affair with the widow and was once troubled by her son, so I wanted to get rid of him." Li Jie released the son and had the Taoist priest and the widow buried together in the same coffin. In the state of Wei, the magistrate of Xinxiang County, Pei Ziyun, was known for his sharp discernment. A man named Wang Jing, who was戍边 (on military service on the frontier), left six cows with his uncle Li Jin to guard. After five years, the cows produced thirty calves, each worth ten guan or more. When Wang Jing returned to claim the cows, Li Jin only gave him two dead cows and four old ones, refusing to return the rest, claiming they were not his calves. Wang Jing was furious and filed a complaint with the county. Pei Ziyun sent Wang Jing to the county jail and ordered the pursuit of the cattle thief, Li Jin. Li Jin was terrified and went to the county, where Pei Ziyun shouted at him, "Thief, bring your accomplice to face the thirty stolen cows hidden in your house." Li Jin was then made to wear a cloth shirt and stand against the south wall. Under pressure, Li Jin confessed, "The thirty cows were all calves born by my nephew, not stolen. Please remove the cloth shirt." Pei Ziyun saw that it was Wang Jing and said, "If it is your nephew, return the cows to him." Li Jin remained silent, and Pei Ziyun said, "You have worked hard for five years to raise these cows. Give him several of them, and the rest can be returned to you." The entire county admired Pei Ziyun's keen judgment.

The Middle Secretariat, Guo Zhengyi, after the conquest of Pingshang, obtained a Korean slave girl named Yusu, who was exceptionally beautiful. He ordered her to be in charge of the treasury. Whenever he needed rice porridge, it had to be prepared by Yusu. One night, he needed rice porridge, and only Yusu could prepare it. Yusu poisoned the rice porridge and served it to Guo Zhengyi. Guo Zhengyi said, "This girl poisoned me. I took earth water and licorice to counteract the poison. It took a long time to recover." When he looked for Yusu, she was missing, and ten valuable items were also lost. He recorded the incident and reported it to the emperor. The emperor ordered the unsavory people to whip and force the search for the thief. After three days of intense search, they still could not find the thief. The commander of the unsavory people, Wei Chang, had a plan. He took a servant from Guo Zhengyi's household, dressed him in a plain shirt and hooded, and took four guards to the prison. The unsavory people were whipped and forced to search for the thief. They searched a vacant house in Jin Cheng Fang and found Yusu and the Korean man hiding there. They were caught and executed by imperial decree in the eastern market.

During the year of Chui Gong, when Wu Zetian was regent and the practice of fabricating charges began, a deputy historian named Jiang Chen from Huzhou took the governor's judgment book, cut and rearranged the characters to form a coherent text, and forged a letter from Xu Jingye, who had rebelled against the state. He reported this to the authorities, who sent officials to investigate. The governor, Pei Guang, confessed to the charges, but the words in his confession were not his own. Three officials were sent to investigate, but they could not reach a conclusion. The emperor then issued an imperial decree, ordering skilled officials to investigate and bring the truth to light. All agreed that Zhang Chu Jin was the right person for the task, so he was sent to handle the case. Zhang Chu Jin was worried and lay down, looking out the west window. When the sun rose, he noticed that the characters seemed to be patched together. When viewed from the side, they appeared normal, but when viewed directly, they were clearly altered. He ordered the state officials to gather and brought a large pot of water. He instructed Jiang Chen to throw the letter into the water, and the characters dispersed one by one. Jiang Chen knelt and confessed his crime. The emperor ordered him to be punished with a hundred strokes of the cane and then executed. Zhang Chu Jin was rewarded with a hundred bolts of silk. In Huai Prefecture's He Nei County, Dong Xingcheng was skilled at catching thieves. One day, a man stole a donkey and its leather bag from a traveler on the road from Heyang to Changdian. As dawn approached, he arrived in Huai Prefecture. Dong Xingcheng saw him in the street and mocked him, saying, "This thief, stop right there. Get off your donkey." The man immediately confessed. Someone asked how he knew. Dong Xingcheng replied, "This donkey was running fast and sweating, which is not typical of a long journey. When he saw people, he led the donkey far away, showing his fear. That is how I knew." He took the thief to the county. Soon after, the donkey's owner arrived, and everything happened exactly as Dong Xingcheng had said.

Zhang Zuo, while serving as the magistrate of Yang County, encountered a case where a false accuser named Lu Yuan forged a document attributed to the warehouse supervisor Feng Chen, stealing grain from the warehouse. Feng Chen denied the document, but Lu Yuan insisted on its authenticity, making it impossible to resolve the case. Zhang Zuo took Lu Yuan's accusation and wrapped both ends of the document, leaving only one character visible. He asked Lu Yuan if the document was his, and if so, to write "yes" with the character stamped; if not, to write "no" with the character stamped. Lu Yuan wrote "no," and when the wrapping was removed, it revealed that the document was indeed Lu Yuan's. Lu Yuan then blushed all over and confessed his guilt. Another case involved a traveler who lost his horse's bridle and saddle after three days of searching without success. He reported the theft to Zhang Zuo, who investigated the matter. That night, Zhang Zuo released the horse and let it go, but kept the saddle hidden. The next morning, Zhang Zuo said, "This is easy to solve." He ordered the horse to be led out with a bridle and sent it back to the traveler's old location. He then instructed a trusted person to watch for anyone talking to an old woman. Soon, a man came and asked the magistrate how he was investigating the case. Zhang Zuo had the man wear a loose robe and lead the horse to the county, and after one question, the man confessed everything, and the thief was caught. The people of the time considered Zhang Zuo a deity or a sage. During Zhang Songshou's tenure as the magistrate of Chang'an, there was a case of robbery and murder near the Kunming Pool. The emperor ordered that the case be solved within ten days, or the responsible officials would be punished. Zhang Songshou arrived at the scene of the crime and followed the trail, finding an old woman selling food under a tree. He sent his horse to the county, where he provided the woman with food and drink for three days. The next morning, he returned the horse to its old location and instructed a trusted person to watch for anyone talking to the old woman. Soon, a man came and asked the magistrate how he was investigating the case. Zhang Songshou had the man wear a loose robe and lead the horse to the county, and after one question, the man confessed everything, and the thief was caught. The people of the time considered Zhang Songshou a deity or a sage.

Young and wise, talented, with exceptional skill and multitasking ability, he could draw circles with his left hand and squares with his right, recite the classics and histories orally, and count a flock of sheep with his eyes. Simultaneously, he composed a forty-character poem in one go, wrote a five-character quatrain, and completed six other tasks at once, earning him the title of "divine child." A man from Bingzhou named Mao Jun gave birth to a son at the age of four. Empress Wu summoned him to test his ability to write the Thousand Character Classic, which he could recite from memory. She bestowed upon him clothes and sent him back, and people believed he was possessed by a spirit. Afterward, his whereabouts were unknown. Liu Shide, a man from Zhengzhou and a minister of war, was sent to Bingzhou. Along the way, he was accompanied by the magistrates of neighboring counties. As the day grew hot, they reached an inn, fearing to disturb the innkeeper, they ordered food to be served. The minister's rice was white and fine, while the others' rice was black and coarse. He called the innkeeper and scolded him, saying, "Why is there such a difference in the quality of the rice?" The innkeeper, fearing to offend, replied, "We happened to mill the rice carelessly, and I am at fault." Liu Shide said, "If the guests are hurried, the host is also hurried, and what harm is there?" He then exchanged the coarse rice for the fine rice and ate it. Inspecting the fields and heading to Liangzhou, there was a man named Lou who was a local official and had committed embezzlement. The military governor Xǔ Qīnmíng wanted to execute him. Liu Shide ordered the locals to petition him to save him. Liu Shide said, "If even a minister's own son cannot be spared for breaking the law, how much less so for someone else?" The next day, at a banquet, the military governor and Liu Shide sat together. Liu Shide said, "I heard that someone has broken the law, and it is said to be a man from my hometown. I do not know him, but I will deal with him accordingly."

When my father was a child, we herded cattle together. The prefect should not be lenient with national laws due to his virtue. The prefect quickly ordered him to remove his枷 (a type of punishment device) and severely rebuked him in the presence of the chancellor. He said, "You left your parents to seek an official position but could not be clean and upright, what else can you do?" He gave him a plate of pounded cakes and said, "Eat them and become a well-fed ghost." The prefect then forgave him, and later he became a chancellor. There was a day when he was inspecting the屯田 (military farming) operation. He consulted the officials and went out early, but due to a foot ailment, he waited for his horse at the横木 (horizontal beam) outside the光政门 (Guangzheng Gate). A county magistrate, not knowing he was the chancellor, told him his name and they sat together. The magistrate's son saw from afar and ran to tell him, "It's the chancellor." The chancellor said, "People don't know each other, what death crime is there?" The magistrate then complained, "There was a person named Zuo Yi who, because of old age and poor eyesight, was relieved of his duties. He wrote a memorial at night, but his eyes were actually not dim." The chancellor said, "If he wrote a memorial at night, why didn't he recognize the chancellor during the day?" The magistrate was greatly ashamed and said, "Please, chancellor, don't tell the emperor." Everyone laughed, and when he was sent to Lingzhou, he indeed stayed at an inn and had food, but when he asked for a horse and water, he couldn't get them at all, and he didn't dare to blame anyone. The chancellor said, "Chancellor Shide has already mounted his horse, let me handle it for you. Go and call the驿长 (postmaster) and scold him, 'why didn't you provide for the chancellor and the judge?' and ask for a stick." The postmaster was terrified and knelt down. The chancellor said, "I wanted to beat you, but the大使 (ambassador) would beat the驿将 (postmaster) to death. It would only soil your reputation. If you are a prefect or county magistrate, then your life is not worth saving, but let's just let the postmaster go. He knelt down, sweating profusely, and ran away in a mess. Liu looked after him and said to the judge, "I've tripped you up." Everyone was surprised and praised his actions, which were all like this. The浮休子 (Floating Leisure Scholar) said, "There is no one better than Sima Hui and Liu Kuan."

Li Jing, the Duke of Ying, served as the Grand Administrator overseeing government affairs. One official who had failed an examination came to bid farewell to Li Jing. Li Jing said, "Come to the court tomorrow morning to see me." When the time came, the official arrived, and the Langzhong (a junior minister) was also present. The official bowed to Li Jing, who frowned and said, "You, who are long-lived, do not even know how to serve as a Shangshu or a Shilang. I, an old man, don’t even know how to tell time. I will be angry and beat you, and you are not even a human being. I will not respect your wife, and so he was let go." From then on, no officials dared to defy him, and if there was any delay or failure, the officials would collectively criticize him. Zhu Qianyi, the Langzhong of the Ministry of War, was unattractive, but his wife was beautiful. During the reign of Empress Wu, in the Xizhi Fang of Luoyang, there was a servant girl who was unkempt, with a hump and a large belly, and her ugliness was unmatched in the world. Yet Zhu Qianyi was greatly pleased with her and almost forgot sleep and food. Thus, he knew that the stories from the past about the love of the humped woman were not false. People in the world have such different tastes and preferences. I have heard that King Wen of Zhou loved a type of vegetable, the king of Chu loved a type of pickled vegetable, Qu Dao loved lotus root, Zeng Xi loved a type of fruit, and Liu Yong of the Song Dynasty loved dried scabs. According to historical records, Liu Yong went to see the governor of Wu Prefecture, Meng Rui, who had removed his shoes and stuck dried scabs to the ground. Liu Yong bent down and ate them. The Song Emperor Mingdi loved to eat candied cicadas and would eat several bowls at a time. Thus, we know that the talk about chasing foul smells by the sea and the story of the man who loved the ugly girl are not strange. It is simply a matter of personal preference.

During the reign of Emperor Taizong, a foreigner from the West presented a piece of music played on the pipa. The strings and plectrum of the pipa were thicker than usual. The emperor always did not want foreigners to surpass the Chinese in musical skill. Therefore, he set up a banquet with high-ranking officials and ordered a palace musician named Luo Heihei to listen to the piece through a curtain. Luo Heihei heard it once and memorized it perfectly. The emperor then told the foreigner, "This piece can be played by our palace musicians." He took a large pipa and ordered Luo Heihei to play it through the curtain. Luo Heihei played the piece without missing a single note. The foreigner was amazed and thought that the player was a palace girl, and he was astonished and left. Several dozen Western countries submitted after hearing this. Wang Yi, who never understood stringed instruments, suddenly fell asleep in the morning and woke up at night. He then requested to play the pipa and composed several pieces. One of them was called "Quail Chattering Snake," another was called "Tribal King Tune," and another was called "Hawthorn Garden." People in the Hawthorn Garden did not recognize it. Those who heard it all shed tears. His sister asked to learn it, and he taught her a few sounds. In a short while, she completely forgot and could not form a complete piece. During the Zhou Dynasty, there was a Brahmin monk named Huifan. He was cunning and deceitful, holding onto evil and creating witchcraft. He was indecisive like a mouse and used heretical ways to manipulate power. Empress Wu Zetian considered him a holy monk and gave him heavy rewards. Princess Taiping considered him a Buddhist king and treated him even better. He grew his influence and increased his fame and value. When Emperor Shang was in power, he often rode an official horse back and forth in the palace quarters. When Emperor Ruizong ascended the throne, he followed with servants in and out of the palace gates. Every time he entered, he was given silk, gauze, gold, and silver objects. He had a very high bearing and an arrogant spirit. The treasures of the imperial treasury were accumulated in the monk's household. He falsely claimed omens and recklessly predicted calamities and blessings. Emperor Xuanzong executed him, and the capital cheered.

The Taoist Shi Chongxuan, originally from Heine County in Huai Prefecture, was a cobbler before becoming a Taoist. He aligned himself with the "Great Peace" and became the head of the Taqing Temple, where he was elevated to the status of a revered teacher. He was frequently admitted to the imperial court to present requests and received generous rewards, with no object being denied him. He was granted the rank of Honglu卿, dressed in a purple robe and a silk sash, and held an elephant-shaped tablet and a fish-shaped emblem. Both officials and commoners showed him great respect, clearing the way for him wherever he went. Shenwu was executed, and the people of the capital celebrated. In southern China, there was a local custom where if someone fell ill, they would first sacrifice chickens, geese, and other animals to appease the illness. If this did not work, they would then sacrifice pigs and dogs. If still unsuccessful, they would offer the most prestigious sacrifice, the "Tai Lao" (a bull, sheep, and pig). If the sacrifices did not work, the person was considered beyond help and would be subjected to a ritualistic death by drumming and ringing of bells in the hall. After the funeral rites, the initial mourning process involved running and crying loudly. During the Jingyun era, there was a man named He Xuanjing with long hair who claimed to be a "Five Precepts Sage." He and about ten others set up a makeshift shelter in Lu Hun Mountain to deceive simple-minded people and their children. People spent all their wealth to follow these deceivers, believing that those who sincerely sought would achieve Buddhahood. He Xuanjing wore a golden robe and sat alone in a dark room, tricking the simple-minded into believing that he was emitting divine light. He arranged for fire to be lit at the edge of a cliff and had several people wear red and green silk cloths to appear as flying deities, blowing in the wind. He led the spectators to believe that they could fly and achieve enlightenment by wearing the "flying" clothes. On a fixed day, he arranged a ritual meal where a poisonous plant (莨菪子) was added to the food. Women with long hair had their hair cut and used to make "flying" clothes. People were pushed to the edge of the cliff and killed, with their belongings seized. When the officials came to investigate, they found hundreds of charred bodies in the ashes. He Xuanjing and the officials involved were punished with execution and demotion.

During the Jinglong period, a woman from Yingzhou presented herself to the court, her body adorned with images of pagodas, temples, and various Buddhas. The governor-general submitted her to the emperor, and she was granted the rank of Fifth Class Official. Her daughter was kept within the inner court, but after the death of the notorious eunuch Wei Zhongxian, her whereabouts became unknown. In the first year of Zhengsheng during the Zhou dynasty, a monk named Huaiyi built a Hall of Merit and Virtue, one thousand feet tall, to the north of the imperial palace. Within this hall, there was a colossal statue of the Buddha, nine hundred feet high, with a nose as large as a thousand-capacity ship, capable of accommodating dozens of people sitting side by side. The statue was made of lacquered wood. On the fifteenth day of the fifth month, a grand assembly known as the "Unobstructed Great Gathering" was held in front of the imperial palace. The ground was dug to a depth of five zhang, and chaotic defenses were used as palaces and towers, with bamboo bent to form the framework and stretched out to create the frame and cover. Large statues of the Buddha and bodhisattvas were also erected and brought up from the pit, falsely claimed to have emerged from the earth. Cow's blood was used to paint the head of a large statue, which was two hundred feet high, with the claim that it was made from the blood of the monk Huaiyi's knees. The crowd filled the city and overflowed the city walls, with men and women gathered like clouds, throwing money inside and trampling on each other, resulting in the deaths of many old and young people.

From the first to the sixteenth day, an image was set up for a vegetarian meal at the southern end of Tianjin Bridge, and during the second watch, a fire broke out in the Hall of Merit, spreading to the Bright Hall. The flames shot up into the sky, illuminating the city of Luoyang as brightly as daylight. The hall's construction was not yet halfway completed, yet it had already reached a height of over seventy feet. The fire then spread to the treasury of gold and silver, and molten iron flowed like liquid across the ground, covering more than a foot. Anyone who accidentally entered the area would be burned to a crisp. The hall was completely destroyed, with not a single piece of wood left. Measures were taken to prevent further damage until dawn, but a sudden, violent wind arose, tearing the bloodied image into hundreds of pieces. Fuhuizi said, "Emperor Wu of Liang offered himself to the Tongtai Temple, and officials emptied their treasuries to redeem him. That night, lightning struck, thunder roared, and heavy rain made it dark and stormy, destroying the temple's pagoda and Buddha hall in an instant. Such irrational events, how could they align with the Buddha's original intentions?" During the Jingyun period, in the western capital, it rained continuously for more than sixty days. A foreign monk named Bao Yan claimed to have a method to stop the rain. He set up an altar and chanted scriptures and incantations, and at that time, slaughtering animals was forbidden. Bao Yan used twenty sheep and two horses for sacrifice and prayer. After more than fifty days, the rain became even heavier, and he was executed and driven away. The rain finally stopped. During the Shengli period of the Zhou Dynasty, in the region of Hongzhou, a foreign monk named Chao left home to study the Dharma at White Deer Mountain. He claimed to have magical powers and said he was hundreds of years old. He claimed to have a recipe for an elixir of immortality, which cost an enormous amount. It took three years to complete, and he presented the elixir to the Sanyang Palace. Empress Wu Zetian took the elixir, believing it to be miraculous, and hoped to live as long as Peng Zu. She changed the era name to "Jiushi" (Long Vision) in the first year, allowed Chao to return to the mountains, and rewarded him generously. Three years after taking the elixir, Empress Wu Zetian died.

During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, she ordered a cat and a parrot to be fed together in the same container, and commanded the censor Peng Xianjue to supervise and display this to all officials and imperial examiners across the empire. Before the display was complete, the cat, feeling hungry, bit and killed the parrot, eating it. Empress Wu Zetian felt great shame, for "Wu" was her imperial surname, and this event was a sign of bad omen. At the time when Pei Yan was the Chief Councillor of State, Xu Jingye intended to rebel and instructed Luo Binwang to devise a plan to recruit Pei Yan to join the rebellion. Luo Binwang stood against the wall, deep in thought, and after a meal's time composed a rhyme: "One piece of fire, two pieces of fire, a red-clothed child sits on the throne." He taught Pei Yan's children and children in the capital city to recite the rhyme. Pei Yan then sought scholars to interpret the rhyme. He summoned Luo Binwang, offering him valuable gifts and silks, but Luo remained silent. Pei Yan also offered Luo music, beautiful women, and fine horses, but Luo still said nothing. They then looked at pictures of ancient loyal ministers and martyrs together. Seeing Sima Xuanwang (Sima Yi), Luo suddenly stood up and said, "This is a true hero!" He explained that throughout history, many ministers had changed the fate of the nation. Pei Yan was greatly pleased. Luo Binwang said, "But I do not know what the rhyme means." Pei Yan explained the rhyme's meaning to Luo Binwang, who then knelt and bowed, saying, "This is truly a real person!" Pei Yan and Xu Jingye then joined in the conspiracy. When the rebellion in Yangzhou began, Pei Yan acted as an insider, writing to Xu Jingye and others to join the plot, but only the word "qing" (青) and "e" (鵞) were mentioned. Someone reported this to the court, but no one could understand it. Empress Wu Zetian said, "The character 'qing' refers to the eleventh month, and 'e' refers to myself." She then executed Pei Yan, and Xu Jingye and others were soon defeated. The sister of Wu Zetian, married to Taihe, was known as "Qiyi." She believed in superstitions, using a leopard-head pillow to ward off evil, a Bai Ze pillow to remove spirits, and a bear pillow to ensure the birth of a male child. After Taihe's death, his son, the Prince of Guo, married her. When the Wu clan fell, the Prince of Guo cut off Qiyi's head and sent it to the court. Thus, it was known that the evil-warding pillows were ineffective.

During the Later Wei Dynasty, Gao Liu served as the governor of Chuzhou. He diverted the waters of the Hu River around the city, which broke open an ancient tomb. Inside, they found an inscription that read: "After my death, three hundred years later, a spring will flow from my back. Thanks to Gao Liu, I was relocated to a high plateau, and he built a coffin and prepared burial clothes for me." In the eastern capital, the Fengdu Market was located in the northeast of the Changshou Market. When the city walls were first constructed, they unearthed an ancient tomb. The tomb was buried in the ground without any bricks or tiles. The coffin and wooden structure were old and decayed, and upon touching them, they crumbled. The corpse was wearing a flat cap and a red robe, and another inscription was found, which said: "Divination methods are practiced in the court, and tortoise shell divination is near the market. In five hundred years, it will be seen here." At that time, those who understood verified that the tomb was buried in the second year of Huangchu of the Wei Dynasty. Taoist Master Kou Qian, who attained the Dao during the Later Wei Dynasty, often carved records on stone and hid them in Songshan Mountain. At the beginning of the Shangyuan era, people from Gaocounty in Luozhou were collecting herbs in the mountains and found one of these stones. They presented it to the county magistrate, Fan Wenyan, who then forwarded it to the prefecture. The prefecture reported it to Emperor Gaoxuan, who ordered it to be stored in the imperial treasury. The inscription on the stone contained many deep and mysterious meanings that could not be easily understood. It briefly said: "Wood son rules the world," "stop the sword dragon," "Li clan generation after generation unchanged," "central tripod reveals true form," and "foundation ten thousand years." "Wood son rules the world" refers to the Tang dynasty receiving the mandate. "Stop the sword dragon" refers to the empress dowager reigning, as "stop the sword" means "peace" and "dragon" symbolizes the empress dowager. "Li clan generation after generation unchanged" refers to Emperor Zhongzong reviving the world, as "Li" is the surname of the Tang imperial family, and "generation after generation unchanged" means the Li clan will remain unchanged. "Central tripod reveals true form" actually refers to Emperor Zhongzong's temple name, as "central tripod" symbolizes the emperor, and "true form" refers to his true identity. "Foundation ten thousand years" refers to Emperor Xuanzong's name, as "ten thousand years" symbolizes long-lasting. After Emperor Zhongzong ascended the throne, Fan Wenyan's son, Qinben, presented the original stone record to the emperor, who ordered it to be compiled into the national history.

In the east of Chenzhou, there are three mountains standing upright like tripod legs, each thousands of feet high. According to ancient legends, Kuafu raced against the sun and reached this place to cook rice; these three mountains are said to be the tripod legs that Kuafu used to support the cauldron. In the first year of the Baoli era (825 AD), in Qinggong Village, Ziyang County, Zizhou, there was a large stone as big as three rooms. It suddenly roared and leaped down the mountain, crossed a ravine, and then climbed back up a slope for about a hundred steps. When the stone was moving, farmers saw it and chased it with their hoes. The stone was two zhang (about 6.6 meters) high. The Zhaozhou Bridge is very well crafted, finely polished, and closely fitted, like it was cut out. From a distance, it looks like a rainbow drinking from a ravine. The railings on the bridge are all made of stone, and there are stone lions on the railings. In the Longshuo era (661-663 AD), a spy from Goguryeo stole two stone lions. Later, when they were replaced, no one could make them look the same. In the Dazhu year of Empress Wu Zetian's reign (695 AD), the Turkic leader Mochuo broke through Zhaozhou and tried to go south. When they reached the stone bridge, his horse knelt down and refused to move. A green dragon appeared on the bridge, flaring up in anger before disappearing. In the Yongchang year (689 AD), a white object flew several miles from the south-west slope of Fushui Inn in Taizhou, blocking the red water slope, where the mulberry fields and wheat ridges remained the same. Zou Camel, a poor man from Chang'an, used to sell steamed buns with a small cart. Every time he reached the corner of Shengye Lane, a hidden brick wall would overturn his cart, scattering dust and dirt on his buns. He was very distressed by this and decided to dig away the bricks with a hoe. After removing more than ten bricks, he found a large ceramic jar that could hold five dou (about 75 liters). Inside, he discovered several dou of gold, and he became very wealthy. His son, Zou Fang, became friends with Xiao Quan, and people would say that Xiao Quan was a prince's son and Zou Fang was a camel boy, implying that their friendship was based on wealth rather than virtue.

In the Tianxian era, a person from Luoyang was pulling a cow and running alongside it, with another person having unusually long hands who was patrolling the streets and begging. During the reign of Emperor Wen of Sui, a thousand-mile horse was presented by the Da Yuan kingdom. It dragged its feet on the ground and was called the Lion-colored Pony. The emperor placed it among other horses, but no one could control the wild and unruly horse. The emperor ordered all the horses to be driven together and asked his attendants, "Who can control this horse?" The general Pei Renji replied, "I can control it." He then raised his sleeves and moved forward, after walking more than ten steps, he jumped up and mounted the horse. One hand grabbed the ear and the other hand grabbed the eye, the horse trembled and dared not move. He then harnessed and rode it, leaving the western capital in the morning and arriving at the eastern capital in the evening. After the end of the Sui Dynasty, its whereabouts were unknown. Emperor Taizong of Tang issued an edict to search for it, and the Tangzhou governor Yuwen Shi found the horse, which was old and worn out, living with a noodle seller in Chao County. The horse had a burnt tail and a bald skin with holes inside. When he saw it, he wept. The emperor himself went out to Changle Slope, where the horse arrived at Xin Feng, facing west, it neighed and leaped. The emperor was very pleased, its teeth and mouth were even and healthy, it was fed with cinnabar, and it gave birth to five foals, all of which were thousand-mile horses. Later, its whereabouts were unknown. Zhang Na, the governor of Dezhou, had a white horse whose color was like silk. His father, Zhang Xiong, was the governor of Jingzhou and often rode this horse. After Zhang Xiong's death, his son Zhang Jingzhi became a Langzhong and later the governor of Shouzhou, also riding this horse. After Zhang Jingzhi's death, his brother Zhang Naji, who was a Shangshu and later the governor of Dezhou, took over. Zhang Naji then served as the Guozijiu and later the governor of Changzhou. The horse is still here today, having lived for over eighty years, extremely fat and strong, running quickly and its hooves do not scatter.

A man from Guangping named Song Cha married a woman from his own county, You Chang. His ancestors were of non-Han Chinese ethnicity, but they had lived in Han territory for three generations. Suddenly, he gave birth to a son with deep-set eyes and a high nose. He doubted that the child was legitimate and considered abandoning him. Shortly after, a red-grass horse gave birth to a white colt. Song Cha realized, "My great-grandfather had a 'Hun' appearance, and now this child resembles our ancestors." He decided to raise the child, hence the name "White Horse Saves the Hun Child." In the East Sea, there is a place called Snake Hill, which is dangerous and swampy, inhabited by many snakes. There are no people living there, and some of the snakes have human heads and snake bodies. In the southern regions, there is a snake known as the "Vindictive Snake." If someone provokes it, it will follow them for several miles and attack. If one kills a snake, a hundred snakes will gather. Carrying a centipede can protect against its attack. In Gu Zhu Mountain, there is a red stone cave where a green snake lives. The snake is about three feet long, similar in size to a small finger, and likes to rest at the tips of trees. When seen, it appears as if a belt is wrapped around branches and leaves. It is not venomous and flies away when it sees people. In the southern regions and the five streams of Qian, there are venomous snakes with black color and a reversed snout. They coil in the grass, and their teeth are curved backward. They can move rapidly towards people, and their bite is deadly. If they bite a person, the hand or foot will be amputated immediately. If not, the person will suffer severe swelling and decay. The mortality rate is extremely high, and they are known as venomous snakes. There is also a yellow-throated snake that likes to live on rooftops. It is non-venomous and harmless, but it primarily feeds on other venomous snakes. After eating, it drools and transforms into sand fleas, which can cause skin irritation. The symptoms of being bitten by this snake include a large "king" character on the forehead. This snake is considered the leader among snakes and often preys on venomous ones.

Planting millet and burning the horns of a goat and human hair would cause snakes to avoid the area. During the Sui Dynasty, in the county of Xuxi, the Shuti family had newly built a house and wanted to move it. Suddenly, an endless number of snakes flowed out of the room and covered the ground like a woven mat. At that time, a traveler claimed to understand the use of talismans. He took four peach branches, wrote a talisman on them, and nailed them around the four sides of the house. The snakes gradually retreated, and the talisman also moved with them. The snakes entered the center of the hall, where there was a hole as large as a basin's mouth. All the snakes entered the hole and disappeared. They then ordered a hundred buckets of boiling water to be poured over the area. After a night, they dug with a spade to a depth of one foot and found twenty million old coins. The coins were melted down and recast into new ones, leading to great wealth. The snakes were the spirits of ancient copper. In the fourth year of the Kaiyuan era, in June, in the mountains near Ma Ling in Chenzhou, there was a white snake about six or seven feet long and a black snake over ten feet long. After a short while, the two snakes fought, and the white snake swallowed the black snake. When it reached a thick part, both its mouths and throats split open, and blood poured out profusely. The black snake's head entered and bit the white snake's ribs, creating a hole. The black snake's head emerged two feet out, and soon both snakes died. After more than ten days, heavy rain caused a flood, and the rising water destroyed the homes of five hundred families and caused the loss of three hundred people. Left Censor Bi Gantai, from Renqiu in Yingzhou, had parents who were fifty years old and had prepared their own burial site. When his father was eighty-five, he built a coffin that was slightly larger, thinking the burial site was too small, and added twenty thousand bricks. When the coffin was opened for repairs, there were countless snakes inside. At that time, it was January, still cold, and the snakes were unable to move. They took the snakes and threw them into an empty well, but the snakes were still not all accommodated. The snakes were golden in color. Bi Gantai himself and his servants opened the coffin, and soon after, he fell ill and died. After a month, his parents also passed away. This was because the coffin was not properly closed.

In Dongguang County, Cangzhou, the Bao Guan Temple often saw gray hawks gathering on the high pavilion, and each time there were thousands of birds. In winter, the hawks would take one dove each night to warm their feet, releasing it at dawn without killing it. Other eagles and hawks dared not bully them. Emperor Taizong raised a white hawk, which he named General. When catching birds, he often drove them to the front of the hall and then killed them, hence the hall was named the Goose-Hunting Hall. He would always send the hawk to deliver messages from the capital to the Eastern Capital, and it would return with replies each day, several times a day. This was akin to the story of Lu Ji and his dog, Yellow Ear. During the first year of the Shangyuan era, in Huarong County, an elephant entered a village and lay in the courtyard, with its feet under a tree. The people drove it out, and the elephant, using its trunk to dig up soil, found dozens of ivory tusks and returned them. Counselor of the Ministry of Personnel Zheng Yin initially relied on Lai Junchen; when Lai was executed, he relied on Zhang Yizhi; when Zhang was killed, he relied on Empress Wei; later he attached himself to the Prince of Zhao, and was eventually executed. Grand Tutor Xue Ji, Prefect of Yongzhou Li Jin, Chancellor Cui Shi, Xiao Zhizhong, and Cen Xi, among others, all outwardly displayed loyalty but inwardly harbored flattery, bowing their shoulders and holding their breath to avoid trouble. They supported Princess Taiping and rose to high positions, believing they were secure. On July 3rd, they were destroyed, their families broken, and they were executed. How different is this from a phoenix perched on reeds, where a sudden strong wind causes the nest to fall and the eggs to break. Future scholars can reflect on this, can they not?

Sīnóng Sī, serving as the Minister of Agriculture, flattered Princess Anle, whose influence was as overwhelming as the mountains and seas, and whose breath could change the weather to frost and snow. A guest asked Zhang Wencheng, "What kind of person is Sīnóng Sī?" Zhang replied, "He is a rampant and cunning small man, dangerous in his actions and proud in his demeanor. He undermines influential families and avoids offending powerful families. He flatters those in power while being arrogant in dealing with others. He is fierce like a hungry tiger and greedy like a starving wolf, loving to devour people and eventually being devoured by others. For Princess Anle, he seized the farmlands and gardens of the common people to build the Ding Kun Pool, a man-made lake modeled after the imperial Kunming Pool, using a hundred million coins from the treasury. He lifted his purple robe and walked behind Princess Anle, pulling a golden ox-drawn carriage, all of which exemplify his servility. During the suppression of the rebels led by Wei, the emperor stood at the Chengtian Gate. Sīnóng Sī pretended to be happy, danced, and shouted "Long live the emperor!" The emperor ordered him to be executed, and swords fell chaotically, and he was executed along with his son. People cut off pieces of his flesh until only bones remained. During the reign of Empress Wu, Zhang Ji flattered Master Xue, holding a yellow silk canopy and following after him, lying on the ground to hold Master Xue's horse stirrups. The imperial censor Guo Ba once cleaned up feces for Lai Junchen, and Song Zhiwen held a chamber pot for Zhang Yizhi, both flattering and seeking favor, which is indeed a great evil in the teachings of morality. During the reign of Empress Wu, the scholar-official Jī Xù, whose father was the governor of Yizhou and was executed for corruption, met with the Chancellor Wei Wang Chengsi at the Tianjin Bridge, kneeling and claiming to be guilty of a capital offense. Chengsi asked him, "Do you have two sisters who can serve the great king?" Chengsi agreed and took Jī Xù into the carriage. For three days, Jī Xù did not speak. Chengsi found this strange and asked him, "My father committed a crime against the state, and I am worried and have no more hope." Since Jī Xù was fortunate to escape his father's extreme punishment, he was soon promoted to the position of horsekeeper and then to the position of Censor-in-Chief and Deputy Minister of Personnel, not based on talent but because the two sisters requested Chengsi.

During the reign of Empress Wu, the Naishi Zong Chuke was known for his sycophancy. At that time, Xue Shi, a eunuch, enjoyed the favor of Empress Wu, comparable to that of Lao Dou in the Han dynasty. Zong Chuke wrote a two-volume biography praising Xue Shi, claiming he was a divine being sent from heaven, unsure of his origin, likened to the reincarnation of the Buddha and Guanyin. Within a year, Xue Shi rose to the position of Naishi. Wu Sansi, a prince, wrote a biography of Zhang Yizhi, claiming he was the reincarnation of Prince Ziqing. A temple was built for Zhang Yizhi on Mount Gou, and poets and flatterers wrote poems to praise him. Cui Rong, a scholar-official, was the best among those who wrote about Zhang Yizhi. After a year, Zhang Yizhi's family and flatterers were exiled to the southern regions. Cui Shi, a scholar-official, flattered Zhang Yizhi and later allied with Empress Wei and her family, gaining favor similar to that of Feng Zidu and Dong Yan. His wife and two daughters entered the imperial harem, and he became a high-ranking official. Someone posted a sign criticizing Cui Shi for using his mediocre talent to gain favor and bringing his beautiful wife into the palace. Zhang Yue, a nobleman, was a flatterer who served Wang Maozhong, a high-ranking official, and received countless treasures. After Wang Maozhong went on a border inspection, Zhang Yue was promoted to a high position at a banquet. Zhang Yue thanked Wang Maozhong, kissed his boots, and danced, showing deep respect and flattery. Gao Lishi, a general, received special favor from Emperor Xuanzong. When he lost his mother, two officials, Cheng Bexian and Feng Shaozheng, showed excessive mourning, which was seen as ridiculous by the public.

A former censor named Wang Jingrong was from Pingye, Yizhou. He moved his father's coffin to Luozhou. During the excavation, they uncovered a dragon's lair as large as the opening of a jar. Wang Jingrong looked down and observed it, and a gas rose like smoke, damaging his eyes. He then became blind and died suddenly after ten days. In the fifth volume of "Chao Ye Qian Zai," under the "Qinding Siku Quanshu," in the sixth volume of "Chao Ye Qian Zai," written by Zhang Zuo of the Tang Dynasty: During the Tianbao era, a county clerk named Han Chaozong once summoned someone who came late. He punished the person with five strokes of the cane and handed them over to the county magistrate, who then punished them with ten strokes of the cane. The person died of an epidemic disease. Later, the person filed a complaint against Han Chaozong, who was then summoned. He entered through a large gate, which was marked by two poplar trees. At the entrance, there was a curtain hanging from a door or partition. Han Chaozong peeked and saw the deceased censor Hong Ziyu sitting there. Hong Ziyu asked, "Why have you come here, Han Da?" Han Chaozong replied, "I was summoned. I don't know why." Hong Ziyu told him to hurry and enter the inner courtyard, where he saw the former Minister of Justice Li Yi. Han Chaozong greeted him and asked, "Why did you punish someone for killing?" Han Chaozong explained that he did not kill anyone; the person died of an epidemic disease, and it was not his fault. Hong Ziyu asked why the county magistrate punished him and involved the main clerk. Han Chaozong had no involvement, but the deputy magistrates all saw and were punished with twenty strokes of the cane. Han Chaozong was released back to court and did not recover until late in the evening, with a bruised and painful back. After one month, he was able to resume his duties. Later, he inspected the streets and alleys and came to the southern part of the city wall of Chang'an. In one of the alleys, he saw a house with a southern-facing entrance, which he remembered as the place where he was summoned and punished. The house was empty, and people told him it was an inauspicious house where a princess died. He realized that such inauspicious houses are inhabited by spirits and believed it.

Shendǐ, the monk, refused to shave his head and eat soy sauce. Every time he went out to beg, he would accept whatever he received, whether it was coarse cloth or tattered clothing, or fine silk and brocade. One day, he asked Master Lìzhēn in front of his seat, "Are all things determined?" Master Lìzhēn replied, "Yes, they are determined." Shendǐ said, "If all things are determined, why do high places become valleys and deep places become hills? Why do the dead come back to life and the living die? All things are interconnected, and there is the cycle of rebirth in the six realms. How can they be determined?" Master Lìzhēn replied, "All things are not determined." Shendǐ said, "If all things are not determined, why not call heaven earth and earth heaven, the moon a star and the star the moon? How can they be undetermined?" Master Lìzhēn had no answer. At that time, Zhang Wencheng saw this and said to Shendǐ, "The Master is a Bodhisattva practitioner." Shendǐ said, "A Bodhisattva is not happy when he gains and not sad when he loses, does not get angry when he is hit, and does not get angry when he is scolded. This is what a Bodhisattva practitioner does." Shendǐ said, "I am happy when I beg and get something, and sad when I do not. I get angry when I am hit and angry when I am scolded. By this reasoning, I am far from being a Bodhisattva." As for Kōngrú, the monk, it is unknown where he came from. When he was young, he aspired to practice the Dharma. His parents forced him to marry, and he was cut with a knife to stop him. After reaching adulthood, he was conscripted for labor and injury, resulting in a disability. He entered Luhun Mountain and sat in a hermitage. The tiger did not act aggressively. One day, he saw a fight between a wild boar and a tiger in the mountains and intervened with a staff, saying, "Venerable, you do not need to fight." The two animals stopped fighting and dispersed. People all respected him and dared not criticize him.

The High Judge Chen Ximin was incompetent in his official duties, causing all affairs to stagnate. The officials of the High Court referred to him as a "high-ranking official who is actually incompetent," saying that he would spend half a day unable to make a decision, hence the term "high-ranking official who is actually incompetent." He was also called "following Confucius," meaning that he made too many deletions and revisions, thus the term "following Confucius." The magistrate of Longyou County, Li Ninding, was narrow-minded and petty. His nephew, who was seven years old, annoyed him, so he went to chase him but failed to catch him. He then lured him with food, bit him on the chest and back, causing him to bleed. His sister saved him. Later, while riding a donkey in the market, he encountered a horseman who poked his knee with the tip of his boot. Li became furious, scolded loudly, and was about to hit him, but the horse ran away, and he could not reach him. Unable to control his anger, he chewed on something by the roadside, causing it to bleed. During the reign of Emperor Taizong in the Zhenguan era, Yao Junqing, the county magistrate of Wuchang in Ji State, lost his horse.
